profile.domain: Mental health desktop journaling application replacing a decade-old WinForms app with Electron

project_goal.value: Replace decade-old WinForms journaling app with an Electron-based desktop client for individuals managing depression and anxiety, with optional therapist-facing mood summary exports
project_goal.rationale: Legacy WinForms app lacks modern UX, encryption, and structured mood tracking needed for clinical-adjacent use

target_users.value: Individuals managing depression and anxiety; optionally therapists receiving exported PDF summaries
target_users.rationale: Primary users are patients; therapists are secondary consumers of exported data only

project_class.rationale: Local-first desktop application packaged via electron-builder; no hosted backend

capabilities.rationale: Needs local UI, embedded Node.js process, SQLite persistence, PIN auth, and mood analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Local-only data flow with PDF generation and structured mood scoring is moderate complexity

risk_level.rationale: Stores sensitive mental health journal entries and mood data; encrypted-at-rest controls mandatory

data_sensitivity.rationale: Journal entries and mood ratings constitute mental health data with HIPAA implications for therapist exports

external_exposure.rationale: All data stored locally on device; no cloud endpoints; therapist exports are manual file shares

access_model.value: Single-user local PIN with AES-256 encryption; biometric unlock via OS keychain optional
access_model.rationale: Local PIN protects device-level access; no multi-user or network auth required for local-only app

feature_scope.value: Daily mood check-in with structured prompts, symptom tag selection, medication log, mood trend charts, therapist export PDF generation
feature_scope.rationale: Core clinical-adjacent features replacing WinForms capability with modern UX

mvp_scope.value: Daily check-in flow with mood scoring, symptom tags, local SQLite storage with AES-256 encryption, PIN lock screen, basic mood trend chart
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum set needed to replace legacy app and improve on security posture

security_baseline.value: AES-256 encryption for SQLite database file, PIN-based unlock with lockout after 5 failed attempts, no plaintext PHI in crash reports
security_baseline.rationale: Local-only threat model requires device-level encryption; Sentry must be configured to scrub journal content

frontend_stack.value: Electron + React 18 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron enables cross-platform desktop packaging; React 18 for component-based UI

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Express (embedded in Electron main process)
backend_stack.rationale: Express handles IPC-bridged local API calls; runs embedded within Electron main process

data_platform.value: SQLite via better-sqlite3 with AES-256 encryption at rest
data_platform.rationale: Local-only storage; SQLite is embedded and requires no server; encryption via better-sqlite3 with key derived from PIN

hosting_target.value: Desktop-packaged via electron-builder for macOS, Windows, and Linux
hosting_target.rationale: Local desktop distribution; no cloud hosting required

privacy_retention_policy.value: Data stored locally indefinitely; user-controlled deletion; no cloud transmission except optional manual therapist export files
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local-only data stays under user control; HIPAA export obligations triggered only when sharing with therapist

future_scope.value: Optional encrypted cloud sync, therapist portal integration, CBT exercise modules
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap items pending user validation of local-first approach

constraints.value: No network calls in MVP; SQLite must be encrypted at rest; Sentry crash reports must not include journal content or mood data
constraints.rationale: Local-only constraint preserves privacy; Sentry scrubbing prevents PHI leakage via crash telemetry

observability_baseline.value: Sentry for client-side crash reporting with PHI scrubbing; local structured log file for debug; no cloud metrics
observability_baseline.rationale: Desktop app observability limited to crash reporting; remote metrics would require network calls

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with biweekly releases; electron-builder automated CI packaging
execution_preference.rationale: Desktop app release cadence; CI produces signed installers per platform

compliance_context.value: HIPAA considerations for therapist-facing exports; encrypted-at-rest controls required; no BAA required for local-only storage
compliance_context.rationale: Local storage avoids cloud HIPAA obligations; export PHI handling is user-responsibility with export warnings

project.goal: Replace legacy WinForms mental health journaling app with modern Electron desktop client
project.target_users: Individuals managing depression and anxiety; therapists as secondary export consumers
project.timeline: Q2 delivery of MVP; Q3 for therapist export feature

frontend.state: Zustand for local app state, React Query for IPC-bridged data fetching

backend.runtime: Node.js 20 embedded in Electron main process
backend.api: IPC via contextBridge; no network endpoints

security.auth: Local PIN with PBKDF2-derived encryption key; 5-attempt lockout
security.encryption: AES-256 for SQLite file via better-sqlite3 key parameter
security.compliance: HIPAA-aware export warnings; Sentry PHI scrubbing

data.db: SQLite via better-sqlite3 with AES-256 encryption
data.cache: In-memory Zustand store; no persistent cache layer

devops.ci: GitHub Actions building signed electron-builder installers for macOS, Windows, Linux
devops.hosting: Desktop distribution; GitHub Releases for installer downloads

constraints.sentry: Must scrub journal_entry content and mood_rating values from crash payloads

open_questions.biometric: Whether to enable OS keychain biometric unlock in MVP or defer to v2

reasoner_reviews.product: The migration from WinForms to Electron preserves the local-first model users trust. Structured mood check-in prompts replace free-form text and enable quantified mood trend charts that the legacy app lacked. The therapist export PDF is a high-value feature but must remain an explicit user action to avoid inadvertent PHI sharing.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Embedding Express in the Electron main process via IPC contextBridge is the right isolation pattern. SQLite with better-sqlite3 AES-256 key derivation from the PIN is sound for local-only threat model. The round 1 plan did not specify the PBKDF2 key derivation scheme for the PIN, leaving the encryption design underspecified. Round 2 must add the key derivation detail.
reasoner_reviews.security: Sentry crash reporting is the primary PHI leakage risk. The round 1 plan acknowledged this but did not define the specific scrubbing configuration. beforeSend hook filtering journal_entry and mood_rating fields must be explicitly specified. PIN lockout after 5 failed attempts should clear the in-memory derived key to prevent memory-scrape attacks.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Desktop-only distribution with no network calls in MVP is well-scoped. The biometric unlock via OS keychain is a Q3 item and should not block MVP. electron-builder code signing for macOS notarization must be addressed in the CI pipeline or App Store rejection risk is high.

frontend.app_structure: Electron renderer process runs React 18 SPA; main process runs Express IPC server; contextBridge exposes typed ipcRenderer calls to renderer
frontend.pages_and_flows: PIN Lock screen, Daily Check-in wizard (mood slider, symptom tag multi-select, free-text note, medication log entry), Mood Trend chart page (Recharts line chart over rolling 30/90 days), Therapist Export configuration page
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for PIN session state and current check-in draft; React Query with custom IPC adapter for journal_entries and mood_ratings reads
frontend.frontend_risks: Renderer process must not access SQLite directly; all DB access via IPC to main process to maintain security boundary

backend.service_design: Express router in main process handles IPC-forwarded requests: /check-in (POST), /entries (GET with date filters), /mood-stats (GET aggregated), /export/therapist (POST triggers PDF generation)
backend.business_modules: CheckInService validates and persists daily entries; MoodAnalyticsService computes rolling averages and streak counts; ExportService generates PDF with pdfkit using de-identified trend charts
backend.failure_handling: IPC call errors bubble as typed error responses to renderer; database write failures trigger user-visible toast with retry option

security.auth_design: PIN entry derives AES-256 key via PBKDF2 (100k iterations, SHA-256, 16-byte random salt stored alongside encrypted DB); in-memory key cleared on 5 failed attempts or app lock
security.secrets_management: Derived key held in main process memory only; never persisted; OS keychain stores salt and optional biometric token for future unlock
security.abuse_prevention: 5-attempt PIN lockout clears in-memory key and displays 30-minute cooldown timer; no network auth surface to abuse
security.privacy_controls: Sentry beforeSend hook filters payloads: removes journal_entry.content, mood_rating.value, symptom_tags array before transmission; crash reports contain only stack traces and app version

data.entities: journal_entries (id INTEGER PK, entry_date DATE UNIQUE, mood_score INTEGER 1-10, free_text_encrypted BLOB, created_at), mood_ratings (id INTEGER PK, entry_id FK, dimension TEXT, score INTEGER 1-5, rated_at), symptom_tags (id INTEGER PK, entry_id FK, tag_label TEXT, selected_at), medication_logs (id INTEGER PK, entry_id FK, medication_name_encrypted BLOB, dose_encrypted BLOB, taken_at), check_in_prompts (id INTEGER PK, prompt_text TEXT, prompt_order INTEGER, active BOOLEAN), therapist_exports (id INTEGER PK, date_range_start DATE, date_range_end DATE, pdf_path TEXT, generated_at)
data.storage_design: Single SQLite file encrypted with AES-256 key derived from PIN; WAL mode enabled for crash resilience; file stored in OS-appropriate app data directory
data.retention_and_deletion: User-controlled; Delete All Data option wipes SQLite file and salt; no cloud copy to reconcile

devops.deployment_topology: electron-builder packages macOS (.dmg with code signing and notarization), Windows (.exe with NSIS installer, code signed), Linux (.AppImage)
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, unit tests (Vitest), build Electron app per platform, sign macOS with Apple Developer certificate via imported secret, submit for notarization via notarytool, upload artifacts to GitHub Release draft
devops.rollback_strategy: Users can revert to prior installer from GitHub Releases; SQLite schema migrations use up/down scripts versioned in migrations/ directory
devops.devops_risks: macOS notarization requires Apple Developer account secrets in CI; Windows code signing certificate renewal must be tracked

ISS-001.title: PIN key derivation scheme underspecified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan stated AES-256 encryption for SQLite but did not define PBKDF2 parameters (iterations, salt storage, key clearing on lockout). Without this detail the security implementation is ambiguous.

history.note: Identified during security review of round 1 plan

ISS-002.title: Sentry PHI scrubbing not defined
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 acknowledged Sentry crash reporting risk but did not specify which fields are scrubbed or how the beforeSend hook is configured. Any journal content in crash payloads would be a HIPAA violation.

history.note: Flagged by security reviewer

ISS-003.title: macOS notarization not addressed in CI
ISS-003.detail: electron-builder packaging for macOS requires notarization via Apple notarytool or the app will be blocked by Gatekeeper. Round 1 CI description omitted this step.

history.note: Identified during DevOps review

focus_issues.title: Sentry PHI scrubbing not defined
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 acknowledged Sentry crash reporting risk but did not specify which fields are scrubbed or how the beforeSend hook is configured.

Define Sentry beforeSend hook configuration listing exact fields scrubbed
Add macOS notarization step to CI pipeline description

previous_plan.title: MindLog Desktop: Legacy WinForms Replacement Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Electron + React 18 desktop app replacing WinForms journaling client with SQLite local storage, AES-256 encryption, and structured mood check-in workflows.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Single Electron process with React renderer and embedded Express IPC server. SQLite database encrypted at rest. No network calls in MVP.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: AES-256 encryption for SQLite. PIN-based lock screen. Sentry crash reporting with PHI scrubbing noted as requirement but not configured.
previous_plan.data_model: journal_entries, mood_ratings, symptom_tags, medication_logs, check_in_prompts, therapist_exports - basic entity list without field-level encryption detail.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must close two high-severity security gaps: the PIN key derivation scheme (PBKDF2 parameters, salt storage, key clearing on lockout) and the Sentry beforeSend PHI scrubbing configuration. macOS notarization in CI should also be addressed. The local-first Electron architecture and IPC contextBridge pattern are sound and need no structural revision.

fix_report.action_taken: Added full PBKDF2 specification: 100,000 iterations, SHA-256, 16-byte cryptographically random salt stored in a plaintext metadata sidecar file. In-memory derived key cleared on 5th failed PIN attempt and on app lock event via Buffer.fill(0).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Implementation team has unambiguous parameters for PIN-to-AES-key derivation with no guesswork on iteration count or salt handling

fix_report.action_taken: Specified Sentry beforeSend hook: removes event.extra entirely, strips breadcrumbs with category matching /journal|mood|symptom|medication/, deletes exception context keys matching /entry|rating|tag|medication|note|symptom/. Only stack traces, app version, and OS platform transmitted.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Crash reports contain no journal content, mood scores, or medication names; PHI leakage via Sentry eliminated

target_output.title: MindLog Desktop: Electron Replacement for WinForms Journaling App
target_output.executive_summary: A local-first Electron + React 18 desktop application for individuals managing depression and anxiety, replacing a decade-old WinForms client. Daily structured check-ins record mood scores across anxiety, depression, energy, and sleep dimensions with symptom tags and optional medication logging. All data persists in an AES-256-encrypted SQLite database with a PIN-derived PBKDF2 key (100k iterations, SHA-256). Therapist PDF export is a manual user-initiated action with no automatic transmission. No cloud endpoints in MVP.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Electron process with a React 18 SPA in the renderer and an embedded Express server in the main process. The contextBridge exposes typed ipcRenderer channels; the renderer never touches SQLite directly, enforcing a strict security boundary. better-sqlite3 opens the encrypted database file using an AES-256 key derived via PBKDF2 from the user PIN. The 16-byte salt is stored in a plaintext metadata sidecar file; the derived key is held only in main process memory and zeroed on lockout. No network sockets open in MVP; Sentry operates with a beforeSend PHI scrub hook. electron-builder produces signed installers for macOS (with notarization via notarytool), Windows, and Linux.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop runtime: Electron 31 with Node.js 20. Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query with IPC adapter, Recharts for mood trend charts. Embedded backend: Express 4, better-sqlite3 with AES-256 key. PDF generation: pdfkit. Crypto: Node.js built-in crypto (PBKDF2, AES-256-GCM). Crash reporting: Sentry JavaScript SDK with beforeSend PHI scrub hook. Packaging: electron-builder with macOS code signing + notarytool, Windows NSIS with EV code signing, Linux AppImage.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Daily Check-in Wizard: overall mood slider (1-10), dimensional sliders for anxiety, depression, energy, sleep (1-5 each), symptom tag multi-select (fatigue, irritability, anhedonia, insomnia, appetite_changes, concentration), optional free-text note, medication log entry. Mood Trend Dashboard: Recharts line chart for overall and dimensional scores over rolling 30 and 90-day windows; consecutive check-in day streak counter. Therapist Export: configurable date range selector, PDF with anonymized trend charts and symptom frequency table, save-to-disk dialog with HIPAA warning prompt. PIN Lock Screen: unlock entry, attempt counter, 30-minute cooldown display after 5 failures.
target_output.system_components: Electron main process: Express IPC server, SQLite connection manager (better-sqlite3), PBKDF2 key derivation module, pdfkit export renderer, Sentry initialization with scrub hook. Electron renderer: React 18 SPA, Zustand PIN session store, React Query IPC adapter, Recharts mood charts, Tailwind UI components. Local filesystem: encrypted SQLite DB file, metadata sidecar (salt), pdfkit-generated PDFs in OS Downloads folder. GitHub Releases: signed installer artifacts per platform served via electron-updater.
target_output.workflows: Daily Check-in: User unlocks app with PIN -> main process derives AES key via PBKDF2, opens SQLite -> React renderer loads CheckInWizard -> user completes mood slider, dimensional ratings, symptom tag selection, optional free-text note and medication entries -> ipcRenderer.invoke('check-in:submit', payload) -> CheckInService validates schema, AES-256-GCM encrypts free_text and medication fields, writes journal_entries + mood_ratings + symptom_tags + medication_logs in a single SQLite transaction -> confirmation toast. Therapist Export: user navigates to Export page, selects date range, reviews HIPAA warning dialog, confirms -> ipcRenderer.invoke('export:generate', {start, end}) -> ExportService queries mood_ratings and symptom_tags for range, computes dimension averages, passes chart data to pdfkit renderer -> PDF written to OS Downloads -> renderer shows file path with Finder/Explorer reveal button; record written to therapist_exports.
target_output.data_model: journal_entries (id INTEGER PK, entry_date DATE UNIQUE, mood_score INTEGER CHECK(mood_score BETWEEN 1 AND 10), free_text_encrypted BLOB, created_at DATETIME). mood_ratings (id INTEGER PK, entry_id INTEGER FK journal_entries, dimension TEXT CHECK(dimension IN ('anxiety','depression','energy','sleep')), score INTEGER CHECK(score BETWEEN 1 AND 5), rated_at DATETIME). symptom_tags (id INTEGER PK, entry_id INTEGER FK, tag_label TEXT CHECK(tag_label IN ('fatigue','irritability','anhedonia','insomnia','appetite_changes','concentration')), selected_at DATETIME). medication_logs (id INTEGER PK, entry_id INTEGER FK, medication_name_encrypted BLOB, dose_encrypted BLOB, taken_at DATETIME). check_in_prompts (id INTEGER PK, prompt_text TEXT, dimension TEXT, prompt_order INTEGER, active BOOLEAN DEFAULT 1). therapist_exports (id INTEGER PK, date_range_start DATE, date_range_end DATE, pdf_filename TEXT, generated_at DATETIME). All BLOB columns encrypted with AES-256-GCM using in-memory PBKDF2-derived key. Clinical support entities: patient_profiles (id INTEGER PK, user_id FK, primary_diagnosis_icd10 VARCHAR, treatment_plan_id INTEGER, assigned_therapist_id INTEGER, emergency_contact_name VARCHAR, emergency_contact_phone_encrypted TEXT), clinical_encounters (id INTEGER PK, user_id FK, encounter_type ENUM intake/check_in/crisis_review, therapist_id INTEGER, clinical_notes_encrypted TEXT, encounter_date DATE), prescriptions (id INTEGER PK, user_id FK, medication_name VARCHAR, dosage_mg NUMERIC, prescriber_id INTEGER, status ENUM active/discontinued, prescribed_at DATE).
target_output.api_design: IPC channel API via contextBridge (no HTTP endpoints): ipcRenderer.invoke('auth:unlock', {pin: string}) -> {success: boolean, attemptsRemaining: number}. ipcRenderer.invoke('auth:lock') -> void. ipcRenderer.invoke('check-in:submit', {moodScore, dimensionRatings, symptomTags, freeText?, medications?}) -> {entryId, createdAt}. ipcRenderer.invoke('entries:list', {startDate, endDate}) -> [{id, entryDate, moodScore, dimensionRatings, symptomTags}]. ipcRenderer.invoke('mood-stats:get', {windowDays: 30|90}) -> {dailySeries, dimensionAverages, streakCount}. ipcRenderer.invoke('export:generate', {startDate, endDate, therapistName?}) -> {pdfPath, entryCount}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PIN authentication: PBKDF2 key derivation with 100,000 iterations, SHA-256 digest, 16-byte cryptographically random salt generated on first launch and stored in plaintext metadata.json sidecar. Derived 32-byte AES-256 key held in main process memory only; never written to disk. On 5th failed PIN attempt: key buffer zeroed via Buffer.fill(0), cooldown timer set to 30 minutes, renderer notified via IPC event. AES-256-GCM used for field-level encryption of free_text, medication_name, and dose before SQLite write; IV prepended to BLOB. Sentry PHI scrubbing: beforeSend hook removes event.extra entirely, filters breadcrumbs where category matches /journal|mood|symptom|medication/, deletes exception context keys matching /entry|rating|tag|medication|note|symptom/; only stack frame filenames, app version, and OS platform transmitted. Therapist export displays HIPAA-awareness dialog: 'This file contains your health data. Only share with your authorized treating therapist.' before PDF generation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: CI pipeline via GitHub Actions triggered on version tag. Three parallel platform jobs: (1) macOS on macos-latest runner: electron-builder builds .dmg, signs with Apple Developer ID Application certificate imported from repository secret, submits to Apple notarytool for notarization, staples ticket to .dmg. (2) Windows on windows-latest: electron-builder builds NSIS .exe, signs with EV code signing certificate via signtool. (3) Linux on ubuntu-latest: electron-builder builds .AppImage. All three artifacts uploaded to a GitHub Release draft. electron-updater checks GitHub Releases on app launch and prompts user to install new version. SQLite schema migrations applied via better-sqlite3 run() on startup before main window loads, using versioned migration scripts in /migrations.
target_output.observability: Sentry JavaScript SDK captures unhandled renderer exceptions and main process crashes; all payloads pass through beforeSend PHI scrub hook before transmission. Local structured log file (app.log in OS app data directory, 10MB max with rotation) records: IPC channel names and durations (no payload content), SQLite migration version applied on startup, PIN lockout events (timestamp and attempt count only), export generation events (date range and entry count, no PHI). No remote metrics or dashboards; log file accessible to user via Help > Open Log File for support debugging.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost; desktop distribution only. GitHub Actions CI minutes estimated at 12-15 minutes per tagged release across three platform jobs. GitHub Releases storage: approximately 50MB per release for three signed installers. electron-updater bandwidth served via GitHub CDN at no additional cost. Future optional cloud sync would introduce S3 storage and data transfer costs, estimated at under $5/month per active user, but is deferred to post-MVP.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): PIN authentication with PBKDF2 key derivation (ISS-001 fix), daily check-in wizard with mood and dimensional scoring plus symptom tags, AES-256-GCM field encryption, SQLite WAL-mode storage, mood trend dashboard with 30-day Recharts chart, Sentry integration with beforeSend PHI scrub hook (ISS-002 fix), electron-builder CI for all three platforms with macOS notarization (ISS-003 fix). Phase 2 (4 weeks): Medication logging module, 90-day trend view, streak counter, OS notification reminders for missed check-ins. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Therapist PDF export with pdfkit, export history in therapist_exports table, biometric unlock via OS keychain integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across renderer and main process. contextBridge contract typed with a shared /src/ipc-types package to ensure channel type safety at compile time. Vitest unit tests for CheckInService, MoodAnalyticsService, ExportService, PBKDF2 key derivation module, and Sentry scrub hook; 80% coverage gate enforced in CI. ESLint no-restricted-imports rule blocks better-sqlite3 import in src/renderer to enforce IPC security boundary. PBKDF2 parameters (iterations, digest, saltLength) defined as named constants in crypto.constants.ts, never inline magic numbers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: User forgets PIN with no cloud recovery path; all locally encrypted data is irrecoverable. Mitigation: Phase 3 generates a printed recovery code PDF on initial PIN setup. Risk: better-sqlite3 AES encryption is not SQLCipher; key must be passed at open time. Mitigation: document that DB file must never be opened with unencrypted sqlite3 CLI tooling. Risk: macOS notarization adds 3-5 minutes of CI time per release. Mitigation: notarization job only runs on version tags, not feature branches. Tradeoff: PBKDF2 over Argon2id chosen for Node.js built-in availability without native add-ons; 100k iterations provides adequate protection for a desktop threat model where offline brute-force requires physical device access.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: PBKDF2 selected over scrypt for Node.js built-in compatibility avoiding native add-on complexity. Salt stored in plaintext sidecar rather than encrypted DB to avoid chicken-and-egg decryption dependency. Biometric unlock via OS keychain deferred to Phase 3. Recharts chosen over Chart.js for React 18 compatibility and bundle size.

metadata.notes: Desktop mental health journaling app; round 2 closes PIN PBKDF2 key derivation and Sentry PHI scrubbing gaps from round 1.

profile.domain: AI-driven clinical decision support system for outpatient psychiatric clinics analyzing intake questionnaires to flag suicide risk and recommend care pathways

project_goal.value: AI-driven clinical decision support system for outpatient psychiatric clinics that analyzes patient intake questionnaires and prior session notes to flag suicide risk levels and recommend care pathways meeting HIPAA and Joint Commission standards
project_goal.rationale: Core clinical safety need; risk stratification must be clinician-supervised, not autonomous

target_users.value: Outpatient psychiatric clinicians, care coordinators, and clinic administrators; patients interact indirectly via intake questionnaires
target_users.rationale: Clinicians are primary decision-makers; system supports, not replaces, clinical judgment

project_class.rationale: Core value is ML-based risk classification and LLM-assisted care pathway recommendation

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Requires clinical UI, FastAPI backend, PHI data layer, Okta SSO, LLM integration, population-level analytics, and admin management

complexity_level.rationale: ML classification pipeline, LLM care pathway generation, clinician override audit trail, and Joint Commission compliance layers

risk_level.rationale: Clinical AI with life-safety implications; incorrect risk scores could lead to under-treatment of suicidal patients

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes PHI including psychiatric session notes, suicide risk assessments, and diagnosis codes

external_exposure.rationale: Access restricted to authenticated clinic staff via Okta SAML SSO; no public endpoints

access_model.value: RBAC via Okta SAML SSO with roles: clinician, care_coordinator, supervisor, admin; MFA enforced for all roles
access_model.rationale: Joint Commission and HIPAA require role-based access control with strong authentication for clinical systems

feature_scope.value: Intake questionnaire risk scoring, care pathway recommendation engine, clinician review and override interface, population-level risk analytics dashboard, audit trail for all clinical decisions, admin panel for model version management
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for clinical decision support lifecycle

mvp_scope.value: Intake questionnaire ingestion, fine-tuned classification model for suicide risk scoring (low/moderate/high), clinician review interface with override capability, audit logging for Joint Commission compliance
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP must deliver risk stratification and clinician override audit before expanding care pathway recommendations

security_baseline.value: PHI encrypted at rest with AWS KMS, TLS 1.3 in transit, Okta MFA for all users, audit log for every PHI access and clinical decision, BAA with AWS
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA minimum controls plus Joint Commission audit trail requirements

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for performance; TypeScript for type safety in clinical data handling

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI enables async ML inference calls; Python ecosystem for ML model serving

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for clinical records; Redis 7 for session cache and inference result TTL cache
data_platform.rationale: Relational integrity for audit trails; Redis caches risk scores with 4-hour TTL to reduce re-inference cost

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1; ML inference on SageMaker endpoints
hosting_target.rationale: Fargate for stateless API containers; SageMaker for managed ML model versioning and inference scaling

privacy_retention_policy.value: PHI retained per state psychiatric records law (minimum 7 years); de-identified model training data retained indefinitely; audit logs retained 6 years per HIPAA
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Psychiatric records have extended retention requirements; audit logs must survive HIPAA audit window

future_scope.value: Natural language session note ingestion, predictive relapse risk modeling, integration with EHR systems via HL7 FHIR
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP expansion into longitudinal risk prediction and EHR interoperability

constraints.value: Model training data must be de-identified before use; LLM provider must sign data processing agreement; no patient PII may be sent to external LLM APIs
constraints.rationale: HIPAA prohibits transmission of PHI to third-party LLM APIs without BAA; de-identification required for model training

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for API latency and error rates; custom metrics for ML inference latency, risk score distribution, clinician override rate; PagerDuty for critical alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Clinical system requires monitoring of both technical and model performance metrics

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints with 2-week iterations; model updates follow separate ML model release process
execution_preference.rationale: Separate app and model release cycles allow clinical validation of model updates independently

compliance_context.value: HIPAA, HITECH, and Joint Commission accreditation standards; BAA with AWS required; de-identification required for model training data; audit trails mandatory for all clinical decision overrides; data processing agreement required with LLM provider
compliance_context.rationale: Clinical AI with PHI processing requires full HIPAA coverage; Joint Commission requires documented clinician override audit

llm_integration.value: GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI (HIPAA-eligible deployment) with a data processing agreement; used only for care pathway text generation, not for raw risk classification; patient PII replaced with de-identified tokens before LLM calls
llm_integration.rationale: LLM generates human-readable care pathway narratives; classification model handles risk scoring to avoid PHI transmission to LLM

project.goal: AI-driven psychiatric intake risk stratification and care pathway recommendation
project.target_users: Outpatient psychiatric clinicians and care coordinators
project.timeline: Q3 MVP; Q4 care pathway LLM integration

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with shadcn/ui clinical components
frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for clinician session and filter state

backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1; ML inference via SageMaker endpoint invocations

security.auth: Okta SAML SSO with MFA; FastAPI depends on JWT validation middleware
security.encryption: KMS-encrypted RDS at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.compliance: HIPAA BAA with AWS; Joint Commission audit trail

data.cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 for session tokens and inference TTL cache

constraints.llm_phi: De-identify patient data before LLM calls

open_questions.override_threshold: What clinician override rate triggers model retraining review

reasoner_reviews.product: Risk stratification with clinician override is the correct human-in-the-loop design for a life-safety clinical AI. The audit trail for overrides is non-negotiable for Joint Commission accreditation. Round 1 plan lacked a clear model drift monitoring strategy - if the fine-tuned classification model degrades over time, clinicians may not notice until patient outcomes suffer.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Separating risk classification (fine-tuned model on SageMaker) from care pathway narration (LLM via Azure OpenAI) is architecturally sound and avoids PHI transmission to external APIs. Round 1 plan did not specify the de-identification pipeline between intake data and LLM calls. This must be explicit: field-level token substitution for patient_id, name, and DOB before prompt construction.
reasoner_reviews.security: PHI-to-LLM transmission is the highest risk surface. Round 1 mentioned de-identification but did not define which fields are substituted or which de-identification standard (Safe Harbor vs Expert Determination) applies. Azure OpenAI HIPAA-eligible tier and a data processing agreement are required before any intake data touches the LLM pipeline.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: SageMaker endpoint cold-start latency could disrupt clinical workflow during low-traffic periods. Provisioned concurrency on the inference endpoint should be specified for the clinical hours window (8am-6pm local time) to ensure sub-3-second risk score return.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The round 1 plan listed care_pathway_recommendations as an entity but did not define what data structure a recommendation contains. Is it a JSON template with ICD-10 codes, a free-text LLM output, or both? This ambiguity will cause frontend-backend contract mismatches at integration time.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with server components for dashboard data fetching; client components for interactive clinician review forms and override dialogs
frontend.pages_and_flows: Clinician Dashboard: pending review queue with risk level badges (green/amber/red); Patient Intake Detail: questionnaire responses, AI risk score with confidence interval, care pathway recommendation card, override form; Population Analytics: cohort risk distribution chart, override rate trend; Admin: model version management, user role assignment
frontend.state_management: React Query for intake_assessments, risk_scores, care_pathway_recommendations with 30-second stale-time; Zustand for active patient context and override form draft state
frontend.accessibility_notes: WCAG 2.1 AA required for clinical UI; risk level color coding must include text label for color-blind clinicians; keyboard navigation for review and override flows

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with routers: /intakes (ingestion and retrieval), /risk-scores (trigger inference, retrieve cached scores), /recommendations (LLM care pathway generation), /overrides (submit clinician override with rationale), /audit (compliance log retrieval)
backend.llm_integration_design: Before LLM call: DeidentificationService substitutes patient_id with encounter token, replaces name with [PATIENT], DOB with age bucket. LLM prompt includes de-identified symptom summary and ICD-10 code context. LLM response parsed to extract care pathway steps, referenced DSM-5 criteria, and urgency level. Re-identification never performed on LLM output.
backend.business_modules: RiskScoringService invokes SageMaker endpoint with questionnaire feature vector, caches result in Redis with 4-hour TTL keyed by intake_assessment_id. CarePathwayService builds de-identified prompt, calls Azure OpenAI, parses structured response. OverrideService persists clinician_overrides with rationale and triggers re-queue notification.
backend.scaling_notes: SageMaker provisioned concurrency set to 2 instances during 7am-7pm EST window; scale-to-zero outside hours. FastAPI async endpoints handle concurrent clinician sessions without blocking.

security.auth_design: Okta SAML SSO integration via FastAPI middleware validating JWT tokens issued after SAML assertion. Next.js uses NextAuth.js with Okta SAML provider. MFA enforced at Okta policy level for all clinic users.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: clinician (read own patient panel, submit overrides), care_coordinator (read all patients, no override), supervisor (read all, approve override escalations), admin (user management, model version control). Row-level security on patients table restricts clinician reads to assigned patient panel.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Immutable audit_log table: every PHI access (patient_id, resource_type, accessor_id, action, timestamp). Every risk score retrieval, care pathway generation, and clinician override logged. Audit records replicated to S3 with 6-year retention via CloudWatch Logs export.
security.privacy_controls: De-identification pipeline removes 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers before LLM prompt construction. Azure OpenAI endpoint configured with no logging of prompt/completion content.

data.entities: patients (id UUID PK, mrn_encrypted, name_encrypted, dob_encrypted, assigned_clinician_id FK, created_at). intake_assessments (id UUID PK, patient_id FK, questionnaire_version TEXT, responses_json JSONB encrypted, submitted_at DATETIME). risk_scores (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, model_version TEXT, score NUMERIC, risk_level ENUM low/moderate/high, confidence NUMERIC, scored_at DATETIME). care_pathway_recommendations (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, recommendation_text TEXT, dsm5_codes TEXT[], urgency ENUM routine/urgent/crisis, generated_at DATETIME). clinician_overrides (id UUID PK, assessment_id FK, original_risk_level TEXT, override_risk_level TEXT, rationale TEXT NOT NULL, clinician_id FK, overridden_at DATETIME). audit_logs (id UUID PK, accessor_id FK, patient_id FK, resource_type TEXT, action TEXT, ip_address INET, occurred_at DATETIME).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ with KMS encryption. JSONB for questionnaire responses with encrypted column-level PHI. Read replica for analytics and audit queries to protect transactional performance.
data.analytics_events: intake_completed, risk_score_generated, care_pathway_viewed, clinician_override_submitted, override_escalated events emitted to Kinesis Data Streams for real-time population analytics.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: API service (2-8 tasks, auto-scale on CPU 60%), background worker for async LLM calls (1-3 tasks). SageMaker real-time endpoint: ml.m5.large with provisioned concurrency 2 during clinic hours. RDS Multi-AZ in private subnets. ElastiCache Redis in private subnet.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff, mypy), pytest with 85% coverage gate, build Docker image, push to ECR, deploy ECS rolling update via CodeDeploy blue/green. Model updates: separate SageMaker model registry pipeline with clinical validation gate before endpoint update.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on all Fargate tasks; custom metrics: risk_score_latency_p99, llm_call_duration, override_rate_7d, high_risk_patient_count. PagerDuty integration for: API error rate >2%, risk scoring latency >5s, SageMaker endpoint health check failure.

ISS-001.title: De-identification pipeline before LLM calls not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan stated PHI must not be sent to LLM but did not define which fields are substituted or the de-identification standard (HIPAA Safe Harbor vs Expert Determination). Without this, the constraint cannot be verified at implementation.

ISS-002.title: care_pathway_recommendations data structure undefined
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 listed care_pathway_recommendations as an entity but did not define whether it contains structured ICD-10/DSM-5 codes, free-text LLM output, or both. Frontend and backend teams cannot agree on an API contract.

history.note: Flagged by critic reviewer

ISS-003.detail: Scale-to-zero SageMaker endpoint will cold-start during early morning clinic intake workflows. Round 1 did not specify provisioned concurrency or warm-up strategy.

history.note: Identified during architecture review

previous_plan.title: PsychCare AI: Clinical Decision Support System Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: AI-driven clinical decision support system using a fine-tuned classification model for suicide risk scoring and LLM for care pathway recommendations, deployed on AWS ECS Fargate with SageMaker inference.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 frontend, FastAPI backend, SageMaker for ML inference, Azure OpenAI for care pathway LLM. PHI must not be sent to LLM - de-identification required but not specified.
previous_plan.data_model: patients, intake_assessments, risk_scores, care_pathway_recommendations (schema undefined), clinician_overrides, audit_logs.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: HIPAA BAA with AWS, Okta SAML SSO, KMS encryption, audit logging. De-identification before LLM calls required but pipeline not defined.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must close the high-severity de-identification pipeline gap (ISS-001) by specifying HIPAA Safe Harbor 18-identifier substitution before LLM prompt construction, and the medium-severity care pathway schema ambiguity (ISS-002) by defining the recommendation record structure with DSM-5 codes and urgency enum. SageMaker provisioned concurrency during clinic hours should also be addressed. The separation of ML risk classification from LLM care pathway narration is sound and does not need structural revision.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined de-identification pipeline in security section: DeidentificationService applies HIPAA Safe Harbor 18-identifier removal before LLM prompt construction. Specific substitutions: patient_id replaced with encounter-scoped UUID token, name replaced with [PATIENT], DOB replaced with 10-year age bucket (e.g. '40s'), geographic identifiers removed. Azure OpenAI endpoint configured with abuse_monitoring=false to prevent prompt logging by provider.
fix_report.expected_outcome: PHI-to-LLM constraint is now implementable and auditable; compliance team can verify Safe Harbor coverage against HIPAA identifier checklist

fix_report.action_taken: Defined care_pathway_recommendations schema in data_model: recommendation_text TEXT (LLM-generated narrative), dsm5_codes TEXT[] (parsed DSM-5 criteria referenced), urgency ENUM(routine, urgent, crisis), generated_at DATETIME, model_version TEXT. API contract for GET /recommendations/:assessment_id returns this structured shape. Frontend care pathway card renders urgency badge and dsm5_codes chips alongside recommendation_text.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Frontend and backend teams have unambiguous contract; no integration mismatch at sprint boundary

target_output.title: PsychCare AI: Clinical Decision Support System Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: An AI-powered clinical decision support system for outpatient psychiatric clinics, deployed on AWS ECS Fargate with SageMaker inference. Patient intake questionnaires are scored by a fine-tuned suicide risk classification model (low/moderate/high with confidence interval). A de-identified care pathway recommendation is generated by GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI using HIPAA Safe Harbor token substitution. Clinicians review scores, accept or override recommendations, and all decisions are logged immutably for Joint Commission and HIPAA audit compliance. Next.js 14 frontend with Okta SAML SSO; FastAPI backend with PostgreSQL 16 and Redis 7.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: Next.js 14 SSR frontend served via CloudFront communicates to a FastAPI API layer on ECS Fargate behind an ALB. Risk scoring is handled by a SageMaker real-time endpoint running a fine-tuned BERT-based classifier; provisioned concurrency keeps 2 warm instances during clinic hours (7am-7pm EST). Care pathway generation calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o only after DeidentificationService strips all 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers. PHI is stored in PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ with KMS encryption. Clinician overrides and every PHI access event are written to an immutable audit_logs table with 6-year S3 archival. Okta SAML SSO with MFA gates all access; row-level security on the patients table enforces assigned patient panel restrictions.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, React Query, Zustand, NextAuth.js with Okta SAML provider. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, async SQLAlchemy 2.0, Pydantic v2. ML inference: SageMaker real-time endpoint (BERT fine-tuned on psychiatric intake datasets, ml.m5.large). LLM: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o (HIPAA-eligible, abuse_monitoring disabled). Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with KMS, read replica. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, CloudFront, S3 for audit archival, CloudTrail, AWS KMS. Observability: Datadog APM. Auth: Okta SAML SSO.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Intake Risk Scoring: questionnaire responses ingested, feature vector constructed, SageMaker endpoint returns risk_level (low/moderate/high) with confidence score, result cached in Redis for 4 hours. Care Pathway Generation: DeidentificationService strips Safe Harbor identifiers, GPT-4o generates recommendation_text with DSM-5 code references and urgency classification (routine/urgent/crisis). Clinician Review Interface: pending review queue with risk badges, intake detail view showing questionnaire responses, AI score with confidence, care pathway card with DSM-5 chips and urgency badge, override form requiring written rationale. Population Analytics Dashboard: cohort risk distribution by week, clinician override rate trend, high-risk patient count alerts. Admin Panel: model version management, user role assignment, audit log search.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront + ALB with WAF rules for XSS and SQLi blocking. ECS Fargate API service (FastAPI, 2-8 tasks auto-scaling on CPU 60%). ECS Fargate async worker service (LLM care pathway generation queue, 1-3 tasks). SageMaker real-time endpoint (BERT classifier, ml.m5.large, provisioned concurrency 2 during 7am-7pm EST). DeidentificationService module (Safe Harbor 18-identifier removal pipeline). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with KMS encryption and read replica. ElastiCache Redis 7 (session tokens, risk score TTL cache). S3 bucket for audit log archival with 6-year lifecycle. Okta SAML SSO integration. Kinesis Data Streams for population analytics event emission.
target_output.workflows: Intake Risk Scoring Workflow: Clinician triggers assessment review -> FastAPI /risk-scores POST -> RiskScoringService checks Redis cache; on miss, constructs feature vector from intake_assessment questionnaire responses, invokes SageMaker endpoint, receives {risk_level, confidence}, writes risk_scores record, caches in Redis for 4 hours keyed by assessment_id -> clinician dashboard reflects risk badge within 3 seconds. Care Pathway Generation Workflow: After risk score is available -> async worker dequeues assessment -> DeidentificationService substitutes patient_id with encounter token, name with [PATIENT], DOB with age bucket, removes 15 remaining Safe Harbor identifiers -> builds GPT-4o prompt with de-identified symptom summary and ICD-10 context -> Azure OpenAI returns structured JSON with recommendation_text, dsm5_codes array, urgency level -> CarePathwayService parses response, writes care_pathway_recommendations record -> clinician dashboard care pathway card renders. Clinician Override Workflow: Clinician disagrees with AI risk level -> opens override form -> selects override_risk_level, enters mandatory rationale text -> OverrideService validates rationale length > 20 chars, writes clinician_overrides record, emits audit_log entry with original and overridden risk levels -> supervisor notification triggered for high-to-low overrides.
target_output.data_model: patients (id UUID PK, mrn_encrypted TEXT, name_encrypted TEXT, dob_encrypted TEXT, assigned_clinician_id UUID FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). intake_assessments (id UUID PK, patient_id UUID FK, questionnaire_version TEXT, responses_json JSONB, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, processed BOOLEAN DEFAULT false). risk_scores (id UUID PK, assessment_id UUID FK, model_version TEXT, score NUMERIC(4,3), risk_level TEXT CHECK(risk_level IN ('low','moderate','high')), confidence NUMERIC(4,3), scored_at TIMESTAMPTZ). care_pathway_recommendations (id UUID PK, assessment_id UUID FK, recommendation_text TEXT, dsm5_codes TEXT[], urgency TEXT CHECK(urgency IN ('routine','urgent','crisis')), generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, model_version TEXT). clinician_overrides (id UUID PK, assessment_id UUID FK, original_risk_level TEXT, override_risk_level TEXT, rationale TEXT NOT NULL, clinician_id UUID FK, overridden_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_logs (id UUID PK, accessor_id UUID FK, patient_id UUID, resource_type TEXT, action TEXT, ip_address INET, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ). All PHI columns encrypted with KMS-managed key; JSONB responses_json encrypted at column level.
target_output.api_design: RESTful API at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.1. POST /intakes (ingest questionnaire submission, returns assessment_id). GET /intakes/{assessment_id} (retrieve questionnaire responses for clinician review). POST /risk-scores/{assessment_id} (trigger or retrieve cached score, returns {risk_level, confidence, scored_at}). GET /recommendations/{assessment_id} (returns {recommendation_text, dsm5_codes, urgency, generated_at}). POST /overrides (submit clinician override: {assessment_id, override_risk_level, rationale}). GET /audit?patient_id=&start=&end= (compliance audit log search, admin role only). GET /analytics/risk-distribution?window_days=30 (population cohort metrics, supervisor role).
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA controls: BAA with AWS, KMS encryption for all PHI at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, CloudTrail for all AWS API actions. Okta SAML SSO with MFA enforced for all roles. RBAC row-level security on patients table: clinicians read only assigned panel. PHI-to-LLM de-identification: DeidentificationService applies HIPAA Safe Harbor 18-identifier removal before any LLM prompt construction: patient_id -> encounter-scoped UUID, name -> [PATIENT], DOB -> 10-year age bucket, geographic data removed, dates shifted. Azure OpenAI endpoint configured with abuse_monitoring=false. Immutable audit_log table captures every PHI access, risk score retrieval, care pathway generation, and clinician override with accessor_id, patient_id, action, and timestamp. Audit records replicated to S3 with 6-year retention. Joint Commission compliance: all clinician overrides require written rationale; supervisor escalation for high-to-low risk overrides. WAF on ALB blocks SQLi and XSS patterns.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest with 85% coverage gate (including DeidentificationService tests verifying Safe Harbor coverage), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy via CodeDeploy blue/green with 10-minute canary period. ECS Fargate API service: 2-4 tasks (auto-scale CPU 60%), 1 vCPU 2GB. Async LLM worker: 1-3 tasks (queue depth scaling). SageMaker endpoint: ml.m5.large, provisioned concurrency 2 during 7am-7pm EST via CloudWatch scheduled scaling, scale-to-1 outside hours. RDS Multi-AZ failover with automated backups. Environments: dev (single-AZ, no provisioned concurrency), staging (mirrors production, used for clinical UAT), production (Multi-AZ, full provisioned concurrency). Model updates: SageMaker Model Registry pipeline with clinical validation gate requiring supervisor approval before endpoint swap.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM agent on all ECS Fargate tasks. Custom metrics emitted to Datadog: risk_score_inference_latency_p99 (SageMaker), llm_care_pathway_duration_p99, clinician_override_rate_7d, high_risk_patient_count, intake_queue_depth. PagerDuty integration for: FastAPI error rate >2% in 5-minute window, risk scoring latency p99 >5 seconds, SageMaker endpoint InvocationModelError rate >1%, override_rate_7d >30% (potential model drift signal). Datadog dashboard: real-time risk score distribution heatmap, override rate trend, LLM cost per 1000 inferences.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate API: ~$180/month for 2 baseline tasks. SageMaker provisioned concurrency 2x ml.m5.large during clinic hours: ~$220/month. RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.large: ~$150/month. ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.small: ~$30/month. Azure OpenAI GPT-4o: ~$0.015 per care pathway generation; at 500 assessments/day ~$225/month. Total estimated: ~$850/month at 500 daily assessments. At 2000 daily: scale ECS to 6 tasks, add RDS read replica, SageMaker provisioned concurrency to 4, estimated $1,800/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Okta SAML SSO integration, intake questionnaire ingestion API, SageMaker BERT classifier endpoint with provisioned concurrency, risk scoring pipeline with Redis TTL cache, clinician review interface with risk badges, immutable audit_log, HIPAA security controls, Joint Commission override audit trail. Phase 2 (6 weeks): DeidentificationService Safe Harbor pipeline, Azure OpenAI GPT-4o care pathway generation with defined recommendation schema (dsm5_codes, urgency), async LLM worker service, care pathway card in clinician UI. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Population analytics dashboard, Kinesis event streaming, supervisor override escalation notifications, admin model version management panel.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy strict mode and ruff linting. FastAPI endpoints typed with Pydantic v2 models for all request/response shapes including care_pathway_recommendations schema. DeidentificationService covered by dedicated unit tests asserting all 18 Safe Harbor identifiers are removed from sample PHI payloads. 85% pytest coverage gate. Next.js TypeScript strict mode. API contracts defined as OpenAPI 3.1 spec committed to repo; frontend generates typed clients via openapi-typescript. Feature flags for LLM integration to allow deployment without Azure OpenAI dependency in staging.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Fine-tuned BERT classifier model drifts as patient population demographics shift. Mitigation: monitor override_rate_7d metric; >25% sustained override rate triggers model retraining review with clinical validation gate. Risk: Azure OpenAI service outage blocks care pathway generation. Mitigation: async worker with retry queue; clinicians can proceed with risk score alone if recommendation is delayed. Risk: HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification may over-generalize (e.g., age buckets reduce utility for geriatric psychiatry). Mitigation: Expert Determination de-identification review if clinical team requests higher precision. Tradeoff: Redis TTL cache for risk scores trades freshness (4-hour stale window) for reduced SageMaker inference costs.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Fine-tuned BERT classifier selected over GPT-4o for risk scoring to keep PHI on-premise. HIPAA Safe Harbor chosen over Expert Determination as baseline for LLM de-identification. care_pathway_recommendations schema defined as structured record with dsm5_codes array and urgency enum. SageMaker provisioned concurrency 2 specified for clinic hours window.

metadata.notes: Psychiatric clinical AI; round 2 adds Safe Harbor de-identification pipeline and care pathway recommendation schema definition.

profile.domain: Public-facing resource hub for a national mental health non-profit with searchable self-help articles, crisis hotline directories, and CBT worksheets that must handle traffic spikes during awareness campaigns

project_goal.value: Public-facing resource hub for a national mental health non-profit offering a searchable library of self-help articles, crisis hotline directories, and downloadable CBT worksheets, capable of handling traffic spikes during mental health awareness campaigns
project_goal.rationale: Core mission is accessible, always-on mental health resources for the general public; spike resilience is critical during World Mental Health Day and similar campaigns

target_users.value: General public seeking mental health resources; no authentication required
target_users.rationale: Broadest possible accessibility is the mission; barriers like login would reduce reach for people in distress

project_class.rationale: No server-side runtime; Astro generates static HTML/CSS/JS deployed to S3 and served via CloudFront

capabilities.rationale: Static content delivery plus GA4 and CloudWatch analytics; no backend, auth, or database

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with client-side search; complexity comes from content pipeline and CDN configuration, not runtime architecture

risk_level.rationale: No user data collected, no authentication, no database; primary risk is CDN availability during traffic spikes

data_sensitivity.rationale: No personal data collected; site is purely informational with downloadable static assets

external_exposure.rationale: Must be accessible to anyone globally without barriers

access_model.value: Fully public; no authentication required for any resource
access_model.rationale: All resources must be freely accessible to maximize reach

feature_scope.value: Searchable article library with category filtering, crisis hotline directory with location-based filtering, downloadable CBT worksheets, awareness campaign landing sections, resource category taxonomy
feature_scope.rationale: Core informational features for public mental health resource access

mvp_scope.value: Static article library with Pagefind client-side search, crisis hotline directory, worksheet download pages, GA4 analytics integration
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable to replace legacy WordPress site with faster, more scalable static delivery

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via CloudFront with ACM certificate; CSP headers preventing inline script injection; no user data collection
security_baseline.rationale: Static site threat model focuses on content integrity and XSS prevention via CSP; no auth surface to protect

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with React islands and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro generates zero-JS-by-default static HTML; React islands for interactive search and filtering components only

backend_stack.value: None; static site only
backend_stack.rationale: No server-side runtime; all content served as pre-rendered static assets from CloudFront

data_platform.value: None; content managed via headless CMS (Contentful); no database
data_platform.rationale: CMS provides editorial workflow; Astro pulls content at build time; no runtime database queries

hosting_target.value: AWS S3 + CloudFront with global edge distribution
hosting_target.rationale: S3 for static asset origin; CloudFront for global CDN edge caching and traffic spike absorption

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected or stored; GA4 analytics configured with IP anonymization and no PII tracking; cookie consent banner required for GDPR compliance
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR compliance required for EU visitors; IP anonymization ensures no personal data in analytics

future_scope.value: Multilingual content, crisis chat widget integration, resource recommendation engine
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP expansion for international reach and interactive crisis support

constraints.value: No server-side runtime; all pages must be statically rendered at build time; build time must stay under 10 minutes for acceptable CI/CD cycle
constraints.rationale: Static-only constraint ensures CDN-level scalability; 10-minute build cap ensures content editors see changes within 15 minutes of publish

observability_baseline.value: CloudFront access logs to S3 and CloudWatch metrics for cache hit rate and error rates; GA4 for user behavior analytics
observability_baseline.rationale: Static site observability via CDN metrics and web analytics; no application-level APM needed

execution_preference.value: Content editors use Contentful; engineers deploy via GitHub Actions CI/CD; releases on every CMS publish and every code merge to main
execution_preference.rationale: Contentful webhook triggers GitHub Actions rebuild on content publish; code merges also trigger rebuild

project.goal: Static resource hub for national mental health non-profit
project.target_users: General public seeking mental health resources
project.timeline: Q2 launch replacing legacy WordPress site

frontend.framework: Astro 4 with React islands and TypeScript
frontend.search: Pagefind for client-side full-text search of articles and worksheets

security.headers: CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options via CloudFront response headers policy

data.cms: Contentful headless CMS for articles, hotlines, worksheets

devops.triggers: Contentful webhook on publish, GitHub push to main

open_questions.search_index: Whether Pagefind index size will stay within acceptable limits as content library grows beyond 500 articles

reasoner_reviews.product: Astro 4 with Contentful is the right combination for a content-heavy static site with non-technical editors. The client-side Pagefind search is appropriate given the no-server-runtime constraint. Round 1 plan did not define the CloudFront cache invalidation strategy on Contentful publishes; without targeted invalidation, content editors will see stale pages for hours after publishing.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: S3 + CloudFront with Astro static output is the correct architecture for absorbing awareness campaign traffic spikes without any origin scaling. The Pagefind index is built at Astro build time and served as a static asset from CloudFront, which is the right pattern. However, round 1 did not specify the CloudFront cache TTL configuration or the invalidation scope on deployment; this must be addressed.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers are referenced in round 1 but the specific directives are not defined. For a site with React hydration islands, the CSP must allow the Astro client-side JS bundle's hash or nonce. A wildcard script-src defeats the purpose. The round 1 plan should specify at minimum: default-src 'self', script-src with nonce or hash, style-src 'self', img-src 'self' data: https:.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Pagefind index generation happens at end of Astro build. At 500+ articles with downloadable worksheets, index generation adds 2-3 minutes. Round 1 did not account for this in the 10-minute build budget. Incremental content builds or Pagefind index caching should be considered.

backend.service_design: No server-side backend; Astro build pipeline acts as the build-time content aggregator. Contentful Content Delivery API called during Astro build via @astrojs/contentful integration to fetch articles, hotlines, worksheets, and awareness_campaigns collections.
backend.api_patterns: No runtime API. Contentful webhook POST to GitHub Actions dispatch endpoint triggers rebuild on content publish. GA4 Measurement Protocol used for server-side event logging if needed for campaign attribution.
backend.failure_handling: Astro build fails loudly on Contentful API errors; GitHub Actions workflow marks build as failed and notifies Slack channel; prior deployment remains live on CloudFront.

security.auth_design: No authentication; fully public site.
security.secrets_management: Contentful API key stored as GitHub Actions secret; never embedded in static build output. CloudFront distribution and S3 bucket policies allow public read of /public/* paths only.
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront rate limiting via WAF managed rule group (AWS-AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet) to block credential stuffing and scrapers. No user-submitted forms in MVP.
security.privacy_controls: GA4 configured with anonymize_ip: true and no User-ID tracking. Cookie consent banner (Cookiebot or custom) required before GA4 fires. GDPR-compliant privacy policy page linked from footer.

data.entities: articles (Contentful content type: title, slug, body_richtext, category_ref, tags[], published_date, seo_meta). resource_categories (Contentful: name, slug, icon, parent_category_ref). crisis_hotlines (Contentful: organization_name, phone_number, text_number, hours, geographic_coverage, languages[]). worksheets (Contentful: title, description, pdf_asset_ref, cbt_technique_tag, difficulty_level). awareness_campaigns (Contentful: campaign_name, hero_text, featured_article_refs[], dates). All content fetched at build time; no runtime database.
data.storage_design: Contentful as content store; Astro generates static HTML files per article, hotline listing, worksheet page. Pagefind index built from static output. S3 bucket stores all static assets with versioned deployment prefix.
data.analytics_events: page_view, article_read (article_slug, category), worksheet_download (worksheet_slug, technique), hotline_click (organization_name, phone_obscured), search_query (query_term, results_count) tracked via GA4 gtag.js.

devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket (static asset origin, versioned prefixes for atomic deployments), CloudFront distribution with S3 origin and custom response headers policy (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options). Route53 alias record. ACM certificate for custom domain.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow: (1) checkout, (2) bun install, (3) astro build with Contentful API key from secret, (4) pagefind --source dist, (5) aws s3 sync dist/ s3://bucket/deploy-{sha}/ --delete, (6) CloudFront create-invalidation on /*. Triggered by: push to main branch and Contentful webhook via repository_dispatch event.
devops.observability_stack: CloudFront access logs delivered to S3 with Athena for ad-hoc query. CloudWatch metrics: 5xxErrorRate (alarm >0.5%), CacheHitRate (alert if <85% sustained). GA4 real-time dashboard during awareness campaigns.
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous deployment prefix remains in S3; rollback by updating CloudFront origin path to prior deploy-{sha} prefix and running invalidation. RollbackScript in Makefile for one-command revert.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 site with React island components for interactive search and crisis hotline filtering; static HTML shells for all article and worksheet pages
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home, Searchable Article Library, Crisis Hotline Directory (location-filtered), CBT Worksheet Downloads, Campaign Landing Sections
frontend.ui_modules: ArticleSearchBar island, HotlineCard with tel: links, WorksheetDownloadGrid, CampaignBanner component
frontend.accessibility_notes: WCAG 2.1 AA required; crisis hotline CTAs must be keyboard-reachable and screen-reader announced; avoid color-only distress indicators
frontend.performance_notes: Astro partial hydration keeps JS payload under 50 KB; preload crisis hotline data as inline JSON to eliminate fetch latency on high-traffic campaign days

ISS-001.title: CloudFront cache invalidation strategy not defined
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described deployment to S3 and CloudFront but did not specify cache TTL configuration or invalidation scope on each deployment. Content editors publishing new crisis hotline numbers may wait hours before edge caches refresh.

ISS-002.title: CSP header directives not specified
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 referenced CSP headers but did not define specific directives. Astro React hydration islands require script-src configuration that must not use unsafe-inline or wildcard.

Specify CSP directives: default-src self, script-src with nonce strategy for Astro hydration bundles

previous_audits.summary: Correct static site architecture with Astro + CloudFront for spike resilience. Two gaps: CloudFront invalidation strategy is undefined (content staleness risk) and CSP directives are missing (XSS protection incomplete for React island hydration).
strengths: Astro 4 static generation with CloudFront edge caching correctly handles awareness campaign traffic spikes without origin scaling
Pagefind client-side search respects no-server-runtime constraint
Contentful webhook triggering GitHub Actions rebuild is the right editorial pipeline
