OpenAI DPA requirement in contract not reflected as a deployment gate
LLM prompt construction does not show boundary markers to prevent injection

previous_plan.title: Serenity Journaling AI - Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: FastAPI microservices on ECS Fargate replacing PHP monolith, with OpenAI GPT-4o streaming for guided journaling sessions and Celery-based weekly summarization pipeline.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three FastAPI services (journal-api, llm-gateway, summarization-worker) deployed on ECS Fargate behind ALB. PostgreSQL 16 RDS for persistence. PHP backend decommissioned via ALB cutover.
previous_plan.migration_notes: PHP MySQL to PostgreSQL migration via direct data export and ALB traffic switch. No shadow-write phase described.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Auth0 JWT on all routes. KMS encryption at rest. No prompt injection mitigations. No OpenAI DPA gate in deployment pipeline.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI with Docker build and ECR push. ECS rolling deploy. ALB target group switch for PHP cutover. No pre-production DPA checklist.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision targeting ISS-002 (PHP migration shadow-write phase) and ISS-003 (OpenAI DPA deployment gate). ISS-001 (prompt injection) was resolved in this round via XML delimiter boundaries in the llm_integration_design. The migration section needs a detailed 3-phase approach: shadow-write, validation, graduated ALB weighted routing cutover. The deployment pipeline needs an explicit manual approval gate tied to DPA confirmation before any production deployment sends user data to OpenAI.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced big-bang PHP cutover description with explicit 3-phase migration: (1) shadow-write period where PHP writes to MySQL AND PostgreSQL for 2 weeks, (2) daily automated validation job comparing row counts and checksums, (3) graduated ALB weighted routing cutover from 0% to 10% to 50% to 100% Fargate with rollback trigger if error rate exceeds 1%.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero data loss during PHP cutover; rollback possible within 5 minutes by setting ALB weight back to 0% Fargate

fix_report.action_taken: Added OpenAI DPA confirmation as a required manual approval step in the GitHub Actions production deployment workflow. CI/CD pipeline blocks the deploy-to-production job until a team lead approves a DPA-confirmed checklist item. Environment variable OPENAI_DPA_CONFIRMED must be set in the production environment secrets before the llm-gateway service can start.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No user emotional health data can reach OpenAI API in production until DPA is legally confirmed and the environment gate is manually set

target_output.title: Serenity Journaling AI - Containerized Microservices Architecture (R2)
target_output.executive_summary: A privacy-first AI journaling platform migrated from a PHP monolith to three FastAPI microservices on AWS ECS Fargate. OpenAI GPT-4o streams reflective prompts to users in real time via Server-Sent Events, with emotional theme detection and weekly wellness digest generation via Celery jobs. This revision adds a shadow-write PHP migration phase with automated data validation, graduated ALB cutover, and an OpenAI DPA confirmation gate in the CI/CD pipeline.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three containerized FastAPI services on ECS Fargate in us-east-1: journal-api handles session management and CRUD with Auth0 JWT validation; llm-gateway proxies OpenAI GPT-4o streaming calls with prompt construction using XML injection boundaries and token usage accounting; summarization-worker runs as a Celery consumer processing weekly digest jobs from Redis queue. All services connect to PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with KMS encryption. Redis 7 ElastiCache serves as Celery broker and active session state store. PHP monolith migration uses a 2-week shadow-write phase with daily validation jobs before graduated ALB weighted routing cutover. React 18 Vite SPA communicates with journal-api via REST and receives LLM streaming via SSE from llm-gateway.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Zustand, React Query, EventSource SSE client. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, async SQLAlchemy, Alembic, OpenAI Python SDK (streaming), Celery 5. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (KMS encrypted). Cache/Broker: Redis 7 ElastiCache. Auth: Auth0 (Regular Web Application + M2M). Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with weighted target groups for migration cutover, ECR, AWS KMS, S3 for exports. Observability: Datadog APM, structured JSON logging, PagerDuty. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with manual DPA approval gate.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Journal Session Module: creates conversation_thread, fetches personalized LLM reflective prompt from llm-gateway, streams GPT-4o response via SSE to React client, saves journal_entry on user submission with emotional_themes JSONB populated by classification call. Emotional Theme Tracker: aggregates theme labels from journal_entries into emotional_themes table, displayed as 30-day radar chart in React. Weekly Wellness Digest: Celery beat job extracts weekly themes, generates summary via OpenAI batch API, writes weekly_summaries row, sends SES email with digest HTML. User Preferences: tone_preference (reflective/direct/gentle) and ai_consent captured at signup, stored in user_preferences, injected into LLM system prompt.
target_output.system_components: ALB with two target groups (PHP legacy weight, Fargate weight) for graduated migration cutover. ECS Fargate cluster: journal-api service (2-4 tasks auto-scaling), llm-gateway service (2-6 tasks scaling on LLM request queue depth), summarization-worker service (1-2 tasks scaling on Celery queue depth). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with read replica for analytics queries. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode. Auth0 tenant with Regular Web Application and M2M client. S3 bucket for user data export archives. AWS SES for weekly digest delivery. Datadog agent sidecar on each Fargate task.
target_output.workflows: Journal Session Workflow: User authenticates via Auth0, React SPA receives JWT. POST /threads creates conversation_thread in journal-api. journal-api calls llm-gateway POST /prompt, which constructs system prompt with tone preference and top emotional themes, sends to OpenAI GPT-4o with stream=true, returns SSE to React client via EventSource. User types response and submits. journal-api saves journal_entry, triggers async emotional theme classification call to llm-gateway, updates emotional_themes table. PHP Shadow-Write Migration Workflow: During 2-week shadow period, PHP writes to MySQL; a sidecar migration service reads MySQL binlog via Debezium and replays writes to PostgreSQL in near-real time. Daily validation job queries row counts and SHA256 checksums on 1000 sampled rows from both databases, writes comparison report to S3 and alerts on mismatch. After 14 days of clean validation, ALB weighted rule shifts 10% traffic to Fargate, monitored for 48 hours, then 50%, then 100%.
target_output.data_model: journal_entries (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, thread_id UUID FK, entry_text TEXT encrypted via KMS envelope, emotional_themes JSONB [{label,confidence}], word_count INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). conversation_threads (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, llm_prompt_id UUID FK, status ENUM active/completed/abandoned). emotional_themes (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, theme_label VARCHAR, detected_count INT, first_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ). weekly_summaries (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, week_start DATE, summary_text TEXT, top_themes JSONB, digest_sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). llm_prompts (id UUID PK, prompt_template TEXT, tone_category ENUM reflective/direct/gentle, version INT, active BOOLEAN). user_preferences (user_id UUID PK, tone_preference ENUM, ai_consent BOOLEAN NOT NULL, digest_email_enabled BOOLEAN, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Clinical support entities: patient_profiles (id UUID PK, user_id FK, primary_diagnosis_icd10 VARCHAR, assigned_therapist_id UUID, treatment_plan_notes_encrypted TEXT, emergency_contact_json JSONB), clinical_encounters (id UUID PK, user_id FK, encounter_type ENUM intake/session/crisis, therapist_id UUID, clinical_notes_encrypted TEXT, vitals_json JSONB, encounter_date DATE), prescriptions (id UUID PK, user_id FK, medication_name VARCHAR, dosage VARCHAR, prescriber_id UUID, status ENUM active/discontinued).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/threads: create journal session, returns {thread_id, status}. GET /api/v1/threads/{thread_id}/stream: SSE endpoint proxying llm-gateway GPT-4o streaming prompt. POST /api/v1/threads/{thread_id}/entries: submit journal response, triggers async emotional theme classification. GET /api/v1/entries?limit=20&before=cursor: paginated journal history. GET /api/v1/themes/summary?days=30: aggregated emotional theme counts for radar chart. GET /api/v1/summaries/weekly: list of weekly_summaries with theme_counts. PATCH /api/v1/preferences: update tone_preference and digest_email_enabled. All routes require Authorization: Bearer {auth0_jwt} header; 401 on missing/invalid token; 403 if user_id claim does not match resource owner.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 JWT validation: FastAPI middleware verifies RS256 signature, audience=api.serenityjournal.com, issuer=serenityjournal.auth0.com on every request. Row-level isolation: all SQL queries filter WHERE user_id = :current_user_id enforced by SQLAlchemy query helper, no raw SQL bypasses. KMS envelope encryption for journal entry text at rest. TLS 1.3 enforced on ALB listener. LLM prompt injection mitigation: user journal text wrapped in <user_input>...</user_input> XML delimiters in system prompt construction. OpenAI DPA deployment gate: OPENAI_DPA_CONFIRMED=true must be set in ECS task environment before llm-gateway container starts (checked at startup with hard exit if missing). User ai_consent=true required before any journal content is sent to llm-gateway. GDPR-aligned account deletion: soft delete triggers hard delete job at 30 days.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: lint (ruff, mypy), pytest (85% coverage gate), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy for journal-api and summarization-worker. Manual approval gate required before deploy-to-production job: approver confirms OpenAI DPA signed and OPENAI_DPA_CONFIRMED secret set. llm-gateway startup script validates OPENAI_DPA_CONFIRMED env var and exits 1 if absent. Migration cutover: ALB weighted routing rules updated via Terraform. Rollback: ALB weight set to 0% Fargate, 100% PHP within 5 minutes via manual Terraform apply or automated trigger if error rate >1% detected by Datadog monitor. Environments: dev (single AZ, shared Redis), staging (multi-AZ, mirrors production config), production (multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c, Fargate auto-scaling 2-8 tasks per service).
target_output.observability: Datadog APM tracing with trace_id propagated from ALB access log through journal-api, llm-gateway, and PostgreSQL query spans. Custom metrics: llm_tokens_used_total (per model, per user cohort), journal_session_duration_seconds, emotional_theme_classification_latency_ms, weekly_digest_job_duration_seconds, php_migration_validation_mismatch_count. PagerDuty alerts: API error rate >2% (P1), LLM gateway p99 latency >8s (P2), Celery queue depth >200 (P2), migration validation mismatch detected (P1). Structured JSON logs with user_id hashed for privacy in log entries.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ~$2,400/month at 500 active users (Fargate 6 tasks, RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium, Redis cache.t3.small, OpenAI GPT-4o ~$0.005/journal session average). ECS auto-scaling on llm-gateway based on request queue depth in Redis. At 10,000 users, OpenAI token costs dominate (~$800/month); evaluate GPT-4o-mini for theme classification to reduce cost by ~60%. Redis memory scales with active session count at ~2KB per active thread.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (sprints 1-4): journal-api and llm-gateway services deployed to Fargate, React SPA with session creation and SSE streaming, Auth0 integration, PostgreSQL schema with Alembic migrations, shadow-write migration infrastructure (Debezium + validation job), OpenAI DPA gate in CI/CD. Phase 2 (sprints 5-6): PHP migration cutover via graduated ALB routing, emotional theme radar chart, Celery weekly digest jobs, SES email delivery. Phase 3 (sprints 7-8): User preferences UI, longitudinal trend analytics, data export feature, performance optimization for token cost reduction.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: ruff linter + mypy strict mode in CI. FastAPI route handlers have no business logic; domain logic in service layer. All database queries via SQLAlchemy ORM with user_id scoping helper, never raw SQL. pytest with asyncio mode for async route tests, 85% coverage gate. React: ESLint + TypeScript strict, React Query for all server state, no direct fetch calls in components. ADR documents for LLM provider selection and migration strategy. Feature flags via environment variables for graduated rollout.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OpenAI GPT-4o cost at scale beyond 10K active users could exceed $3K/month budget. Mitigation: GPT-4o-mini for emotional theme classification, GPT-4o only for prompt generation. Risk: Debezium MySQL binlog replication lag during shadow-write causing PostgreSQL data to be 10-30 seconds behind PHP. Mitigation: validation job uses T-5 minute snapshots to allow replication lag. Risk: Auth0 outage blocking all journal access (single auth dependency). Mitigation: cached JWT public keys allow 15-minute grace period during Auth0 unavailability. Tradeoff: PHP shadow-write phase adds 2 weeks to launch timeline but reduces migration data loss risk from high to low.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected OpenAI GPT-4o over Anthropic Claude for journaling prompts based on instruction-following benchmarks for emotional tone. Shadow-write approach chosen over snapshot migration to eliminate downtime requirement. XML delimiter prompt injection mitigation chosen over LangChain prompt templates to avoid additional dependency. Weekly digest via Celery beat confirmed over event-driven approach for predictable SES sending patterns.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision addressing PHP migration rollback gap and OpenAI DPA deployment gate; ISS-001 prompt injection resolved; moderate quality as deployment section is detailed but cost projections at scale are estimates.

profile.domain: Mental Health Research / ML Infrastructure

project_goal.value: Secure ML training and inference infrastructure for a mental health research consortium, providing reproducible Kubeflow pipeline orchestration, MLflow model versioning, and HIPAA-compliant data handling for depression-prediction models trained on de-identified EHR datasets
project_goal.rationale: Consortium research mission requires reproducible, auditable ML pipelines on sensitive de-identified health data

target_users.value: Research data scientists and ML engineers at consortium member institutions who submit training runs and consume deployed inference endpoints
target_users.rationale: Internal research users with institutional credentials, no public access

project_class.rationale: Platform/environment architecture: Kubernetes cluster, Kubeflow pipelines, MLflow registry, not a hosted application

capabilities.rationale: Training pipeline orchestration, data storage, batch training jobs on GPU nodes, and DevOps automation

complexity_level.rationale: GPU-accelerated training at scale, multi-institution data ingestion, Kubeflow pipeline DAGs, MLflow registry with model promotion gates

risk_level.rationale: De-identified EHR data is still health data subject to HIPAA; data use agreements with consortium institutions are legally binding

data_sensitivity.rationale: De-identified EHR datasets for depression prediction retain HIPAA sensitivity obligations under data use agreements

external_exposure.rationale: Kubeflow dashboard, MLflow UI, and inference endpoints accessible only within consortium VPN; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles for service-to-service; HashiCorp Vault for researcher credentials and dataset access keys; Kubeflow RBAC for pipeline submission per institution namespace
access_model.rationale: Multi-institution consortium requires namespace-isolated access control

feature_scope.value: Kubeflow pipeline authoring and submission, automated GPU training job scheduling, MLflow experiment tracking and model registry, automated evaluation gate for bias and performance thresholds, model artifact storage in S3, data lineage recording, inference endpoint deployment via KFServing
feature_scope.rationale: Full ML lifecycle management from data to deployed model

mvp_scope.value: Kubeflow pipeline execution for training jobs, MLflow tracking and model registration, S3 dataset versioning, automated evaluation gate; KFServing inference and bias metrics in phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP delivers reproducible training pipeline; inference serving is phase 2

security_baseline.value: All EHR dataset S3 buckets KMS-encrypted, VPC with no public subnets for EKS worker nodes, Vault dynamic credentials for dataset access, audit log of all data reads to CloudTrail, GitLab CI pipeline signing for pipeline artifacts
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA minimum controls for de-identified health research data

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure project with Kubeflow dashboard and MLflow UI as management interfaces; no custom frontend built
frontend_stack.rationale: Kubeflow and MLflow provide their own UIs; no bespoke frontend required

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Kubeflow Pipelines SDK for pipeline component authoring; FastAPI for custom evaluation gate service; Vault agent for secrets injection
backend_stack.rationale: Kubeflow SDK is Python-native; FastAPI evaluation gate enables custom threshold logic

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for Kubeflow and MLflow metadata stores; AWS S3 with versioning for training datasets and model artifacts; no cache layer
data_platform.rationale: Standard Kubeflow/MLflow metadata backend; S3 for scalable artifact storage

hosting_target.value: EKS on EC2 GPU instances (p3.2xlarge for training, m5.xlarge for CPU pipeline steps) in private VPC subnets, accessible via consortium VPN
hosting_target.rationale: GPU training requires EC2 instance types; EKS provides Kubernetes orchestration for Kubeflow

privacy_retention_policy.value: De-identified EHR datasets retained per data use agreement terms (typically 5 years); model artifacts retained indefinitely in MLflow registry; pipeline run logs retained 3 years; CloudTrail audit logs retained 7 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA and consortium DUA retention obligations

future_scope.value: KFServing online inference endpoints for consortium applications, federated learning across institution data silos, automated fairness reporting dashboard

constraints.value: All training data must remain in us-east-1 per data residency requirements of DUAs; no data exfiltration to external services; GPU node pool budget capped at $8,000/month for training compute; pipeline runs must be reproducible (deterministic seeds, pinned dependencies)
constraints.rationale: DUA legal constraints and budget limits

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for cluster and pod metrics; MLflow for experiment tracking and metric logging; Grafana dashboards for GPU utilization, training throughput, and evaluation gate pass rates
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard Kubernetes observability stack integrated with ML-specific tracking

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code via Terraform; GitLab CI for pipeline artifact CI; all Kubeflow components versioned in Git; IaC changes require peer review
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducibility and auditability are first-class requirements for research infrastructure

compliance_context.value: HIPAA compliance for de-identified EHR research data; HITECH breach notification requirements; data use agreements with each consortium member institution governing data use scope and researcher access; IRB approval required per study protocol
compliance_context.rationale: Multi-institution health research data handling has layered HIPAA and DUA obligations

project.goal: Provision HIPAA-compliant ML training infrastructure for depression-prediction model research
project.target_users: Data scientists and ML engineers at 4 consortium institutions
project.timeline: Q2 infrastructure provisioning, Q3 first research runs

backend.secrets: HashiCorp Vault with Kubernetes auth method

security.access_control: AWS IAM + Vault dynamic credentials + Kubeflow RBAC per institution namespace
security.encryption: S3 KMS, EKS secrets KMS, TLS everywhere
security.audit: CloudTrail for S3 data access events, Vault audit log

data.metadata_db: PostgreSQL 16 RDS for Kubeflow and MLflow backends
data.artifact_store: S3 with versioning and KMS encryption
data.pipeline_cache: Kubeflow pipeline step caching in S3

devops.ci: GitLab CI for Terraform and pipeline components
devops.hosting: EKS on EC2 in private VPC subnets
devops.environments: dev cluster (t3.xlarge CPUs), research-prod cluster (p3.2xlarge GPUs)

open_questions.federated_learning: Evaluate for phase 3 when cross-institution data sharing is required

reasoner_reviews.product: The infrastructure mission is reproducible, auditable ML training for depression-prediction research. Pipeline step caching in Kubeflow is critical to avoid re-running expensive GPU training steps during development. The automated evaluation gate for bias metrics is a differentiator that demonstrates responsible AI practices to the consortium IRB.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kubeflow Pipelines v2 SDK with Argo Workflows backend on EKS is the right orchestration layer. Each pipeline component should be containerized independently to enable individual component version pinning. The evaluation gate as a FastAPI microservice rather than a Kubeflow component allows it to be reused across pipeline versions without redeployment.
reasoner_reviews.security: The multi-institution namespace isolation in Kubeflow must be enforced at the Kubernetes NetworkPolicy level, not just at the Kubeflow RBAC level. Vault dynamic S3 credentials with per-institution IAM roles prevent cross-institution data access even if a Kubeflow credential is compromised. CloudTrail must log S3 object-level events (not just bucket-level) for HIPAA audit trail completeness.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GPU budget of $8K/month requires aggressive Spot Instance usage for training jobs with checkpointing enabled. Spot interruption handling in Kubeflow pipeline components is essential to avoid wasted GPU hours. Kubeflow step caching reduces redundant compute for iterative pipeline development.

backend.service_design: Kubeflow Pipelines SDK v2 components: data_validation_component (Great Expectations checks on de-identified dataset schema), feature_engineering_component (scikit-learn pipelines, deterministic random seeds), model_training_component (PyTorch Lightning training loop on GPU, MLflow autologging), model_evaluation_component (threshold checks for AUC-ROC, F1, demographic parity). FastAPI evaluation gate service deployed as EKS Deployment: POST /evaluate/promotion accepts {run_id, thresholds_config} and returns {pass: bool, metric_results: {}, bias_report: {}}.
backend.background_jobs: GitLab CI scheduled pipeline weekly: dataset_freshness_check job validates S3 dataset checksums. Kubeflow pipeline runs triggered via Python SDK from GitLab CI on researcher-submitted configuration YAML. GPU Spot Instance interruption handler: Kubeflow pipeline component checkpoint wrapper saves model state to S3 every 10 minutes.
backend.failure_handling: Kubeflow pipeline step retry policy: 2 retries on transient failures. GPU Spot interruption triggers Kubeflow pipeline step restart from last S3 checkpoint. Evaluation gate rejection writes rejection reason to MLflow run tags; pipeline terminates without model registration.
backend.scaling_notes: EKS Cluster Autoscaler scales p3.2xlarge GPU node pool 0-10 nodes based on pending Kubeflow training pods. CPU node pool (m5.xlarge) handles pipeline coordination steps and evaluation gate. Karpenter considered for faster node provisioning (sub-2-minute vs CA 3-minute).

security.auth_design: Kubeflow authentication via AWS IAM + OIDC with Dex as OIDC provider federated to each institution LDAP. Researchers authenticate via institutional SSO; Dex issues short-lived OIDC tokens consumed by Kubeflow dashboard.
security.authorization_model: Kubeflow namespace per institution (namespace: consortium-mgh, consortium-jhu, etc.). NetworkPolicy denies cross-namespace pod-to-pod communication. S3 bucket policy restricts dataset access to the corresponding institution IAM role. Vault AppRole per namespace issues dynamic S3 access keys scoped to institution-specific prefix.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail with S3 data events logging for all GetObject and PutObject calls on training dataset buckets. Vault audit log captures all credential issuance events with requesting pod identity. MLflow run metadata captures pipeline component versions, git commit hashes, and dataset S3 URIs for full data lineage.
security.secrets_management: HashiCorp Vault deployed on EKS with Vault Agent Injector. Kubeflow pipeline pods annotated for Vault sidecar injection of S3 credentials. Database passwords for PostgreSQL metadata stores rotated every 30 days via Vault dynamic secrets.

data.entities: ml_experiments (MLflow experiment: id, name, institution_namespace, artifact_location_s3, tags_json, lifecycle_stage). model_versions (MLflow model: name, version, run_id, source_s3_uri, stage ENUM None/Staging/Production/Archived, evaluation_gate_passed BOOLEAN). pipeline_runs (Kubeflow: run_id, pipeline_id, institution_namespace, status ENUM Running/Succeeded/Failed, start_time, end_time, node_count). training_datasets (id UUID PK, dataset_name, s3_uri, s3_version_id, schema_hash, row_count, source_institution, deidentification_method, dua_reference, registered_at). evaluation_metrics (run_id FK, metric_name VARCHAR, metric_value FLOAT, step INT, timestamp). data_lineage_records (id UUID PK, pipeline_run_id FK, input_dataset_id FK, output_model_version_id FK, transformation_code_commit VARCHAR, created_at).
data.storage_design: S3 buckets: consortium-training-data-{institution} (versioning enabled, KMS CMK per institution, lifecycle rule archives to Glacier after 5 years), consortium-model-artifacts (MLflow artifact store, versioning enabled, KMS CMK). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ: Kubeflow metadata DB (pipelines, runs, artifacts) and MLflow tracking DB (experiments, runs, metrics). No cache layer; pipeline step caching uses S3 keyed by component hash.
data.retention_and_deletion: Training datasets: per DUA terms, minimum 5 years, lifecycle policy archives to S3 Glacier after active period. Model artifacts: indefinite retention in MLflow registry. Pipeline run logs: 3-year retention in CloudWatch Logs. CloudTrail audit logs: 7-year retention in S3 Glacier with Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode.
data.data_risks: De-identified EHR dataset re-identification risk if model artifacts memorize training examples. Mitigation: differential privacy noise added at training component level (PyTorch Opacus). Cross-institution data leakage risk if S3 bucket policies misconfigured. Mitigation: quarterly IAM access reviews and S3 Access Analyzer alerts.

devops.deployment_topology: Two EKS clusters: dev (us-east-1a, m5.xlarge node pool, single AZ) and research-prod (us-east-1a/1b, mixed p3.2xlarge GPU Spot + m5.xlarge on-demand). Kubeflow installed via kfctl or kustomize manifests version-pinned in Git. MLflow tracking server deployed as EKS Deployment behind internal ALB (VPN-only). HashiCorp Vault HA cluster (3 Vault pods with Raft storage) on research-prod EKS.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipelines: terraform-plan job on MR (requires peer approval for research-prod apply), pipeline-component-build job (Docker build + push to ECR per component, tagged by git SHA), integration-test job (runs Kubeflow pipeline on dev cluster with synthetic dataset). Researcher pipeline submissions via GitLab CI triggered by researcher YAML config merged to feature branch.
devops.environments: dev: single-AZ EKS for pipeline development and component testing, no real EHR data allowed. research-prod: multi-AZ EKS with GPU nodes, KMS-encrypted S3, Vault HA, restricted to VPN access.
devops.rollback_strategy: Terraform state stored in S3 with DynamoDB lock; rollback via git revert + terraform apply. Kubeflow components rolled back by updating Kustomize image tags to prior ECR image SHA. MLflow model stages: Production model demoted to Archived before rollback model promoted.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Kubeflow pipeline metrics (run duration, step failure rate), GPU utilization via DCGM exporter, PostgreSQL metrics via postgres_exporter. Grafana dashboards: GPU Cluster Utilization, Pipeline Success Rate by Institution, Evaluation Gate Pass Rate by Model Version. MLflow experiment tracking for per-run AUC-ROC, F1, demographic_parity_difference, equalized_odds_difference. Alerts: Prometheus AlertManager to PagerDuty on GPU node OOM, pipeline failure rate >20%, Vault seal event.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing HIPAA-compliant ML training infrastructure for a multi-institution mental health research consortium. Key decisions: Kubeflow Pipelines v2 with per-institution namespace RBAC, Vault dynamic S3 credentials for cross-institution data isolation, differential privacy via PyTorch Opacus at training component level to prevent EHR memorization, Spot Instance checkpointing for GPU cost control within $8K budget. The evaluation gate as a standalone FastAPI service enables reuse across pipeline versions.
target_output.title: Mental Health Consortium ML Training Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: A HIPAA-compliant ML training and inference infrastructure built on EKS with Kubeflow Pipelines v2 and MLflow for a multi-institution mental health research consortium. Trains depression-prediction models on de-identified EHR datasets with reproducible pipeline orchestration, per-institution Kubernetes namespace isolation, HashiCorp Vault dynamic credentials, an automated evaluation gate checking AUC-ROC and demographic parity thresholds before model promotion, and GPU Spot Instance checkpointing to stay within the $8K/month compute budget.
target_output.architecture_overview: EKS clusters in private VPC subnets accessible only via consortium VPN handle all ML workloads. Kubeflow Pipelines v2 (Argo Workflows backend) orchestrates four containerized pipeline components: data validation, feature engineering, model training (GPU), and model evaluation. MLflow tracking server stores experiment metrics, hyperparameters, and model artifacts in S3. A FastAPI evaluation gate service enforces AUC-ROC, F1, and fairness metric thresholds before any model version is promoted in MLflow. Per-institution Kubernetes namespaces with NetworkPolicy and Vault-issued dynamic S3 credentials prevent cross-institution data access. CloudTrail logs S3 object-level events for HIPAA audit trail. GitLab CI manages Terraform IaC for cluster provisioning and pipeline component Docker builds. GPU training nodes use p3.2xlarge EC2 Spot Instances with MLflow checkpoint recovery on interruption.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Kubeflow Pipelines v2 SDK, Argo Workflows 3.5. Model Tracking: MLflow 2.x with PostgreSQL 16 backend and S3 artifact store. Training: PyTorch Lightning 2.x with PyTorch Opacus for differential privacy, scikit-learn for feature engineering. Evaluation: FastAPI 0.110 evaluation gate service. Secrets: HashiCorp Vault 1.16 HA with Vault Agent Injector. Auth: Dex OIDC federated to institutional LDAP. Infrastructure: EKS 1.29, Karpenter for node provisioning, AWS KMS, S3 with versioning, RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, DCGM GPU exporter, MLflow. CI/CD: GitLab CI with Terraform, kustomize.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Pipeline Submission: researcher merges pipeline config YAML to Git; GitLab CI builds component images, runs integration test on dev cluster, and on approval submits Kubeflow pipeline run to research-prod namespace. Data Validation Gate: Great Expectations suite checks de-identified dataset schema, column types, PHI field absence patterns, and row count bounds before training proceeds. GPU Training Step: PyTorch Lightning model with Opacus DP-SGD optimizer, epsilon budget tracked per run, checkpoints written to S3 every 10 minutes, MLflow autologging captures loss curves and hyperparameters. Model Evaluation Gate: FastAPI service checks AUC-ROC >= 0.80, F1 >= 0.75, demographic_parity_difference <= 0.05, equalized_odds_difference <= 0.08; rejects run and writes failure tags to MLflow if thresholds not met. Model Registry Promotion: MLflow model transitions from None to Staging to Production via API calls gated by evaluation gate pass and peer review approval in GitLab.
target_output.system_components: EKS research-prod cluster: GPU node pool (p3.2xlarge Spot, 0-10 nodes via Karpenter), CPU node pool (m5.xlarge on-demand, 2-6 nodes). Kubeflow Pipelines control plane (pipeline controller, artifact manager, Argo Workflows server). MLflow tracking server (EKS Deployment, internal ALB). FastAPI evaluation gate service (EKS Deployment, 2 replicas). HashiCorp Vault HA cluster (3 pods, Raft backend). Dex OIDC provider (2 replicas). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (Kubeflow metadata + MLflow tracking DBs). S3 buckets: training data (per institution, KMS CMK), model artifacts (shared, KMS CMK), pipeline step cache (KMS CMK). Prometheus + Grafana stack (kube-prometheus-operator). DCGM GPU metrics exporter DaemonSet.
target_output.workflows: Pipeline Training Run Workflow: Researcher creates pipeline config YAML (dataset_s3_uri, hyperparameters, thresholds, random_seed) and opens GitLab MR. CI builds component Docker images tagged by git SHA, pushes to ECR, runs integration test on dev cluster with 1000-row synthetic dataset. On peer approval and merge, CI submits Kubeflow pipeline run to research-prod using institution namespace credentials. Kubeflow executes: (1) data_validation_component pulls dataset from S3 using Vault-issued dynamic credential, runs Great Expectations suite; (2) feature_engineering_component applies scikit-learn transformers with deterministic seed; (3) model_training_component schedules on GPU node, trains PyTorch Lightning model with Opacus DP-SGD, writes checkpoints to S3 every 10 min, logs metrics to MLflow; (4) model_evaluation_component calls FastAPI /evaluate/promotion endpoint with run_id and thresholds, registers model in MLflow if passed, terminates pipeline with failure tag if rejected. Model Promotion Workflow: MLflow model in Staging triggers GitLab pipeline job notifying model review committee; reviewer inspects evaluation report and bias metrics in Grafana, approves MLflow stage transition to Production via API call authenticated with institutional Vault credential.
target_output.data_model: training_datasets (id UUID PK, dataset_name VARCHAR, s3_uri VARCHAR, s3_version_id VARCHAR, schema_hash SHA256, row_count BIGINT, source_institution VARCHAR, deidentification_method VARCHAR, dua_reference VARCHAR, epsilon_budget_allocated FLOAT, registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ml_experiments (MLflow-managed: experiment_id, name, artifact_location, institution_namespace, tags). model_versions (MLflow-managed: name, version, run_id, source_s3_uri, stage, evaluation_gate_passed BOOLEAN, dp_epsilon_used FLOAT). pipeline_runs (Kubeflow-managed: run_id, pipeline_id, institution_namespace, status, create_time, finish_time). evaluation_metrics (run_id VARCHAR FK, metric_name VARCHAR, metric_value FLOAT, step INT, timestamp BIGINT). data_lineage_records (id UUID PK, pipeline_run_id VARCHAR FK, input_dataset_id UUID FK, output_model_version VARCHAR, transformation_git_commit VARCHAR, kubeflow_pipeline_version VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_events (CloudTrail S3 object-level: event_time, user_identity_arn, action GetObject/PutObject, s3_bucket, s3_key, source_ip). Clinical research entities: patient_cohorts (id UUID PK, cohort_name VARCHAR, diagnosis_icd10_list TEXT[], inclusion_criteria_json JSONB, min_encounter_count INT, patient_count INT), clinical_feature_sets (id UUID PK, dataset_id FK, cohort_id FK, feature_schema_json JSONB — includes vitals, lab_result, diagnosis, and treatment variables, phi_removed BOOLEAN, de_identification_method VARCHAR), model_clinical_evaluations (id UUID PK, model_version_id FK, cohort_id FK, clinical_metric VARCHAR, metric_value NUMERIC, evaluated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Internal-only FastAPI evaluation gate service at http://evaluation-gate.kubeflow.svc.cluster.local: POST /evaluate/promotion body {run_id: str, thresholds: {auc_roc_min: float, f1_min: float, demographic_parity_max: float, equalized_odds_max: float}} returns {pass: bool, metric_results: {auc_roc, f1, demographic_parity_difference, equalized_odds_difference}, bias_report: {subgroup_performance: {}}, rejection_reason: str|null}. POST /evaluate/regression body {candidate_run_id, baseline_run_id} returns {pass: bool, delta_auc_roc, delta_f1}. Kubeflow Pipelines SDK POST /apis/v2beta1/runs for pipeline submission. MLflow REST API PATCH /api/2.0/mlflow/model-versions/transition-stage for Production promotion.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA controls: S3 training data buckets encrypted with per-institution KMS CMK; EKS node volumes encrypted with KMS; TLS 1.3 on all service mesh communication. Per-institution namespace isolation: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy blocks all cross-namespace pod communication; S3 bucket policy restricts GetObject to institution-specific IAM role; Vault AppRole per namespace issues dynamic AWS credentials scoped to institution S3 prefix. Vault audit log captures every credential issuance with pod identity, institution, and dataset accessed. CloudTrail S3 object-level logging for HIPAA breach detection (abnormal GetObject patterns trigger Macie alert). Differential privacy: PyTorch Opacus DP-SGD with epsilon <= 8.0 per run, epsilon budget tracked per dataset in training_datasets table. Pipeline artifact signing via GitLab CI cosign for supply chain integrity. Annual IAM access review and S3 Access Analyzer findings review. DUA compliance: dataset access logs presented to consortium data governance committee quarterly.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI workflows: terraform-validate (every MR), terraform-plan (every MR with research-prod flag), terraform-apply (requires two approvers for research-prod). Pipeline component CI: Docker build per component, Trivy security scan (block on CRITICAL CVE), push to ECR, integration test on dev EKS. Cluster provisioning: EKS via Terraform EKS module, Kubeflow via kustomize manifests in Git, MLflow via Helm chart, Vault via Vault Helm chart. Environments: dev cluster (single AZ, no real data, researchers can experiment freely), research-prod (multi-AZ, KMS-encrypted, VPN-only, GPU Spot fleet). GPU Spot interruption: Karpenter node termination handler triggers Kubeflow pipeline step retry from last S3 checkpoint. Rollback: Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB lock; Kustomize image tag pinning enables instant rollback by reverting git commit and re-running CI.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics via kube-prometheus-operator: pod CPU/memory, Kubeflow pipeline_run_duration_seconds, pipeline_step_failure_total by component and institution. DCGM Exporter metrics: DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL, DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED for GPU utilization and memory per node. MLflow experiment tracking: per-run auc_roc, f1_score, demographic_parity_difference, equalized_odds_difference, dp_epsilon_spent, training_duration_seconds, gpu_hours_billed. Grafana dashboards: GPU Cluster Utilization (Spot vs on-demand breakdown), Pipeline Success Rate by Institution and Model Version, Evaluation Gate Pass/Fail Rate by Threshold, Monthly GPU Compute Cost vs Budget. AlertManager: pipeline_failure_rate >20% per institution (P2), GPU node OOM (P1), Vault seal event (P1), S3 Macie finding (P1).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GPU compute: ~$5,500/month for 10 concurrent p3.2xlarge Spot instances ($3.06/hr * 1800 GPU-hours/month average). Spot pricing saves ~65% vs on-demand. Kubeflow step caching eliminates redundant feature_engineering_component re-runs (estimated 30% job cost reduction). RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.large: ~$180/month. S3 storage for training datasets and artifacts: ~$200/month at 2TB. Total: ~$6,200/month, within $8K budget with ~$1,800 headroom for burst runs. Scale-out: Karpenter adds GPU nodes in <2 minutes; 10-node pool cap enforced via Karpenter NodePool maxNodes constraint.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Infrastructure (weeks 1-4): Terraform EKS cluster provisioning (dev and research-prod), Vault HA installation and institution namespace AppRole configuration, S3 bucket creation with KMS CMK per institution, Kubeflow Pipelines installation via kustomize, MLflow tracking server deployment, PostgreSQL RDS provisioning, GitLab CI terraform and component build pipelines. Phase 2 Pipeline MVP (weeks 5-8): Kubeflow pipeline component containers (data_validation, feature_engineering, model_training with Opacus, model_evaluation), FastAPI evaluation gate service, MLflow autologging integration, Spot Instance checkpoint wrapper, integration test with synthetic dataset on dev cluster, first research run on research-prod with real de-identified dataset from one consortium institution. Phase 3 Hardening (weeks 9-12): Per-institution NetworkPolicy enforcement, CloudTrail S3 object-level logging, Grafana bias metric dashboards, DCGM GPU exporter, quarterly IAM access review automation, pipeline artifact cosign signing, DUA compliance reporting export.
target_output.development_guidelines: Infrastructure-as-code: all EKS, RDS, S3, KMS, Vault resources in Terraform; no manual console changes. Kubeflow pipeline components containerized independently; each component has its own requirements.txt with pinned versions for reproducibility. GitLab CI enforces Trivy image scan on all component builds; CRITICAL CVEs block pipeline. Python: ruff + mypy in CI for evaluation gate FastAPI service. All Kubeflow pipeline runs must set random_seed in config YAML; training components raise ValueError if seed is absent. MLflow run names include git SHA and dataset version_id for traceability. Peer review required for terraform-apply to research-prod and for MLflow model Production promotion.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPU Spot Instance interruption during long training runs wastes compute hours if checkpointing interval is too long. Mitigation: 10-minute S3 checkpoint interval balances S3 write cost against interrupted work. Risk: PyTorch Opacus DP-SGD with epsilon <= 8.0 may degrade model AUC-ROC by 3-8% compared to non-private training. Mitigation: evaluation gate threshold set with DP accuracy degradation factored in; non-private baseline tracked in MLflow for comparison. Risk: Cross-institution data leakage if Vault AppRole token is exfiltrated. Mitigation: short-lived dynamic credentials (TTL 1 hour), immediate revocation on suspicious access pattern. Tradeoff: Kubeflow pipeline step caching uses content-addressed S3 keys which can share cached outputs across institutions for identical preprocessing steps - acceptable since caching is at feature level, not raw EHR level.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Kubeflow Pipelines v2 SDK over Apache Airflow for native Kubernetes integration and artifact lineage tracking. Chose Karpenter over Cluster Autoscaler for faster GPU node provisioning (<2 min vs 3 min). Selected PyTorch Opacus over TensorFlow Privacy for compatibility with PyTorch Lightning. Determined per-institution S3 CMK over shared CMK to enforce cryptographic isolation between institution datasets.

metadata.notes: Strong first-pass: HIPAA-compliant multi-institution ML infrastructure with Kubeflow, differential privacy, per-institution Vault dynamic credentials, and GPU Spot checkpointing within budget.

profile.domain: Clinical Research / Outcome Measurement Data Migration

project_goal.value: Command-line tool to migrate legacy SPSS .sav-formatted PHQ-9, GAD-7, and PCL-5 outcome measurement datasets to standardized CSV and JSON schemas, replacing a decade-old Visual Basic migration script
project_goal.rationale: Existing VB script is unmaintainable and produces inconsistent output schemas across research sites

target_users.value: Clinical researchers and therapists running data migrations on local workstations; distributed via PyPI
target_users.rationale: Tool is run locally by research staff, not web-deployed

project_class.rationale: Standalone Python CLI with Click; no browser UI, no persistent server, no hosted database

capabilities.rationale: CLI data transformation logic and schema mapping; no auth, no frontend, no hosting

complexity_level.rationale: Single-purpose ETL CLI with local file I/O; no network calls in core migration path

risk_level.rationale: Processes de-identified health outcome data; mishandled output could expose participant scores

data_sensitivity.rationale: PHQ-9, GAD-7, and PCL-5 scores are sensitive mental health outcome measures even when de-identified

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs entirely on researcher local workstation; no network calls, no cloud storage

access_model.value: Local execution by authenticated OS user; no application-level authentication required
access_model.rationale: Access controlled by OS filesystem permissions on input files

feature_scope.value: Read SPSS .sav files via pyreadstat, map SPSS variable names to standardized schema fields via configurable YAML mapping file, validate item response ranges for PHQ-9 (0-3 per item), GAD-7 (0-3), PCL-5 (0-4), compute total scores, write CSV and JSON output, generate migration log
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for replacing VB script functionality

mvp_scope.value: PHQ-9 migration with YAML mapping, range validation, CSV and JSON output, dry-run mode, migration log; GAD-7 and PCL-5 support in subsequent release
mvp_scope.rationale: PHQ-9 is most common scale across consortium sites

security_baseline.value: Output files written to caller-specified directory only; tool does not read from or write to any network path; migration log does not include raw item responses, only summary counts
security_baseline.rationale: Sensitive health outcome data must not be transmitted or stored beyond researcher-specified local path

frontend_stack.value: None - CLI tool with stdout/stderr output only
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI project class requires no browser UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click 8 for command parsing, pyreadstat for SPSS file reading, PyYAML for mapping config
backend_stack.rationale: pyreadstat is the standard Python library for SPSS .sav parsing; Click provides mature CLI argument handling

data_platform.value: Local filesystem only: SPSS .sav input, CSV and JSON output, YAML mapping config, migration log text file; no database
data_platform.rationale: CLI tool with no persistent server-side storage

hosting_target.value: PyPI package for pip/uv install; local execution on researcher workstations (Windows, macOS, Linux)
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python package distribution for research tooling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Tool does not retain data; output files are owned by the researcher; migration logs contain only counts and field mapping summaries, not raw participant responses
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: IRB data management requirements prohibit tool-side data retention

future_scope.value: Support for GAD-7 and PCL-5 scales, DASS-21 mapping, batch directory migration mode, schema validation report HTML export
future_scope.rationale: Additional scales needed by consortium sites

constraints.value: Must run on Python 3.9+ for compatibility with older research workstations; no network calls in core migration path; output schema must match published consortium standardization spec v2.1
constraints.rationale: Researcher workstations vary; network-free operation is a hard requirement for air-gapped sites

observability_baseline.value: CLI --verbose flag for debug output to stderr; migration log file written alongside output; exit code 0 on success, 1 on validation errors, 2 on file I/O errors
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard CLI observability via exit codes and log files; no APM tooling for a local tool

execution_preference.value: Single developer, test-driven, releases via GitHub Actions to PyPI on version tag
execution_preference.rationale: Small-scope tool maintained by one engineer

compliance_context.value: De-identified PHQ-9, GAD-7, PCL-5 data handling best practices; institutional IRB data management requirements may apply; HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification standard assumed for input data
compliance_context.rationale: Mental health outcome scores are sensitive health data; tool must not introduce re-identification risk

project.goal: Replace VB migration script with maintainable Python CLI for SPSS outcome data migration
project.target_users: Clinical researchers on local workstations

security.data_handling: Local files only, no network I/O
security.log_policy: Migration log excludes raw item responses

open_questions.batch_mode: Directory-level batch migration deferred to v1.1

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope of PHQ-9 only is appropriate for a first release. The dry-run preview mode is essential for researcher trust - showing a mapping preview before committing the migration reduces risk of silently incorrect output. The migration log is the audit trail for IRB data management requirements.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Click 8 subcommand structure with a top-level migrate command and a --dry-run flag is idiomatic. YAML mapping config should be validated against a schema at startup before any file I/O begins, so invalid configs fail fast with clear error messages rather than producing partial output.
reasoner_reviews.security: The security baseline is appropriate for a local CLI. The critical control is ensuring the migration log never contains raw item response values - only mapping summaries and validation counts. This must be enforced at the logging layer, not left to developer discipline.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Python 3.9+ compatibility means f-strings and walrus operator are fine but match-case is not. pyreadstat wheels are available for Windows, macOS, and Linux on Python 3.9+. CI must test on all three platforms via GitHub Actions matrix.

backend.service_design: Click CLI with two subcommands: 'migrate' (performs migration, writes output) and 'validate-mapping' (checks YAML mapping file against SPSS variable names without writing output). Global options: --verbose (debug logging to stderr), --log-file (path for migration log, defaults to migration_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.log next to output). Entry point: spss-migrate CLI installed by pip.
backend.api_patterns: CLI interface: spss-migrate migrate --input data.sav --mapping phq9_map.yaml --output-dir ./results --format csv,json [--dry-run]. spss-migrate validate-mapping --input data.sav --mapping phq9_map.yaml. Exit codes: 0 success, 1 validation errors (unmapped variables, out-of-range scores), 2 file I/O errors.
backend.failure_handling: MappingValidationError raised if YAML mapping references SPSS variable names absent in .sav file; lists all unmapped variables in error output. RangeValidationError raised per row for out-of-range item responses; dry-run shows all violations without writing output.

security.auth_design: No application-level authentication; OS filesystem permissions control input file access.
security.privacy_controls: MigrationLogger class filters all log write calls through a response_value_redactor that strips numeric item response values from log messages; only field names, counts, and mapping labels are logged. Output CSV and JSON contain transformed scores only.
security.abuse_prevention: Tool validates --output-dir is a local filesystem path and rejects paths beginning with http:// to prevent accidental network writes on misconfigured systems. YAML loaded via yaml.safe_load() to prevent arbitrary Python object deserialization.

data.entities: questionnaire_responses (in-memory pandas DataFrame: participant_id, scale VARCHAR, item_1 through item_N INT, total_score INT, severity_band VARCHAR, missing_item_count INT, validation_flags list). schema_mappings (YAML config: scale_name, spss_variable_map dict, item_range dict, scoring_formula). migration_logs (text file: timestamp, input_file, scale_detected, row_count, mapped_count, unmapped_variables list, range_violations_count, output_files_written list).
data.storage_design: No persistent storage. Input: pyreadstat reads .sav into pandas DataFrame in memory. Output: pandas to_csv() and to_json() to caller-specified directory. Migration log: Python logging FileHandler.
data.migration_notes: VB script produced inconsistent column naming (PHQ_1 vs phq1 vs Q_PHQ_1 across sites). YAML mapping file per research site maps local SPSS variable names to consortium standard field names (phq9_item_1 through phq9_item_9, phq9_total). Site-specific mapping files distributed in a mappings/ directory alongside the package.

devops.deployment_topology: PyPI package distribution. No server deployment. GitHub Releases for each version tag.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix: test job runs pytest on ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest for Python 3.9, 3.11, 3.12. Release job triggered on version tag: build wheel via python -m build, publish to PyPI via twine with TWINE_PASSWORD from GitHub secret.
devops.environments: Local developer: uv venv + uv pip install -e .[dev]. CI: GitHub Actions. Distribution: PyPI.
devops.observability_stack: No APM. CLI --verbose flag enables DEBUG-level logging to stderr. pytest --cov coverage report in CI. Migration log file as runtime audit trail.

target_output.thinking_summary: Simple CLI tool for SPSS outcome data migration. Key design choices: Click 8 with migrate and validate-mapping subcommands, pyreadstat for .sav parsing, YAML mapping files per research site for variable name normalization, pandas DataFrame for in-memory transformation. Weak quality plan - observability and cost sections are appropriately thin for a local CLI; functional sections meet minimums but lack depth of stronger plans.
target_output.title: SPSS Outcome Measure Migration CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python CLI tool distributed via PyPI that migrates legacy SPSS .sav files containing PHQ-9, GAD-7, and PCL-5 mental health outcome scores to standardized CSV and JSON output formats. Replaces an unmaintainable Visual Basic script with a testable, cross-platform Python 3.9+ package using Click 8 for command parsing and pyreadstat for SPSS file reading.
target_output.architecture_overview: Standalone Python package with Click CLI entry point. The migrate subcommand reads an SPSS .sav file via pyreadstat into an in-memory pandas DataFrame, applies variable name mapping from a researcher-supplied YAML config file, validates PHQ-9 item responses against 0-3 range constraints, computes total scores, and writes CSV and JSON output to a caller-specified directory. A validate-mapping subcommand previews the mapping without writing output. No network calls, no database, no server runtime. Distributed as a wheel on PyPI for Windows, macOS, and Linux on Python 3.9+.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 (compatible with 3.9+). Click 8 for subcommand CLI argument parsing. pyreadstat for SPSS .sav file reading into pandas DataFrame. PyYAML for mapping config loading. pandas for in-memory data transformation and CSV/JSON output. pytest plus pytest-cov for unit testing with sample .sav fixtures. python-build plus twine for PyPI packaging. GitHub Actions for CI matrix testing across three OS platforms and PyPI release on version tag.
target_output.functional_feature_map: migrate command: reads .sav, loads YAML mapping, validates all SPSS variable names present, applies column rename, validates item response ranges, computes total_score, maps to severity_band (PHQ-9 bands: minimal 0-4, mild 5-9, moderate 10-14, moderately severe 15-19, severe 20-27), writes output files, writes migration log. --dry-run flag: runs validation and prints mapping preview and violation counts to stdout without writing files. validate-mapping command: loads .sav and YAML mapping, reports unmapped and extra variables.
target_output.system_components: Click application object with migrate and validate-mapping command groups. SpssReader class wrapping pyreadstat.read_sav(). MappingLoader class loading and schema-validating YAML config. ResponseValidator class enforcing per-item range rules per scale. ScoreComputer class computing total_score and severity_band. OutputWriter class writing CSV via pandas to_csv() and JSON via DataFrame.to_json(). MigrationLogger class with response_value_redactor filter preventing raw item values from appearing in log output.
target_output.workflows: SPSS Migration Workflow: researcher runs spss-migrate migrate --input site_phq9_2024.sav --mapping mappings/site_A_phq9.yaml --output-dir ./output --format csv,json. CLI loads YAML mapping and validates all referenced SPSS variable names exist in the .sav file metadata. If mapping validation fails, prints unmapped variable list and exits 1. CLI reads .sav via pyreadstat, renames columns per mapping, validates each item response value against scale range. Rows with range violations are flagged in validation_flags column and counted in log. Total scores computed, severity bands assigned. Output written to ./output/phq9_migrated_YYYYMMDD.csv and .json. Migration log written with processing counts. Dry-Run Preview Workflow: researcher runs the same command with --dry-run flag. CLI performs all validation steps and prints a mapping table showing SPSS variable name to standard field for each column, lists any out-of-range violations with row index and item name, prints row counts. No files written to disk.
target_output.data_model: questionnaire_responses (in-memory DataFrame: participant_id str, scale str, phq9_item_1 through phq9_item_9 int, phq9_total int, severity_band str, missing_item_count int, validation_flags list). schema_mappings (YAML: scale_name str, version str, spss_variable_map dict mapping spss_var to standard_field, item_ranges dict mapping field to min/max int, scoring_formula str). migration_logs (text file: timestamp, input_file path, scale_name, spss_row_count int, output_row_count int, unmapped_variables list, range_violation_count int, output_files list). Clinical outcome entities (post-migration database schema): patient_outcomes (participant_id VARCHAR PK, site_id VARCHAR, enrollment_date DATE, primary_diagnosis_icd10 VARCHAR, treatment_arm VARCHAR, phq9_total INT, gad7_total INT, audit_c_total INT, qids_total INT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), clinical_encounters (id UUID PK, participant_id FK, visit_number INT, encounter_date DATE, assessor_id VARCHAR, encounter_type ENUM baseline/follow_up/exit, notes_encrypted TEXT), adverse_events (id UUID PK, participant_id FK, encounter_id FK, ae_description TEXT, severity ENUM mild/moderate/severe, physician_notified BOOLEAN, reported_at DATE).
target_output.api_design: CLI commands: spss-migrate migrate --input PATH --mapping PATH --output-dir PATH [--format csv|json|both] [--dry-run] [--verbose] [--log-file PATH]. spss-migrate validate-mapping --input PATH --mapping PATH [--verbose]. Exit codes: 0 success with all rows valid or with range violations flagged in output (non-blocking), 1 mapping validation failure (unmapped variables blocking migration), 2 file I/O error. Stdout: progress messages and dry-run tabular report. Stderr: DEBUG logging when --verbose is set.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Output directory validation: tool checks that --output-dir resolves to a local path and rejects URL-like paths starting with http:// to prevent accidental remote writes. MigrationLogger filters prevent raw item response values from appearing in log file; only summary counts and field names logged. YAML mapping config loaded with yaml.safe_load() to prevent arbitrary Python object deserialization. Migration log serves as IRB audit trail showing which files were processed and which mapping was applied.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: matrix of ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest across Python 3.9, 3.11, 3.12; runs pytest --cov with 80% coverage gate on each combination. Release workflow triggered on git tag vX.Y.Z: builds wheel via python -m build, publishes to PyPI via twine using TWINE_PASSWORD GitHub secret. Local developer setup: uv venv and uv pip install -e .[dev]. No server infrastructure, no Docker, no cloud hosting required.
target_output.observability: CLI exit codes are the primary observability mechanism: 0 success, 1 validation failure, 2 I/O error. --verbose flag enables Python logging DEBUG level to stderr for troubleshooting individual rows. Migration log file written per run with processing counts. pytest --cov coverage report in GitHub Actions CI summary.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No hosting cost; distributed as a free open-source PyPI package. Processes a 10,000-row .sav file in under 5 seconds on a standard research workstation via pandas vectorized column operations.
target_output.phased_implementation: v1.0 (6 weeks): PHQ-9 migrate and validate-mapping commands, YAML mapping config loader with schema validation, item range validation, CSV and JSON output, dry-run preview mode, migration log with IRB-compliant field filtering, GitHub Actions CI matrix, PyPI release. v1.1 (4 weeks): GAD-7 and PCL-5 scale support with scale-specific item range rules and severity bands. v1.2 (future): DASS-21 support, batch directory migration mode processing all .sav files in a folder, HTML validation report export.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.9+ compatible syntax only; no match-case statements. Type hints on all public functions. pytest fixtures provide sample .sav test files in tests/fixtures/ generated from pyreadstat write_sav(). Unit tests cover: valid PHQ-9 migration output schema matches consortium spec v2.1, range validation flag on out-of-range item value, MappingValidationError on missing SPSS variable, dry-run produces zero output files. 80% coverage gate enforced in CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: pyreadstat may not parse .sav files saved by SPSS versions older than v16. Mitigation: document supported SPSS version range in package README; test fixtures include files from SPSS v16, v20, and v25. Risk: researcher supplies incorrect YAML mapping that silently misaligns item fields. Mitigation: validate-mapping subcommand and --dry-run mode provide a mandatory review step before committing the migration. Both controls reduce silent data corruption risk.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected pyreadstat over savReaderWriter for active maintenance and native pandas DataFrame integration. Chose per-site YAML mapping config over hardcoded variable name mappings to support the naming variation across consortium research sites. Decided migration log filters out raw item values by default to comply with IRB data minimization guidance.

metadata.notes: Weak quality: observability and cost sections are appropriately thin for a local CLI tool; plan meets minimums but lacks depth of stronger plans.

profile.domain: Mental Health / Clinical Outcome Measurement SDK

project_goal.value: Python SDK for computing and interpreting standardized mental health outcome measures (PHQ-9, GAD-7, AUDIT, PCL-5, DASS-21), providing fast batch scoring, clinical cutoff interpretation, and normative comparison utilities for researchers and EHR integrators
project_goal.rationale: Researchers and EHR vendors need a reliable, well-tested Python library for programmatic scoring of clinical instruments without re-implementing scoring algorithms per project

target_users.value: Python-using clinical researchers, data scientists, and EHR integration engineers who import the SDK as a dependency
target_users.rationale: Library callers, not end-patients; SDK is a developer tool

project_class.rationale: Package architecture with public API surface, not a hosted platform; distributed via PyPI

capabilities.rationale: Pure computation and data transformation library; no auth, no hosting, no frontend

complexity_level.rationale: Multiple clinical scales with distinct scoring algorithms, missing data handling strategies, normative table lookups, and NumPy-vectorized batch processing

risk_level.rationale: SDK is a computation library; it does not store or transmit data; incorrect scores could mislead clinical interpretation but the library itself has no attack surface

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK operates on caller-provided data in memory and does not persist any participant data

external_exposure.rationale: Published as an open-source PyPI package; documentation hosted publicly

access_model.value: Public open-source package; no authentication; callers import the SDK as a Python dependency
access_model.rationale: Library with no runtime auth requirement

feature_scope.value: Single-item and batch scoring for PHQ-9, GAD-7, AUDIT, PCL-5, DASS-21; clinical cutoff interpretation with severity band labels; missing data handling (mean imputation and prorated scoring); normative comparison against published population tables; pandas DataFrame batch mode with NumPy vectorization; typed result objects with score, severity_band, missing_count, and normative_percentile
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for v1 covering most common research and EHR use cases

mvp_scope.value: PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scoring with severity bands, basic missing data handling, and pandas batch mode; AUDIT, PCL-5, DASS-21, normative tables, and percentile lookup in v1.1
mvp_scope.rationale: PHQ-9 and GAD-7 are the most widely deployed scales; fast delivery of these scales unblocks the most urgent EHR integrator use cases

security_baseline.value: No network calls; no file I/O; no logging of caller data; all operations on caller-supplied in-memory objects only
security_baseline.rationale: Library with zero network and zero persistence surface

frontend_stack.value: None - pure Python library; documentation site via MkDocs with mkdocs-material theme hosted on GitHub Pages
frontend_stack.rationale: Library SDK with no application UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 (library package compatible with 3.9+); NumPy for vectorized batch scoring; pandas optional dependency for DataFrame batch mode
backend_stack.rationale: NumPy vectorization is the performance optimization approach for tens-of-thousands row datasets; pandas optional to avoid mandatory heavy dependency

data_platform.value: No persistent storage; normative comparison tables bundled as JSON files in the package data directory; all computation in-memory
data_platform.rationale: Library SDK cannot use a database; normative tables must be shipped with the package

hosting_target.value: PyPI for package distribution; GitHub Pages for documentation; no server infrastructure
hosting_target.rationale: Standard open-source Python library distribution

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK retains no caller data; all scoring computations are stateless; no telemetry or usage logging
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Library must not introduce data retention risk for callers handling sensitive participant data

future_scope.value: Composite scoring (DASS-21 Depression/Anxiety/Stress subscales), longitudinal change score calculation (reliable change index), R package port, REST microservice wrapper for non-Python EHR systems

constraints.value: Core scoring functions must have zero mandatory dependencies beyond the Python standard library; NumPy and pandas are optional extras; library must be pip-installable on Python 3.9+; all scoring algorithms must cite published scoring manual references in docstrings
constraints.rationale: EHR integrators have strict dependency policies; optional extras prevent bloated installs for users who only need single-item scoring

observability_baseline.value: No runtime observability; library emits no logs; ValueError and TypeError exceptions with descriptive messages are the error interface; public changelog and semantic versioning for API stability tracking
observability_baseline.rationale: Library SDK with no runtime infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Open-source development on GitHub; semantic versioning; automated PyPI release on git tag; documentation auto-deployed to GitHub Pages on merge to main
execution_preference.rationale: Standard open-source library governance

project.goal: Publish a well-tested Python SDK for clinical outcome measure scoring
project.target_users: Researchers and EHR integration engineers

reasoner_reviews.product: The zero-mandatory-dependency constraint is the right call for EHR integrators who run strict dependency audits. Separating core scoring (stdlib only) from batch mode (NumPy + pandas extra) lets the library serve both minimal and high-throughput use cases. Shipping normative tables as bundled JSON files keeps the library self-contained.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The API surface design is the most important architecture decision for a library SDK. ScoreResult should be a frozen dataclass (or named tuple) rather than a dict to give callers type-safe attribute access and IDE autocompletion. The scale registry pattern (a dict mapping scale name to ScaleDefinition) enables adding new scales without modifying core scoring logic.
reasoner_reviews.security: The library has effectively zero security attack surface since it performs pure in-memory computation with no I/O. The main risk is algorithm correctness: an incorrect severity band definition could cause a researcher to misinterpret a score. All severity cutoff values must be unit-tested against the published scoring manuals with exact expected values.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: NumPy vectorization on a 50,000-row DataFrame should process in under 1 second. The optional dependency model requires testing both the no-NumPy path (scalar scoring) and the NumPy path in CI. Optional extras declared in pyproject.toml as [numpy] and [pandas] extras.

backend.service_design: Package structure: outcomes_sdk/ __init__.py (public API exports), scales/ (one module per scale: phq9.py, gad7.py, audit.py, pcl5.py, dass21.py), models.py (ScoreResult frozen dataclass, SeverityBand enum, MissingDataStrategy enum), registry.py (ScaleRegistry mapping scale_name to ScaleDefinition), batch.py (NumPy/pandas batch scoring with optional import guards), normative.py (NormativeTable loader from bundled JSON). Public API: score(scale, responses, missing_strategy) -> ScoreResult and batch_score(scale, dataframe, item_columns, missing_strategy) -> DataFrame with appended result columns.
backend.api_patterns: score('phq9', {'phq9_item_1': 2, 'phq9_item_2': 1, ..., 'phq9_item_9': 0}) returns ScoreResult(scale='phq9', total=21, severity_band=SeverityBand.SEVERE, missing_count=0, subscores=None). batch_score('phq9', df, item_columns=['q1','q2',...,'q9']) returns df with appended columns: phq9_total, phq9_severity_band, phq9_missing_count. All public functions have full type annotations. TypeError raised for non-dict input. ValueError raised for unrecognized scale name or response values outside valid range when validate=True.
backend.scaling_notes: NumPy vectorized batch_score processes item response columns as a 2D ndarray; total scores computed via np.nansum or np.sum depending on missing_strategy; severity band assignment via np.digitize with cutoff arrays. Benchmarked at 50,000 rows in under 0.4 seconds on a standard laptop. Pure Python scalar path available for callers without NumPy installed.

security.auth_design: No authentication; public library.
security.privacy_controls: Library performs no logging, no telemetry, no file writes. ScoreResult and all intermediate objects are ephemeral in caller memory. Normative tables loaded from package data at first access and cached in module-level dict (read-only after load).
security.security_risks: Algorithm correctness is the primary risk: wrong cutoff values or incorrect subscale aggregation could produce clinically misleading severity bands. Mitigation: unit tests assert exact ScoreResult values against published scoring manual test cases for every scale and every severity band boundary.

data.entities: questionnaire_scales (ScaleDefinition: scale_name str, version str, item_count int, item_range ItemRange, subscales list[SubscaleDefinition], scoring_formula ScoringFormula, severity_cutoffs list[SeverityCutoff], citation str). item_responses (caller-supplied dict or DataFrame column: item_key -> int). composite_scores (ScoreResult: scale str, total int, severity_band SeverityBand, missing_count int, subscores dict[str,int] or None, normative_percentile float or None). severity_bands (SeverityBand enum: MINIMAL/MILD/MODERATE/MODERATELY_SEVERE/SEVERE for PHQ-9; MINIMAL/MILD/MODERATE/SEVERE for GAD-7; LOW_RISK/HAZARDOUS/HARMFUL/POSSIBLE_DEPENDENCE for AUDIT). normative_tables (bundled JSON: {scale_name: {population: str, n: int, mean: float, sd: float, percentile_table: [[raw_score, percentile], ...]}})
data.storage_design: No persistent storage. Normative table JSON files stored in outcomes_sdk/data/ directory, included in package via package_data in pyproject.toml. Loaded lazily on first normative_percentile call and cached in module-level NormativeTable registry dict.
data.analytics_events: No telemetry. Package changelog tracks API changes per semantic version. Unit test coverage report generated per CI run.

devops.deployment_topology: PyPI package distribution. Documentation via MkDocs with mkdocs-material on GitHub Pages. No server infrastructure.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: test job matrix (ubuntu, windows, macos) x (Python 3.9, 3.11, 3.12) x (core-only install, full extras install). pytest --cov with 95% coverage gate. mypy --strict type checking. ruff linting. Release job on vX.Y.Z tag: build wheel + sdist, publish to PyPI via trusted publisher (OIDC, no API key), deploy docs to GitHub Pages.
devops.environments: Developer: uv venv, uv pip install -e .[dev,numpy,pandas]. CI: GitHub Actions. Distribution: PyPI. Docs: GitHub Pages.
devops.rollback_strategy: Yank PyPI release via twine if critical scoring bug discovered post-release. Follow up with patch release within 24 hours. Changelog entry required for every yanked version explaining the scoring error.

target_output.thinking_summary: Python SDK for clinical outcome measure scoring. Key architecture decisions: zero-mandatory-dependency core scoring with NumPy/pandas as optional extras, frozen dataclass ScoreResult for type-safe caller API, scale registry pattern for extensibility without core changes, normative tables as bundled JSON loaded lazily. Moderate quality: functional sections are strong but the deployment_and_operations section is thinner than ideal given there is no server infrastructure to describe.
target_output.title: Mental Health Outcome Measures Python SDK
target_output.executive_summary: A Python library SDK distributed via PyPI that provides scoring, clinical cutoff interpretation, and normative comparison for PHQ-9, GAD-7, AUDIT, PCL-5, and DASS-21 mental health outcome measures. Core scoring has zero mandatory dependencies; NumPy and pandas are optional extras for vectorized batch processing of large research datasets. Typed ScoreResult objects include total score, SeverityBand enum, missing item count, and optional normative percentile.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure Python package with a public API exposing two primary functions: score() for single-participant scoring and batch_score() for DataFrame-level batch processing. A ScaleRegistry maps scale name strings to ScaleDefinition objects encoding item counts, valid response ranges, scoring formulas, severity cutoff arrays, and citation references. Core scoring logic has no external dependencies beyond the Python standard library, enabling installation in dependency-restricted EHR environments. NumPy vectorization is activated when the numpy extra is installed, enabling batch_score() to process a 50,000-row DataFrame in under 0.4 seconds via np.digitize cutoff assignment and np.nansum aggregation. Normative comparison tables are bundled as JSON files in the package data directory, loaded lazily on first use and cached module-level for the process lifetime.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 (compatible with 3.9+). Core scoring: Python standard library only (dataclasses, enum, typing). Batch processing: NumPy 1.26+ and pandas 2.x as optional extras declared in pyproject.toml [numpy] and [pandas] extra groups. Documentation: MkDocs 1.5 with mkdocs-material theme, API reference via mkdocstrings. Testing: pytest 8, pytest-cov (95% gate), mypy --strict, ruff linter. Packaging: Hatchling build backend, twine for PyPI trusted publisher release. CI/CD: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: PHQ-9 Scorer: 9-item depression scale, item range 0-3, total 0-27, severity bands minimal/mild/moderate/moderately_severe/severe per Kroenke et al. 2001. GAD-7 Scorer: 7-item anxiety scale, item range 0-3, total 0-21, bands minimal/mild/moderate/severe per Spitzer et al. 2006. AUDIT Scorer: 10-item alcohol use scale, item ranges vary per item (0-4), bands low_risk/hazardous/harmful/possible_dependence per Babor et al. 2001. PCL-5 Scorer: 20-item PTSD scale, item range 0-4, total 0-80, provisional PTSD cutoff 33 per Weathers et al. 2013. DASS-21 Scorer: 21-item scale with Depression/Anxiety/Stress subscales, item range 0-3, double-multiplied subscale totals per Lovibond and Lovibond 1995. Missing Data Handling: SKIP_MISSING (exclude item from total), MEAN_IMPUTE (replace with scale mean), PRORATED_TOTAL (scale total by n_valid / n_items). Normative Comparison: percentile lookup against bundled population normative tables.
target_output.system_components: ScaleRegistry: module-level dict mapping scale_name to ScaleDefinition, populated at import time from scale module registrations. ScaleDefinition: dataclass with scale_name, version, item_count, item_keys list, ItemRange, subscales list, ScoringFormula, severity_cutoffs list of SeverityCutoff(threshold, band), citation str. ScoreResult: frozen dataclass with scale, total, severity_band, missing_count, subscores dict or None, normative_percentile float or None. MissingDataStrategy: Enum with SKIP, MEAN_IMPUTE, PRORATED_TOTAL values. NormativeTable: class loading JSON from package data directory via importlib.resources, caching loaded tables in class-level dict. BatchScorer: wraps NumPy vectorized scoring when numpy is available, falls back to scalar loop otherwise.
