target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (6 weeks): Three REDCap project connectors (PHQ-9, GAD-7, PCL-5 instruments), FHIR Observation transformation with LOINC code mapping, JSON schema validation gate, Snowflake COPY INTO with MERGE deduplication, data quality checks for out-of-range scores and missing values, PostgreSQL pipeline_runs and phi_access_log, SES quality report email, Terraform MWAA and RDS infrastructure. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Additional REDCap project connectors for multi-site expansion, Snowflake ANALYST de-identified views with Dynamic Data Masking, IRB compliance reporting queries, Vault dynamic secret integration replacing static Airflow connections. Phase 3 (4 weeks): OMOP CDM mapping alongside FHIR for compatibility with clinical data network, automated IRB compliance report generation, longitudinal follow-up record linkage across REDCap timepoints.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with type hints on all Airflow operator functions. pytest with 90% coverage gate on FHIR transformation and data quality check logic. DAG code: all REDCap field mappings in versioned JSON files under config/field_mappings/{project_id}.json - no hardcoded field names in operator code. Snowflake SQL in .sql template files under sql/ directory - no inline SQL strings in Python. All Vault secret lookups use context manager pattern ensuring lease revocation on task completion. PR requires data governance team review for any changes to field_mapping files.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: REDCap field renaming by project administrators breaks FHIR transformation silently. Mitigation: transformation task validates all mapped fields exist in response schema and raises a high-severity quality check on unknown field names. Risk: Snowflake X-Small warehouse exceeds $500/month credit budget during multi-study expansion. Mitigation: separate Snowflake warehouses per study with credit monitors and auto-suspend; analyst query warehouse billed separately from pipeline warehouse. Risk: PHI in dead-letter S3 bucket if records fail FHIR transformation after retries. Mitigation: dead-letter bucket has 7-day lifecycle policy; governance team alerted on any dead-letter writes. Tradeoff: incremental export over full refresh - chosen for REDCap rate limit compliance, but requires accurate last_run timestamp tracking to prevent gaps.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Snowflake COPY INTO over row-by-row insertion to stay within $500/month credit budget. Confirmed participant ID SHA-256 hashing as de-identification method per IRB Safe Harbor requirements. Chosen MWAA over self-managed Airflow to eliminate cluster maintenance overhead. Vault dynamic secrets chosen over AWS Secrets Manager for per-DAG-task credential isolation with TTL enforcement.

metadata.notes: Strong first-pass for HIPAA research data pipeline: REDCap REST API to FHIR Observation Snowflake ingestion with data quality gates and IRB audit trail.

profile.domain: AI Therapy Session Summarization for Group Practices

project_goal.value: AI-powered therapy session summarization tool for group practices using speech-to-text and LLM-based summarization to generate structured SOAP clinical notes from session audio recordings, containerized and deployed via GitOps pipeline on AKS
project_goal.rationale: Core productivity tool enabling therapists to spend less time on documentation and more time on patient care

target_users.value: Licensed therapists at group practices uploading session audio and reviewing AI-generated SOAP note drafts before approval
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in discovery sessions with three group practice partners

project_class.rationale: Core value is speech-to-text transcription and LLM SOAP note generation; compute-intensive async processing pipeline

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, batch_jobs, devops
capabilities.rationale: React UI for therapist note review, FastAPI backend, async transcription/summarization jobs, Azure AD auth, LLM integration, GitOps devops

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-step async AI pipeline (upload, transcribe, summarize, review), PHI data handling, AKS GitOps deployment

risk_level.rationale: Session audio recordings and clinical notes are highly sensitive PHI; HIPAA requires BAA with Azure; patient consent required for AI processing

data_sensitivity.rationale: Audio recordings and SOAP notes contain PHI including session content, diagnoses, and treatment plans

external_exposure.rationale: Therapist portal requires Azure AD SSO; no public unauthenticated access

access_model.value: Azure AD SSO with Entra ID; RBAC roles: therapist (upload recordings, review and approve notes), practice_admin (manage therapist accounts, view audit log), billing_admin (export approved notes to EHR)
access_model.rationale: Role separation between clinical and administrative functions

feature_scope.value: Session recording upload, Azure Speech Services transcription, GPT-4o SOAP note generation, therapist review and edit interface, note approval workflow, HL7 FHIR note export to EHR, audit trail for all AI-generated content
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for practice-ready clinical documentation tool

mvp_scope.value: Audio upload and storage, transcription via Azure Speech Services, GPT-4o SOAP note draft, therapist review and approval UI, PostgreSQL note storage
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for pilot with three group practices

security_baseline.value: Azure Blob Storage with customer-managed keys for audio recordings; PostgreSQL column encryption for transcript content; Azure AD MFA for all therapist accounts; append-only ai_generation_audit log for every AI model invocation on PHI
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA controls for session audio and clinical notes

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript for therapist note review and approval interface
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; React Query for async job status polling

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for REST API; Celery for async transcription and summarization jobs
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for async endpoints; Celery for long-running speech-to-text and LLM tasks decoupled from HTTP request lifecycle

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for clinical notes, transcripts, and audit log; Azure Blob Storage for session audio files
data_platform.rationale: Relational integrity for note approval workflow; Blob Storage for large audio file storage with CMK encryption

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) with ArgoCD GitOps deployment
hosting_target.rationale: Kubernetes for workload isolation between web API and Celery workers; ArgoCD for declarative GitOps pipeline aligned with devops modernization theme

privacy_retention_policy.value: Session audio recordings: deleted 30 days after note approval; transcripts: retained with notes per practice retention policy (minimum 7 years); note_reviews and ai_generation_audit: retained 7 years; patient consent records retained indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA minimum retention and audio data minimization after note generation

future_scope.value: Real-time transcription during live sessions, multi-language support (Spanish), group therapy session multi-speaker diarization, direct EHR write integration via Epic FHIR sandbox

constraints.value: Azure region us-east (East US 2) for data residency; audio file size limit 500MB per session; GPT-4o token limit requires transcript chunking strategy for sessions over 90 minutes; BAA required with Azure before production
constraints.rationale: Data residency, file size limits, LLM context window constraints, and compliance

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor with Grafana dashboards for AKS workload metrics; structured logging from all pods to Log Analytics Workspace; alert on Celery job failure rate and transcription error rate
observability_baseline.rationale: Azure-native monitoring for AKS deployments

execution_preference.value: GitOps via ArgoCD: all AKS workloads declaratively defined in Helm charts versioned in Git; ArgoCD syncs from main branch on merge
execution_preference.rationale: DevOps modernization goal requiring auditable infrastructure-as-code deployment pipeline

compliance_context.value: HIPAA for session audio and clinical notes; BAA required with Azure; patient written consent required before audio recording and AI processing; AI-generated content must be clearly labeled as AI-drafted in the clinical record
compliance_context.rationale: HIPAA and emerging AI-in-healthcare disclosure requirements

llm_integration.value: Azure Speech Services for speech-to-text transcription; GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI Service for SOAP note generation from transcript; system prompt instructs SOAP format with Subjective/Objective/Assessment/Plan sections and explicit AI-draft watermark
llm_integration.rationale: Azure OpenAI Service keeps PHI within Azure compliance boundary for BAA coverage

project.goal: Automated SOAP note generation from therapy session audio with therapist review and approval workflow
project.target_users: Licensed therapists at group practices

frontend.state: React Query for job status polling, Zustand for note editor state

backend.api: REST API with OpenAPI 3.0; FHIR R4 export endpoint

security.auth: Azure AD Entra ID SSO with MFA
security.encryption: Azure Blob CMK for audio; PostgreSQL column encryption for transcripts
security.audit: ai_generation_audit log for all LLM invocations on PHI

data.storage: Azure Blob Storage for audio files

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for test and container build
devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: The SOAP note generation workflow correctly places the therapist in the approval seat - AI drafts must require explicit therapist review and approval before entering the clinical record. The 'AI-draft' watermark label on generated notes is a compliance requirement and should be surfaced prominently in the review UI, not buried in metadata.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Celery workers for async transcription and summarization are the right pattern here - Azure Speech Services transcription for a 60-minute session can take 2-5 minutes, far beyond HTTP timeout constraints. The React frontend should poll job status via React Query with exponential backoff rather than holding a websocket connection. The transcript chunking strategy for sessions over 90 minutes needs explicit specification.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure OpenAI Service is the correct choice over direct OpenAI API because it keeps PHI within Azure's HIPAA BAA boundary. The ai_generation_audit log must capture model version, prompt template version, input token count, and output token count for each invocation - this is required for HIPAA accountability rule compliance.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GPT-4o has a 128K token context window. A 90-minute therapy session transcript is approximately 15,000-20,000 tokens. Sessions under 90 minutes fit in a single GPT-4o call. For longer sessions, a sliding window chunking strategy with section-overlap is needed. The plan should specify the chunking threshold and overlap strategy.

frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA with route-based code splitting: /dashboard (recent sessions), /upload (new recording), /notes/{session_id} (review and edit), /admin (practice_admin only).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Upload Flow: drag-and-drop audio file upload with progress bar, patient consent checkbox validation, session metadata form (client pseudonym, session date, session type). Note Review: side-by-side transcript view and SOAP note editor with AI-draft badge, section-level editing, approve and request-revision actions. Dashboard: session list with processing status (uploading/transcribing/summarizing/pending_review/approved), filter by date and therapist.
frontend.state_management: React Query for session status polling (polling interval 5s while status is transcribing or summarizing, stops on terminal status). Zustand for note editor unsaved changes state and draft edit tracking.
frontend.frontend_risks: Audio file upload over 500MB must be blocked client-side before upload initiation to prevent failed uploads after partial transfer.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with routers: /sessions (upload, status, list), /notes (SOAP note CRUD, approval), /export (FHIR export). Celery app with two queues: transcription_queue (Azure Speech Services jobs) and summarization_queue (Azure OpenAI GPT-4o jobs). Redis as Celery broker.
backend.api_patterns: REST with Azure AD JWT validation. Multipart upload endpoint streams audio directly to Azure Blob Storage without buffering in API pod. Celery task chain: transcribe_session_task -> chunk_transcript_task -> summarize_soap_task -> notify_therapist_task.
backend.llm_integration_design: summarize_soap_task calls Azure OpenAI Service GPT-4o (azure.openai.com endpoint within Azure tenant). System prompt: SOAP clinical note format with explicit AI-DRAFT watermark header. For transcripts under 18000 tokens: single GPT-4o call. For longer transcripts: sliding window chunking with 500-token overlap, merge SOAP sections across chunks. ai_generation_audit row written per GPT-4o call: session_id, model_version, prompt_version, input_tokens, output_tokens, generated_at.
backend.background_jobs: Celery task chain triggered on successful audio upload to Blob Storage. transcribe_session_task: calls Azure Speech Services batch transcription API, polls for completion, writes transcript_text to transcripts table. summarize_soap_task: reads transcript, applies chunking if needed, calls GPT-4o, writes SOAP draft to clinical_notes with status=pending_review.

security.auth_design: Azure AD Entra ID SSO. FastAPI validates Azure AD JWT (access token) on every request. MFA enforced via Conditional Access Policy for all therapist and admin accounts. Session tokens 1-hour lifetime with refresh.
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced in FastAPI: therapist accesses only their own sessions and notes; practice_admin accesses all sessions within their practice; billing_admin accesses approved notes only for EHR export.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault for PostgreSQL credentials, Redis connection string, Azure OpenAI API key, and Speech Services API key. Kubernetes secrets injected via Azure Key Vault Provider for Secrets Store CSI Driver.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: ai_generation_audit: session_id, therapist_id, model_version, prompt_version, input_tokens, output_tokens, generated_at. note_reviews: session_id, therapist_id, action (approved/revision_requested), edited_sections[], decided_at. All AKS pod logs forwarded to Azure Log Analytics Workspace with PHI field masking.

data.entities: session_recordings (id UUID PK, therapist_id, client_pseudonym, session_date, audio_blob_url, file_size_bytes, consent_recorded BOOL, status ENUM, created_at), transcripts (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, transcript_text_encrypted TEXT, word_count INT, duration_seconds INT, transcribed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), clinical_notes (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, soap_draft TEXT, therapist_edits_json JSONB, status ENUM(pending_review,approved,archived), approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, approved_by UUID), note_reviews (id UUID PK, note_id UUID FK, therapist_id UUID, action ENUM, edited_sections TEXT[], decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ), ai_generation_audit (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, task_type VARCHAR, model_version VARCHAR, prompt_version VARCHAR, input_tokens INT, output_tokens INT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ)
data.storage_design: Azure Blob Storage container 'session-audio' with customer-managed keys (Azure Key Vault CMK) and soft delete enabled. PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with column encryption on transcripts.transcript_text and session_recordings client information.
data.retention_and_deletion: Blob Storage lifecycle: delete audio files 30 days after clinical_notes.status=approved. PostgreSQL: archive clinical_notes and transcripts to cold storage after 7 years per practice retention policy.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster with two node pools: system (Standard_D2s_v3, 2-3 nodes for API and Ingress) and worker (Standard_D4s_v3, 2-5 nodes for Celery transcription and summarization workers, auto-scales on Celery queue depth via KEDA). ArgoCD deployed in-cluster watching Git repo main branch.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest 85% coverage, mypy type checking, Docker build and push to Azure Container Registry, update Helm chart image tags in Git (triggers ArgoCD sync). ArgoCD syncs Helm charts from Git to AKS; sync requires manual approval for production environment.
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor with Grafana dashboards: AKS node CPU/memory, Celery queue depths (transcription_queue, summarization_queue), transcription job latency P50/P95, GPT-4o token usage rate. Structured JSON logs from all pods to Log Analytics Workspace. Azure Monitor alert: Celery job failure rate > 5%, transcription error > 3%.

target_output.thinking_summary: AI therapy summarization system on Azure AKS with GitOps. Key design decisions: Celery task chain (transcribe->chunk->summarize->notify) for long-running Azure Speech and GPT-4o jobs outside HTTP timeout; Azure OpenAI Service over direct OpenAI API to keep PHI within Azure BAA boundary; KEDA-based AKS worker node auto-scaling on Celery queue depth; ArgoCD GitOps with manual production approval gate. The plan is moderate quality - the transcript chunking strategy is specified but the EHR FHIR export section is slightly thin.
target_output.title: Therapy Session AI Summarization Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A HIPAA-compliant AI system on Azure AKS enabling licensed therapists to upload session audio recordings and receive structured SOAP clinical note drafts generated by Azure Speech Services transcription followed by GPT-4o summarization via Azure OpenAI Service. A React 18 therapist portal supports inline note editing, approval, and HL7 FHIR export to practice EHR systems. All PHI stays within Azure's HIPAA BAA boundary. Deployment is fully GitOps-managed via ArgoCD on AKS.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA communicates over HTTPS to a FastAPI backend running in an AKS system node pool. Audio uploads stream directly to Azure Blob Storage with customer-managed keys. A successful upload triggers a Celery task chain on Redis broker: transcribe_session_task calls Azure Speech Services batch API, chunk_transcript_task splits transcripts over 18K tokens with 500-token overlap, and summarize_soap_task calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o to produce a SOAP note draft written to PostgreSQL with status=pending_review. KEDA scales the AKS worker node pool (Standard_D4s_v3) on Celery queue depth. ArgoCD watches the Git repo main branch and syncs Helm charts to AKS, with manual approval required for production. Azure Key Vault provides secrets via CSI Driver. All pod logs forward to Azure Log Analytics. An ai_generation_audit table records every GPT-4o invocation on PHI.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Celery 5.3, Redis 7 as broker. AI/ML: Azure Speech Services batch transcription, Azure OpenAI Service GPT-4o (gpt-4o deployment in Azure East US 2). Database: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server 16. Storage: Azure Blob Storage with CMK. Auth: Azure AD Entra ID SSO. Infrastructure: AKS with KEDA, ArgoCD, Helm, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault CSI Driver. Monitoring: Azure Monitor, Grafana, Log Analytics Workspace.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Recording Upload: therapist uploads audio file (max 500MB) with patient consent checkbox and session metadata; React validates file size before upload; FastAPI streams to Azure Blob Storage and triggers Celery chain. Transcription Job: Azure Speech Services batch transcription with speaker diarization; transcript stored in PostgreSQL with word_count and duration_seconds. Transcript Chunking: sessions over 18K tokens split with 500-token overlapping sliding windows to stay within GPT-4o context limit. SOAP Generation: GPT-4o with versioned system prompt produces Subjective/Objective/Assessment/Plan note with 'AI-DRAFT' header watermark; ai_generation_audit row written per invocation. Therapist Review: side-by-side transcript and SOAP editor; therapist edits tracked per section in therapist_edits_json; approve or request-revision actions. FHIR Export: approved notes exported as FHIR R4 DocumentReference resources via GET /export/fhir/{note_id}.
target_output.system_components: AKS cluster: system node pool (FastAPI API pods, Ingress, ArgoCD), worker node pool (Celery workers scaled by KEDA on queue depth). Azure Application Gateway Ingress Controller with WAF. Azure Blob Storage container 'session-audio' with CMK and soft delete. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Redis 7 (Azure Cache for Redis) as Celery broker. Azure Speech Services batch transcription API. Azure OpenAI Service GPT-4o endpoint. Azure Key Vault with CSI Driver for pod secret injection. Azure Container Registry for Docker images. ArgoCD in-cluster. Azure Monitor and Grafana.
target_output.workflows: Session Upload and Transcription: Therapist drags audio file onto upload page; React validates file size under 500MB and consent checkbox is checked; POST /sessions/upload streams multipart to FastAPI; FastAPI writes session_recordings record with status=uploading, uploads to Azure Blob via azure-storage-blob SDK, updates status=transcribing, enqueues transcribe_session_task to transcription_queue. Celery worker calls Azure Speech Services POST /speechtotext/v3.2/transcriptions with audio blob SAS URL, polls completion every 30s, writes transcript_text_encrypted to transcripts table, chains to chunk_transcript_task. chunk_transcript_task checks word_count: if under 18K tokens proceeds directly; if over, splits with 500-token sliding window overlap. summarize_soap_task calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with versioned SOAP system prompt, writes clinical_notes record with soap_draft and status=pending_review, enqueues notify_therapist_task. Note Review: React polls GET /sessions/{id}/status every 5s via React Query until status=pending_review; therapist opens /notes/{session_id}, reviews SOAP draft and source transcript side by side, edits sections, clicks Approve; PATCH /notes/{id}/approve writes approved_at, approved_by, triggers Blob Storage lifecycle tag for audio deletion after 30 days.
target_output.data_model: session_recordings (id UUID PK, therapist_id UUID, client_pseudonym VARCHAR, session_date DATE, audio_blob_url VARCHAR, file_size_bytes BIGINT, consent_recorded BOOLEAN, status ENUM(uploading,transcribing,summarizing,pending_review,approved,failed), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). transcripts (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, transcript_text_encrypted TEXT, word_count INT, duration_seconds INT, transcribed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). clinical_notes (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, soap_draft TEXT, therapist_edits_json JSONB, status ENUM(pending_review,approved,archived), approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, approved_by UUID). note_reviews (id UUID PK, note_id UUID FK, therapist_id UUID, action ENUM(approved,revision_requested), edited_sections TEXT[], decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ai_generation_audit (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, task_type VARCHAR, model_version VARCHAR, prompt_version VARCHAR, input_tokens INT, output_tokens INT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST API with Azure AD JWT validation. POST /sessions/upload (multipart; streams to Blob Storage; returns session_id). GET /sessions/{id}/status (returns status ENUM and job progress). GET /notes/{id} (returns soap_draft, transcript side-by-side view data, ai_draft_flag=true). PATCH /notes/{id} (body: {edited_sections, soap_draft}; saves therapist edits). POST /notes/{id}/approve (writes approved_at; triggers audio deletion lifecycle tag). GET /export/fhir/{note_id} (returns FHIR R4 DocumentReference JSON for EHR integration; requires billing_admin or therapist role). GET /admin/sessions (practice_admin role; returns all practice sessions with status filter).
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA BAA signed with Azure. Azure Blob Storage CMK via Azure Key Vault for audio file encryption at rest. PostgreSQL column encryption on transcripts.transcript_text_encrypted and session_recordings.client_pseudonym. Azure AD Conditional Access Policy enforces MFA for all therapist and admin accounts. RBAC in FastAPI: therapist accesses own sessions only; practice_admin accesses all sessions in practice; billing_admin accesses approved notes for export. ai_generation_audit table: every GPT-4o invocation logged with model_version, prompt_version, input_tokens, output_tokens - satisfies HIPAA accountability rule for AI use on PHI. Patient consent boolean stored on session_recordings; API rejects upload if consent_recorded=false. AI-DRAFT watermark in GPT-4o system prompt; clinical_notes.soap_draft always includes 'AI-DRAFT' prefix until approved. Audio files deleted 30 days post-approval via Blob Storage lifecycle policy.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ArgoCD GitOps: Helm charts for api-deployment, celery-worker-deployment, redis-deployment versioned in Git under /helm directory. GitHub Actions: pytest 85% coverage, mypy, Docker build and push to Azure Container Registry, automated PR to update image tags in Helm chart values.yaml; ArgoCD detects image tag change and auto-syncs staging; production sync requires manual ArgoCD approval. AKS node pools: system pool (Standard_D2s_v3, 2-3 nodes, cluster-autoscaler), worker pool (Standard_D4s_v3, 2-5 nodes, KEDA ScaledObject targeting Redis list length for transcription_queue and summarization_queue). Rollback: ArgoCD 'rollback to previous sync' in UI or CLI. Secrets: Azure Key Vault CSI Driver mounts secrets as pod environment variables. PostgreSQL managed via Alembic migrations run as Kubernetes Job pre-deploy.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor with Grafana: AKS node CPU and memory per pool, Celery queue depths (transcription_queue, summarization_queue), transcription job P50/P95 latency (target: P95 under 5 minutes for 60-minute sessions), GPT-4o tokens consumed per day, note approval rate per therapist. Log Analytics Workspace: structured JSON logs from all pods; PHI masking on client_pseudonym and therapist_id fields in log streams. Azure Monitor alerts: Celery failure rate > 5% (PagerDuty), Azure Speech Services error rate > 3% (email), KEDA worker pool at max capacity for 10+ minutes (email to engineering).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS system pool ~$150/month, worker pool (2 baseline D4s_v3) ~$280/month scaling to ~$700 at 5 nodes peak. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (General Purpose 4 vCores) ~$200/month. Azure Cache for Redis (C1) ~$55/month. Azure Blob Storage ~$20/month at 1TB audio retention. Azure Speech Services batch transcription ~$1/hour audio. Azure OpenAI GPT-4o ~$0.01/1K output tokens. Total baseline ~$750/month scaling to ~$1200 at peak session volume.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Azure AD SSO integration, audio upload to Blob Storage with consent validation, Azure Speech Services transcription Celery task, GPT-4o SOAP generation with ai_generation_audit, React therapist review and approval UI, PostgreSQL schema with Alembic, ArgoCD GitOps pipeline for staging and production, HIPAA security controls (CMK, MFA, column encryption). Phase 2 (4 weeks): FHIR R4 DocumentReference export endpoint, practice_admin portal with session oversight, note_reviews audit trail UI, KEDA auto-scaling configuration for worker pool. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Azure Monitor Grafana dashboards, GPT-4o prompt versioning and A/B comparison, transcript chunking for sessions over 90 minutes, EHR write integration via Epic FHIR sandbox.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy strict type checking on all FastAPI route handlers and Celery task signatures. pytest 85% coverage gate on summarization chunking logic and FHIR export serialization. FastAPI dependency injection for Azure AD role checks - no inline permission logic. Celery task idempotency: all tasks check session_recordings.status before processing to prevent duplicate transcription or summarization on retry. Azure OpenAI calls wrapped in retry decorator with exponential backoff (max 3 retries, exclude 400/401 errors). GPT-4o system prompt versioned as prompt_v{n}.txt in Git; prompt_version recorded in ai_generation_audit on every invocation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Azure Speech Services transcription quality for therapy session audio (background noise, accents, clinical terminology) may produce transcripts requiring significant therapist correction. Mitigation: custom speech model training with therapy-domain vocabulary as Phase 3 option; transcript quality rating collected in note_reviews. Risk: GPT-4o SOAP output quality degrades for chunked long-session transcripts due to context discontinuity at chunk boundaries. Mitigation: 500-token overlap preserves context across chunk boundaries; SOAP sections from chunks merged with explicit section-level stitching. Risk: AKS worker pool auto-scaling lag causes transcription queue backlog during session upload spikes (e.g. end of business day). Mitigation: KEDA ScaledObject with aggressive scale-up threshold (queue depth > 3) and scale-down cooldown of 5 minutes.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Azure OpenAI Service over OpenAI API to keep PHI within Azure HIPAA BAA boundary. Chosen Celery over Azure Service Bus for job orchestration to leverage existing Python expertise and avoid Azure vendor lock-in for job queue. ArgoCD selected over Azure DevOps release pipelines to align with GitOps modernization requirement. 500-token chunk overlap selected based on typical SOAP note context requirements for 15-minute session segments.

metadata.notes: Moderate first-pass: therapy session AI summarization on AKS with ArgoCD GitOps. FHIR export section is slightly thin; transcript chunking strategy and AI audit trail are strong.

profile.domain: Secure Teletherapy Platform for Regional Mental Health Provider Network

project_goal.value: Secure teletherapy platform for a regional mental health provider network enabling HIPAA-eligible video sessions, end-to-end encrypted messaging between therapists and clients, appointment scheduling, and insurance billing integration with hardened security controls
project_goal.rationale: Core clinical operations platform for a 120-therapist regional network replacing fragmented scheduling and telehealth tools

target_users.value: Clients seeking mental health therapy, licensed therapists, practice administrators, billing staff, and network-level administrators
target_users.rationale: All roles in a regional provider network clinical operations workflow

project_class.rationale: Full-stack application with client portal, therapist portal, admin panel, real-time video/messaging, and billing backend

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, payments, realtime, admin_panel, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Video sessions, encrypted messaging, scheduling, Twilio integration, insurance billing, analytics dashboard

complexity_level.rationale: 120 therapists with concurrent video sessions, real-time messaging, insurance EDI 837 billing, and network-level analytics

risk_level.rationale: PHI in session content and clinical notes; payment card data; HIPAA, HITECH, and PCI-DSS compliance required

data_sensitivity.rationale: Session content, diagnoses, clinical notes, and messaging constitute PHI; payment card data requires PCI-DSS controls

external_exposure.rationale: All access requires Auth0 authentication; no public unauthenticated access to clinical or billing data

access_model.value: Auth0 with MFA enforcement; RBAC roles: client (scheduling, video, messaging, payment), therapist (scheduling, video, messaging, notes), practice_admin (practice user management, billing config), billing_admin (claim submission, EOB review), network_admin (cross-practice analytics, compliance reporting)
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege access across all clinical and administrative functions

feature_scope.value: Therapist and client scheduling portal, Twilio HIPAA-eligible video sessions, Signal Protocol encrypted messaging, session notes with CPT code assignment, insurance eligibility verification, CMS-1500 claim construction and EDI 837 submission, EOB processing and therapist payout, analytics dashboard for network administrators
feature_scope.rationale: Full clinical operations and billing feature set for regional network

mvp_scope.value: Appointment scheduling with client self-booking, Twilio video sessions, therapist session notes with CPT codes, insurance eligibility check, EDI 837 claim submission to clearinghouse, client copay collection via Stripe
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for network launch with revenue cycle management

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all transport; message content encrypted end-to-end with Signal Protocol; session notes AES-256 encrypted at rest with KMS; Auth0 MFA enforced for all roles; WAF on ALB; Stripe for payment card handling (PCI scope reduction via tokenization); append-only PHI access audit log
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA, HITECH, and PCI-DSS baseline controls

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript; separate client portal and therapist portal apps sharing a component library
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js for SSR SEO on marketing pages and fast portal load times; separate apps for clean role separation

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin for REST API; Go for WebSocket messaging server; Python 3.12 for billing EDI processing batch jobs
backend_stack.rationale: Go for high-throughput API and low-latency WebSocket server; Python for EDI 837 generation and clearinghouse integration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for clinical and billing data; Redis 7 for session presence and message delivery; S3 for session recording storage
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for relational clinical and billing integrity; Redis pub/sub for real-time message delivery; S3 for optional session recording retention

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 and us-west-2 for active-active multi-region availability
hosting_target.rationale: Multi-region for video session resilience; ECS Fargate for managed containers; HIPAA-eligible AWS services

privacy_retention_policy.value: Session notes and clinical records: 7 years per state mental health records law; secure_messages: 3 years; billing_claims: 7 years per Medicare/Medicaid requirements; session recordings (if enabled by client consent): 90 days; PHI audit log: 6 years per HIPAA
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State mental health records law and federal HIPAA/Medicare billing retention requirements

future_scope.value: Group therapy session support with multi-participant video, psychiatric prescription management integration with e-prescribing, client outcomes tracking with PHQ-9/GAD-7 longitudinal trends, payer contract management for in-network rate negotiations

constraints.value: BAAs required with Twilio and Stripe before production; Twilio HIPAA-eligible video rooms must be used (not standard Twilio Video); multi-region deployment must achieve 99.9% uptime SLA; PCI-DSS scope minimized via Stripe.js tokenization (card numbers never touch backend)
constraints.rationale: Compliance, SLA, and PCI scope requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for Go API and billing batch services; Datadog RUM for Next.js portals; PagerDuty for production alerts; custom metrics for video session join latency and message delivery latency
observability_baseline.rationale: Full-stack observability for clinical SLA monitoring

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints; feature flags via LaunchDarkly for phased rollout to practices; trunk-based development
execution_preference.rationale: Controlled rollout to 120 therapists with rollback capability per practice

compliance_context.value: HIPAA and HITECH for all PHI; PCI-DSS SAQ D for insurance billing; BAA required with Twilio (HIPAA-eligible Video), Stripe, AWS, and Auth0; SOC 2 Type II audit recommended for enterprise payer contracts
compliance_context.rationale: PHI in clinical operations, payment card handling, and enterprise payer requirements

project.goal: Full-stack teletherapy platform with video, encrypted messaging, scheduling, and insurance billing
project.target_users: Clients, therapists, practice admins, billing staff, network admins

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for video session UI state

backend.framework: Gin for REST API; gorilla/websocket for messaging
backend.api: REST API v1 with OpenAPI 3.0; WebSocket for real-time messaging

security.encryption: Signal Protocol for messages; AES-256 KMS for session notes; TLS 1.3
security.pci: Stripe.js tokenization eliminates backend card data

data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache for presence and pub/sub

devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP correctly prioritizes appointment scheduling, Twilio video, and EDI 837 billing as the revenue cycle foundation for network launch. The insurance eligibility check before session start is a critical workflow that reduces claim denials and should be surfaced prominently in the therapist scheduling UI, not just in the billing backend.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go for the API and WebSocket server is a strong choice for high-concurrency video session signaling and encrypted message delivery across 120 concurrent therapists. The EDI 837 billing pipeline in Python is correctly separated as a batch process from the real-time API to avoid blocking clinical workflows. Multi-region active-active on us-east-1 and us-west-2 requires careful Aurora Global Database configuration for cross-region replication.
reasoner_reviews.security: Signal Protocol for messaging end-to-end encryption is the right choice for therapist-client communication - it provides forward secrecy and break-in recovery that standard TLS does not. The implementation requires key exchange during client onboarding and ratcheting state stored in Redis per conversation. PCI scope is correctly minimized by Stripe.js tokenization keeping card data in Stripe's environment. The PHI audit log must capture therapist_id, client_id, action, and resource_type for every note access and messaging decryption event.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Twilio HIPAA-eligible Video Rooms require explicit room type configuration (group or peer-to-peer with 'go' room type in HIPAA mode) - standard Twilio Video rooms are not HIPAA-eligible. This must be explicit in the Twilio integration design. Auth0 MFA enforcement via Auth0 Actions is straightforward but must be verified for all RBAC roles including client accounts.

frontend.app_structure: Two Next.js 14 apps sharing a component library package: client-portal (scheduling, video join, messaging, payment, appointment history) and therapist-portal (schedule management, video host, note editor, billing dashboard). Shared library: ui-components, auth hooks wrapping Auth0 SDK, Twilio Video React SDK.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Client Portal: /schedule (therapist search and booking), /appointments (upcoming and past), /session/{appointment_id} (Twilio video room join + Signal Protocol messaging), /billing (copay payment with Stripe.js, insurance on file). Therapist Portal: /dashboard (today's schedule), /session/{appointment_id} (host video, session notes editor with CPT code picker), /clients/{id} (client history), /billing/claims (claim status).
frontend.state_management: React Query for appointment data, claim status, message history. Zustand for active video session UI state (muted, camera, layout). Twilio Video React SDK for video track management. Signal Protocol state managed in IndexedDB for E2E message key ratchet.
frontend.performance_notes: Twilio Video SDK loaded lazily on session page only. Signal Protocol key operations run in Web Worker to avoid blocking main thread during message encryption.

backend.service_design: Go Gin REST API (appointments, clients, therapists, session notes, billing). Go WebSocket server for Signal Protocol message relay and Twilio video signaling. Python Celery batch service for EDI 837 claim generation and clearinghouse HTTP submission. Separate Go billing-api service for Stripe payment intents and Twilio room provisioning.
backend.api_patterns: REST API v1 with Auth0 JWT validation middleware. WebSocket /ws/messages with per-user channel keyed by conversation_id. Twilio room provisioning via Twilio Programmable Video REST API POST /v1/Rooms with RoomType=group and MaxParticipants=2 in HIPAA mode.
backend.business_modules: Scheduling: appointment CRUD with therapist availability slots, Google Calendar-style free/busy query. Billing: insurance_eligibility check via Availity API before appointment confirmation; EDI 837 claim construction from session_notes CPT codes and billing_claims table; EDI submission to Change Healthcare clearinghouse; ERA/EOB processing from clearinghouse response files.
backend.failure_handling: Twilio room creation failure returns 503 to client with retry guidance. EDI 837 submission failure writes claim to dead_letter_claims table for manual review. Stripe payment failure returns 402 with Stripe error code. WebSocket disconnection triggers message re-delivery from Redis queue on reconnect.

security.auth_design: Auth0 with Universal Login, MFA enforced via Auth0 Actions for all roles. Auth0 RBAC with role-based access tokens. JWKs endpoint cached in Go API for JWT validation without Auth0 round-trip per request.
security.authorization_model: RBAC in Go middleware: client role restricted to own appointments, messages, and payment data; therapist role accesses own schedule and assigned clients only; practice_admin manages own practice users; billing_admin accesses all claims within practice; network_admin accesses cross-practice analytics views only.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting in Go API: 60 req/min per authenticated user, 5 req/min for appointment booking to prevent slot-holding abuse. Twilio room access token issued per appointment_id with 1-hour expiry and room name = appointment_id to prevent cross-session access.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: phi_access_log PostgreSQL table: user_id, user_role, action ENUM(viewed_note,sent_message,decrypted_message,viewed_appointment,submitted_claim), resource_id, resource_type, timestamp. Forwarded to CloudTrail and Datadog with therapist_id and client_id fields masked in log stream.

data.entities: clients (id UUID PK, auth0_sub, name_encrypted, email_encrypted, dob_encrypted, insurance_member_id_encrypted, primary_therapist_id FK, created_at), therapists (id UUID PK, auth0_sub, name, npi_number, license_state, license_number, cpt_codes_authorized TEXT[], practice_id FK), appointments (id UUID PK, client_id FK, therapist_id FK, scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, duration_minutes INT, appointment_type ENUM, twilio_room_name VARCHAR, status ENUM, confirmed_at, completed_at), video_sessions (id UUID PK, appointment_id FK, twilio_room_sid VARCHAR, started_at, ended_at, duration_seconds INT), secure_messages (id UUID PK, conversation_id UUID, sender_id UUID, recipient_id UUID, ciphertext_blob BYTEA, signal_ratchet_header BYTEA, sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ), session_notes (id UUID PK, appointment_id FK, therapist_id FK, note_content_encrypted BYTEA, cpt_codes TEXT[], diagnosis_codes_icd10 TEXT[], signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), billing_claims (id UUID PK, appointment_id FK, client_id FK, therapist_id FK, cpt_code VARCHAR, diagnosis_code VARCHAR, billed_amount NUMERIC, insurance_plan_id FK, status ENUM, edi_837_payload TEXT, submitted_at, clearinghouse_claim_id VARCHAR), insurance_plans (id UUID PK, payer_name VARCHAR, payer_id VARCHAR, plan_type VARCHAR, copay_amount NUMERIC)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Aurora Global Database (primary us-east-1, read replica us-west-2) with KMS encryption at rest. Redis 7 ElastiCache for Signal Protocol ratchet state cache, Twilio room presence, and appointment slot availability. S3 with SSE-KMS for optional session recordings with 90-day lifecycle.
data.retention_and_deletion: session_notes and billing_claims: 7-year retention. secure_messages: 3-year retention with scheduled batch deletion. phi_access_log: 6-year retention per HIPAA. S3 session recordings: 90-day lifecycle unless client consent extends to 7 years for clinical use.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate active-active: us-east-1 and us-west-2 clusters, each running api-service, websocket-service, and billing-batch-service tasks. Route 53 latency-based routing to nearest region. Aurora Global Database primary in us-east-1 with failover replica in us-west-2. Redis ElastiCache Global Datastore for cross-region session presence sync.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Go tests (90% coverage gate), Python pytest for billing logic, Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. LaunchDarkly feature flags for per-practice rollout. Blue-green deployment for api-service via ALB weighted routing.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM: Go API trace sampling, WebSocket message latency P50/P95, Twilio room join latency. Datadog RUM on Next.js portals. Custom metrics: concurrent_video_sessions, message_delivery_latency_ms, claim_submission_success_rate, eligibility_check_latency. PagerDuty: error rate > 1%, p99 API latency > 2s, concurrent_video_sessions > 100 (capacity warning).

target_output.thinking_summary: High-scale teletherapy platform with multiple critical compliance dimensions: HIPAA for PHI, PCI-DSS for payments, BAA with Twilio and Stripe. Key decisions: Go for low-latency API and WebSocket server supporting 120 concurrent therapists; Signal Protocol for therapist-client messaging forward secrecy; Aurora Global Database for multi-region active-active; EDI 837 billing as separate Python batch process to isolate from clinical real-time path; Stripe.js tokenization for PCI scope reduction. The plan is moderate quality with strong security and billing sections but the analytics section is slightly thin.
target_output.title: Regional Mental Health Teletherapy Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A HIPAA-compliant high-scale fullstack teletherapy platform for a 120-therapist regional network, built with Next.js 14 client and therapist portals communicating to a Go 1.22 Gin API and WebSocket server on AWS ECS Fargate in an active-active multi-region deployment. Twilio HIPAA-eligible video rooms power clinical sessions. Signal Protocol provides end-to-end encrypted therapist-client messaging with forward secrecy. Insurance billing flows through a Python EDI 837 batch service submitting claims to Change Healthcare clearinghouse. Stripe.js tokenization reduces PCI scope by keeping card data in Stripe's environment. BAAs are in place with Twilio, Stripe, AWS, and Auth0.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two Next.js 14 apps (client portal and therapist portal) share a Tailwind component library and communicate over HTTPS to a Go Gin REST API and a Go WebSocket server running on ECS Fargate in active-active regions (us-east-1 and us-west-2) behind Route 53 latency-based routing and Application Load Balancers with WAF. Aurora Global Database (primary us-east-1, failover us-west-2) stores all clinical and billing data with KMS encryption. Redis 7 ElastiCache Global Datastore maintains Signal Protocol ratchet state and Twilio room presence across regions. Auth0 with MFA enforces role-based access for all five user roles. Twilio HIPAA-eligible Video Rooms are provisioned per appointment via the Go billing-api service with access tokens scoped to appointment_id. A Python Celery billing batch service constructs EDI 837 claims from session_notes CPT codes and submits them to Change Healthcare clearinghouse. All PHI access is logged to an append-only phi_access_log table.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, Twilio Video React SDK, Signal Protocol JS library (libsignal-client). Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, gorilla/websocket. Billing: Python 3.12, Celery, pyx12 for EDI 837 generation. Database: Aurora PostgreSQL 16 Global Database with KMS. Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache Global Datastore. Auth: Auth0 with MFA and RBAC. Integrations: Twilio HIPAA Video, Stripe (payments), Availity (insurance eligibility), Change Healthcare EDI clearinghouse. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate multi-region, ALB with WAF, Route 53. Monitoring: Datadog APM and RUM, PagerDuty. Feature Flags: LaunchDarkly.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Appointment Scheduling: client self-booking with therapist availability calendar; insurance eligibility verification via Availity API before confirmation; automated appointment reminders via SNS. Twilio Video Sessions: therapist provisions HIPAA-eligible room via Go billing-api; access tokens scoped to appointment_id with 1-hour expiry; both parties join via Twilio Video React SDK. Signal Protocol Messaging: E2E encrypted messaging with forward secrecy; key exchange at client onboarding; ratchet state in Redis; ciphertext_blob stored in secure_messages table. Session Notes: therapist writes SOAP note with CPT code picker and ICD-10 diagnosis code autocomplete; note encrypted with KMS before PostgreSQL storage. Insurance Billing: EDI 837 claim constructed from session_notes (CPT + ICD-10); submitted to Change Healthcare; ERA/EOB processed for therapist payout via ACH. Client Payment: Stripe.js tokenization for copay collection; payment_intent created in Go billing-api; receipt linked to appointment. Network Analytics: cross-practice session volume, revenue by payer, claim denial rate, no-show rate for network_admin role.
target_output.system_components: Route 53 latency-based routing to us-east-1 and us-west-2 ALBs. ALB with WAF (OWASP ruleset, rate limiting) in each region. ECS Fargate clusters: api-service (4-12 tasks, scales on CPU), websocket-service (2-8 tasks, scales on connection count), billing-batch-service (1-4 tasks, Celery workers). Aurora Global Database primary and failover. Redis 7 ElastiCache Global Datastore. S3 buckets for session recordings (SSE-KMS). Auth0 tenant with MFA Actions. Twilio HIPAA Video Rooms API. Stripe payment processing. Availity insurance eligibility API. Change Healthcare EDI clearinghouse. LaunchDarkly feature flag SDK. Datadog APM and RUM agents.
target_output.workflows: Session Lifecycle: Client books appointment via POST /v1/appointments; Go API runs Availity eligibility check via GET /eligibility?member_id=&payer_id=; if eligible, appointment saved and client notified via SNS. 15 minutes before session, Go billing-api provisions Twilio HIPAA room: POST /v1/Rooms with RoomType=group, RecordParticipantsOnConnect=false, UniqueName=appt_{appointment_id}; access tokens issued to therapist and client scoped to room name. Both join via Twilio Video React SDK. After session, therapist writes SOAP note with CPT code 90837 and ICD-10 code; note_content encrypted with KMS CMK before INSERT to session_notes; appointment status updated to completed. Billing: Python Celery billing_batch_dag runs nightly; for each completed session with signed note: constructs EDI 837 professional claim using pyx12 with NPI, CPT, ICD-10, and insurance_plan payer_id; POSTs to Change Healthcare /v3/x12/claim; writes edi_837_payload and clearinghouse_claim_id to billing_claims; ERA response processed to update claim status and trigger ACH payout to therapist. Messaging: Client sends message; Signal Protocol JS encrypts to ciphertext_blob on client device; POST /ws/messages over WebSocket; Go server relays to recipient WebSocket connection or queues in Redis for offline delivery; ciphertext_blob persisted to secure_messages table without server decryption.
target_output.data_model: clients (id UUID PK, auth0_sub VARCHAR, name_encrypted BYTEA, email_encrypted BYTEA, dob_encrypted BYTEA, insurance_member_id_encrypted BYTEA, primary_therapist_id UUID FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). therapists (id UUID PK, auth0_sub VARCHAR, name VARCHAR, npi_number VARCHAR UNIQUE, license_state CHAR(2), cpt_codes_authorized TEXT[], practice_id UUID FK). appointments (id UUID PK, client_id UUID FK, therapist_id UUID FK, scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, duration_minutes INT, appointment_type ENUM(initial,followup,crisis), twilio_room_name VARCHAR, status ENUM(scheduled,confirmed,in_progress,completed,cancelled,no_show), confirmed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). video_sessions (id UUID PK, appointment_id UUID FK, twilio_room_sid VARCHAR, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ). secure_messages (id UUID PK, conversation_id UUID, sender_id UUID, recipient_id UUID, ciphertext_blob BYTEA, signal_ratchet_header BYTEA, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). session_notes (id UUID PK, appointment_id UUID FK, therapist_id UUID FK, note_content_encrypted BYTEA, cpt_codes TEXT[], diagnosis_codes_icd10 TEXT[], signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). billing_claims (id UUID PK, appointment_id UUID FK, billed_amount NUMERIC, status ENUM(draft,submitted,accepted,denied,paid), edi_837_payload TEXT, clearinghouse_claim_id VARCHAR, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). insurance_plans (id UUID PK, payer_name VARCHAR, payer_id VARCHAR, plan_type VARCHAR, copay_amount NUMERIC).
target_output.api_design: REST API v1 with Auth0 JWT validation. POST /v1/appointments (body: {therapist_id, scheduled_at, appointment_type}; triggers eligibility check; returns appointment with twilio_room_name). GET /v1/appointments?status=upcoming (client/therapist role; returns paginated list). POST /v1/sessions/{appointment_id}/room-token (issues Twilio access token scoped to room; requires appointment ownership check). GET /v1/clients/{id}/notes (therapist role; returns session notes with decrypted content). POST /v1/notes (therapist role; body: {appointment_id, note_content, cpt_codes, diagnosis_codes}; encrypts and stores). POST /v1/payments/intent (client role; creates Stripe PaymentIntent for copay; returns client_secret). GET /v1/billing/claims?status=submitted (billing_admin role; returns claim status). WebSocket /ws/messages (Auth0 JWT in query param; upgrades to WebSocket; relays Signal Protocol ciphertext_blob messages per conversation_id).
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA: BAAs with AWS, Auth0, Twilio (HIPAA-eligible Video), and Stripe. Aurora KMS encryption at rest. session_notes.note_content_encrypted: AES-256 via KMS CMK. clients.name/email/dob/insurance fields: column-level encryption. TLS 1.3 for all transport. Signal Protocol E2E encryption for messaging: server relays ciphertext only, cannot decrypt. PCI-DSS: Stripe.js tokenization eliminates backend card number handling; no PAN stored in PostgreSQL; Stripe handles PCI-DSS compliance for card storage. Auth0 MFA enforced for all roles via Auth0 Actions. RBAC in Go middleware: client accesses own appointments and messages only; therapist accesses own schedule and assigned clients; billing_admin accesses claims within practice only. phi_access_log append-only table: every note view, message relay, and appointment access logged. WAF rules: OWASP top 10, rate limiting 60 req/min per user, Twilio webhook signature validation. Twilio room provisioning with RoomType=group in HIPAA mode; access tokens expire after 1 hour and are scoped to appointment_id room name.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: Go tests with 90% coverage gate, mypy for Python billing service, Hadolint Dockerfile lint, Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Blue-green for api-service via ALB weighted target groups. Environments: dev (single-region ECS, RDS single AZ), staging (multi-region ECS, Aurora Global Database), production (active-active us-east-1 and us-west-2, Aurora Global Database primary us-east-1 with failover replica us-west-2, Redis Global Datastore). LaunchDarkly feature flags for per-practice rollout with kill switches. Database migrations via golang-migrate run as ECS one-off task pre-deploy. Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation. Rollback: ECS service update to previous task definition. Route 53 health checks trigger automatic failover to us-west-2 if us-east-1 ALB health check fails.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on all ECS tasks: trace sampling for Go API and WebSocket server. Datadog RUM on Next.js portals: client portal session page performance and video join success rate. Custom metrics: concurrent_video_sessions (target < 100, scale alert > 90), message_delivery_latency_ms (P95 target < 200ms), claim_submission_success_rate (target > 98%), eligibility_check_latency_p95, no_show_rate_weekly. PagerDuty escalation: API error rate > 1%, p99 latency > 2s, claim submission error rate > 5%, Twilio room provisioning failure rate > 2%, Aurora replication lag > 30s.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate api-service and websocket-service (~$400/month baseline scaling to ~$900 at 120 concurrent sessions). Aurora Global Database (~$600/month for primary plus cross-region replica). Redis ElastiCache Global Datastore (~$200/month). Twilio HIPAA Video ~$0.004/participant minute (~$800/month for 120 therapists at 6 sessions/day). Stripe payment processing fees passed to practice. Total infrastructure ~$2200/month baseline. ECS auto-scaling on CPU 70% target for api-service; websocket-service scales on ALB active connection count.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Auth0 integration with RBAC, appointment scheduling with Availity eligibility check, Twilio HIPAA video room provisioning, session notes with CPT code picker and KMS encryption, PostgreSQL schema with golang-migrate, Signal Protocol message infrastructure, Stripe copay collection, EDI 837 claim generation and Change Healthcare submission, multi-region ECS and Aurora Global Database, HIPAA security controls. Phase 2 (6 weeks): E2E Signal Protocol messaging in Next.js portal, ERA/EOB processing and ACH payout, LaunchDarkly feature flag rollout, network analytics dashboard for network_admin role, phi_access_log audit reporting. Phase 3 (4 weeks): SOC 2 Type II evidence collection, group therapy multi-participant video support, Datadog RUM instrumentation, payer contract management for in-network rates.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go code: golangci-lint with go vet, staticcheck, errcheck. 90% test coverage on billing claim construction and RBAC middleware. Python billing service: mypy strict, pytest 85% coverage on EDI 837 generation. PHI fields never appear in log output - enforced by Datadog field masking config and Go log wrapper that scrubs encrypted field keys. Twilio access token generation must include room name = appointment_id and expiry = now + 3600; no generic room tokens. Auth0 JWT validation uses JWKs caching with 24-hour TTL and background refresh. LaunchDarkly flag evaluation in API middleware for per-practice feature rollout; flags keyed by practice_id.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Signal Protocol ratchet state in Redis is single point of failure for message delivery; Redis ElastiCache Global Datastore replication lag could lose in-flight ratchet state during region failover. Mitigation: Signal Protocol client resync on reconnect; messages held in delivery queue until ack. Risk: Availity eligibility API latency during peak hours delays appointment confirmation. Mitigation: eligibility check result cached per insurance_plan_id for 24 hours; background re-verification before session start. Risk: Change Healthcare EDI clearinghouse outage blocks claim submission during billing batch window. Mitigation: dead_letter_claims table and manual resubmission workflow with 3-business-day SLA. Tradeoff: Go over Node.js for WebSocket server - higher throughput for concurrent video signaling at cost of smaller team Go expertise pool.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Twilio HIPAA-eligible Video Rooms (group type in HIPAA mode) over Zoom for Healthcare SDK - Twilio BAA process is faster for MVP timeline. Selected Signal Protocol over Virgil Security for E2E messaging - Signal Protocol is open standard with strong auditing record. Decided Aurora Global Database over two independent RDS instances for active-active - Aurora handles cross-region replication lag and failover automatically. PCI scope confirmed as SAQ D given insurance billing; Stripe.js tokenization reduces merchant card data scope significantly.

metadata.notes: Moderate first-pass: high-scale teletherapy platform with Twilio HIPAA video, Signal Protocol messaging, EDI 837 billing, and multi-region active-active deployment. Analytics section slightly thin.

profile.domain: Consumer Mental Wellness / Wearable Biometrics

project_goal.value: Real-time mental wellness coaching AI for consumer wearable ecosystem, analyzing biometric streams (HRV, skin conductance) and delivering LLM-generated micro-interventions for stress management
project_goal.rationale: Core product differentiator combining biometric sensing with personalized AI coaching

target_users.value: Consumer wearable device owners seeking proactive stress management coaching via a companion mobile app
target_users.rationale: Primary audience identified in user research; secondary audience is enterprise wellness programs

project_class.rationale: Core value is real-time biometric inference and LLM-driven intervention generation, not a traditional CRUD application

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, realtime, analytics, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Wearable integration, streaming inference, LLM coaching, and analytics are all core capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Concurrent WebSocket streams from potentially millions of wearable devices with sub-second inference SLA

risk_level.rationale: Processes sensitive biometric health data; FDA SaMD guidance may apply; duty-of-care obligations for stress intervention

data_sensitivity.rationale: HRV and skin conductance are biometric health indicators; HIPAA considerations apply

external_exposure.rationale: Mobile app requires Firebase Auth before any biometric data is transmitted

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with per-user data isolation; device pairing tokens scoped to authenticated user ID
access_model.rationale: Each wearable device binds to exactly one authenticated user; no cross-user data access permitted

feature_scope.value: Real-time biometric stream ingestion, stress signature detection, LLM micro-intervention delivery, personalized baseline modeling, weekly wellness digest, device management, coaching history
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for v1 consumer launch

mvp_scope.value: HRV stream ingestion, basic stress threshold alerting, 3 intervention types (breathing, grounding, reframe), coaching session history
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for beta launch with first wearable hardware partner

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all WebSocket and REST traffic, AES-256 encryption at rest for biometric data, Firebase Auth JWT validation, data minimization on device payloads
security_baseline.rationale: Health data classification requires encryption baseline; device payloads must not include PII beyond user ID

frontend_stack.value: React Native with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform mobile for iOS and Android; team has existing React Native expertise

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI and WebSocket support
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async WebSocket handling for biometric streams; Python ecosystem for ML inference integration

data_platform.value: TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension) for biometric time-series; Redis 7 for stream buffering and session state
data_platform.rationale: TimescaleDB hypertables optimized for high-frequency biometric time-series; Redis for sub-second stream buffering

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot on GCP us-central1
hosting_target.rationale: Autopilot manages node scaling for variable WebSocket load; GCP offers Vertex AI integration for model serving

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw biometric streams retained 90 days; aggregated stress events retained 2 years; intervention logs retained 3 years; user-initiated deletion deletes all records within 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Balances regulatory obligations with storage cost; user deletion right per CCPA

future_scope.value: Multi-device support, therapist-linked coaching mode, clinical trial data export, Apple Health and Google Fit integration
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap dependent on v1 traction and regulatory clarity

constraints.value: Sub-500ms intervention delivery SLA from stress detection; $0.008 per LLM intervention cost ceiling; no biometric data stored outside GCP us-central1 region
constraints.rationale: Latency SLA from UX research; LLM cost ceiling from unit economics model; data residency from legal

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for infrastructure metrics; custom dashboards for per-user stress event rates, intervention latency P95, and WebSocket connection counts
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard GKE observability stack with domain-specific biometric KPI dashboards

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints with continuous deployment to staging; monthly beta releases to wearable partner test cohort
execution_preference.rationale: Hardware partner release cadence constrains production deployment frequency

llm_integration.value: Google Gemini Flash 1.5 via Vertex AI for intervention generation; prompt templates versioned in GCS; output validated against safe-messaging guidelines before delivery
llm_integration.rationale: Gemini Flash meets sub-200ms generation latency requirement; Vertex AI keeps data within GCP trust boundary for compliance

compliance_context.value: HIPAA considerations for biometric health data; FDA SaMD wellness boundary must be maintained; user informed consent for biometric processing; CCPA data subject rights implementation required
compliance_context.rationale: Biometric data triggers health data classification; FDA boundary maintained to avoid device reclassification as medical device

project.goal: Real-time biometric stress detection with LLM-generated personalized interventions
project.target_users: Consumer wearable device owners on iOS and Android
project.timeline: Beta with hardware partner Q3, consumer launch Q4

frontend.state: Zustand for local state, React Query for server sync
frontend.realtime: Native WebSocket client for biometric stream

backend.api: REST for CRUD operations, WebSocket for biometric streams

security.auth: Firebase Auth with JWT validation on every API and WebSocket connection
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.data_isolation: TimescaleDB row-level security keyed to user_id

data.cache: Redis 7 for stream buffering and inference result caching
data.llm: Vertex AI Gemini Flash for intervention generation

devops.environments: dev, staging, production; separate GCP projects per environment

constraints.latency: <500ms end-to-end from stress detection to intervention delivery

open_questions.fda_boundary: Legal review of FDA SaMD wellness boundary ongoing

reasoner_reviews.product: The real-time biometric coaching loop is the core product differentiator. The MVP scope of HRV ingestion plus 3 intervention types is achievable for a beta with a hardware partner but LLM intervention quality will define retention. Personalized baseline modeling is essential — generic stress thresholds will produce too many false positives and erode user trust before the product can demonstrate value.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: TimescaleDB hypertables are the right choice for high-frequency biometric time-series; the chunk interval should be tuned to the expected write rate per device. The WebSocket fan-out architecture needs careful backpressure management — a slow inference service must not block the stream ingestion path. A Redis Stream as a decoupling buffer between the WebSocket gateway and the ML inference workers is essential for SLA reliability.
reasoner_reviews.security: Firebase Auth JWT validation must occur at the WebSocket upgrade handshake, not only at REST endpoints. Biometric payload schema must be validated server-side to prevent injection of malformed sensor data. The LLM prompt must be constructed server-side — never pass user-controlled text directly as a prompt prefix to prevent prompt injection attacks on the intervention generator.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The sub-500ms intervention SLA is aggressive given the LLM generation step. Gemini Flash typically returns in 150-250ms at P90, leaving 250ms for biometric analysis, stress detection, and network round trip. This is feasible but requires co-locating the inference service and LLM call in the same GCP region with no synchronous database writes in the hot path.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The FDA SaMD boundary is the most significant unresolved risk. If stress detection is positioned as diagnosing or treating a medical condition, the product could be reclassified as a medical device. The safe framing is 'wellness coaching' with explicit disclaimers — this must be reflected in intervention copy guidelines, onboarding consent flow, and marketing language, not just in legal documents.

frontend.app_structure: React Native app with feature modules: biometric-stream (WebSocket client, device pairing), coaching (intervention display, session history), wellness-insights (weekly digest, trend charts), settings (device management, privacy controls)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Onboarding: device pairing wizard, biometric consent, baseline calibration. Main: real-time stress gauge with active intervention overlay. History: coaching session timeline with intervention efficacy ratings. Insights: weekly HRV trend chart and intervention frequency heatmap.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for biometric stream state and active intervention; React Query for historical data (coaching sessions, weekly summaries); expo-sqlite for offline session caching when WebSocket drops
frontend.performance_notes: WebSocket reconnect with exponential backoff and jitter; biometric gauge renders at 60fps using Reanimated 3 worklets running on the UI thread; intervention cards pre-fetched on stress threshold approach to eliminate perceived latency at delivery time

backend.service_design: Three FastAPI services: stream-gateway (WebSocket ingestion and Firebase Auth validation), inference-worker (stress detection + Vertex AI orchestration as Redis Stream consumer), coaching-api (REST for history, preferences, and device management)
backend.api_patterns: WebSocket at /ws/biometric/{device_id} for stream ingestion; REST at /v1/sessions, /v1/interventions, /v1/insights, /v1/devices; Server-Sent Events at /v1/coaching/live for real-time intervention push to mobile client
backend.background_jobs: Nightly per-user baseline calibration job recomputing HRV mean and std from prior 7 days; weekly wellness digest generation job; Redis Stream consumer worker processing biometric events for stress inference
backend.failure_handling: Redis Stream as buffer between WebSocket gateway and inference workers with consumer group acknowledgment; dead-letter queue for failed inference jobs; circuit breaker around Vertex AI calls with fallback to pre-cached intervention library serving within 50ms

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth JWT validated at WebSocket upgrade handshake (connection rejected on 401) and on every REST request; device pairing tokens are 24h HMAC-SHA256 signed tokens binding device_id to user_id
security.privacy_controls: Biometric data stored with user_id as only identifier; no name, email, or location in time-series tables; CCPA deletion implemented as immediate soft-delete flag with 30-day hard purge job and append-only GCS audit log
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting on WebSocket connections: max 1 active stream per device_id enforced at gateway; Vertex AI LLM output validated against safe-messaging word list before delivery; intervention rate-limited to 6 per user per hour
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager for Vertex AI API keys and Firebase service account credentials; External Secrets Operator syncs secrets to GKE pod environment variables; secret rotation every 90 days via automated pipeline

data.entities: biometric_streams (user_id UUID, device_id UUID, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, hrv_ms SMALLINT, skin_conductance_us NUMERIC(6,3), motion_artifact_flag BOOL) as TimescaleDB hypertable with 1-hour chunks. stress_events (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, severity ENUM mild/moderate/severe, hrv_baseline_deviation_pct NUMERIC(5,2)). interventions (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, stress_event_id UUID FK, intervention_type ENUM breathing/grounding/reframe, llm_prompt_version VARCHAR, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, efficacy_rating SMALLINT). user_baselines (user_id UUID PK, hrv_mean_7d NUMERIC, hrv_std_7d NUMERIC, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). wearable_devices (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK, hardware_model VARCHAR, paired_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB hypertables with 1-hour chunks and 90-day automated retention policy; continuous aggregates for hourly HRV statistics; Redis Streams for real-time biometric event queue with consumer groups; GCS bucket for versioned LLM prompt templates and weekly digest PDFs
data.retention_and_deletion: TimescaleDB retention policy drops raw biometric chunks after 90 days; stress_events and interventions retained 3 years; user-initiated CCPA deletion triggers async purge job across all tables within 30 days with completion audit record in GCS

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1 with three Helm-managed deployments: stream-gateway (HPA on WebSocket connection count, min 3/max 20 pods), inference-workers (HPA on Redis stream consumer lag, min 2/max 15 pods), coaching-api (HPA on CPU 60%, min 2/max 8 pods)
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI pipeline: ruff lint + mypy type check, pytest unit tests with 85% coverage gate, Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, helm upgrade to staging namespace; production deployment gated by manual approval + ArgoCD canary at 5% traffic for 30 minutes with automatic rollback on error rate >2%
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboards: WebSocket active connections, biometric ingestion rate (events/sec), Redis stream consumer lag (alert >1000), stress detection latency P50/P95 (SLA alert >300ms), LLM intervention latency P50/P95 (SLA alert >400ms), intervention delivery success rate (alert <99%)
devops.rollback_strategy: ArgoCD manages Helm releases with automatic rollback if canary error rate exceeds 2% or P95 latency exceeds 800ms within 30-minute canary window; Alembic database migrations are additive-only with documented rollback scripts

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a real-time biometric AI coaching system on GKE. The critical architectural decision is decoupling the WebSocket ingestion path from LLM inference using Redis Streams as a buffer — this protects the sub-500ms SLA by ensuring biometric data is never lost even if inference workers are temporarily saturated. TimescaleDB hypertables are optimal for HRV time-series at device scale. The FDA SaMD boundary risk drives server-side LLM output validation against safe-messaging guidelines before any delivery to the mobile client.
target_output.title: Real-Time Wearable Biometric Coaching AI Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale AI wellness coaching system for consumer wearables built on GKE Autopilot with FastAPI WebSocket gateway, TimescaleDB for biometric time-series, and Vertex AI Gemini Flash for personalized micro-intervention generation. The architecture decouples biometric ingestion from inference via Redis Streams, enabling sub-500ms stress-detection-to-intervention delivery while maintaining HIPAA-aligned data handling and FDA SaMD wellness boundary compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven streaming architecture with three GKE services: a stream-gateway service maintaining WebSocket connections from React Native clients, a Redis Streams buffer decoupling ingestion from inference, and inference-worker pods running HRV stress detection and orchestrating Vertex AI Gemini Flash calls. The coaching-api provides REST endpoints for session history, personalized baselines, and device management. TimescaleDB stores biometric hypertables with 1-hour chunks; Redis Streams buffer real-time events with consumer group acknowledgment. Firebase Auth JWT validation occurs at both WebSocket upgrade and REST request boundaries. All data is confined to GCP us-central1; LLM calls use Vertex AI to keep biometric context within the GCP trust boundary. ArgoCD manages Helm releases with canary deployment and automatic rollback.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native with TypeScript, Zustand, React Query, expo-sqlite, Reanimated 3. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI with async WebSocket handlers, Redis Streams consumer workers. AI: Vertex AI Gemini Flash 1.5, prompt templates versioned in GCS, safe-messaging output validator. Data: TimescaleDB hypertables on CloudSQL, Redis 7 Memorystore. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot us-central1, Artifact Registry, GCP Secret Manager, External Secrets Operator, ArgoCD. CI/CD: CircleCI, Helm. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus, Alertmanager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Biometric Stream Ingestion: React Native app maintains persistent WebSocket to stream-gateway transmitting HRV and skin conductance at 1Hz with device_id binding validated against wearable_devices table. Stress Detection: inference-worker consumes Redis Stream events, computes HRV z-score deviation from user-specific 7-day rolling baseline, classifies severity as mild/moderate/severe. LLM Intervention Engine: on stress_events insert, orchestrator calls Vertex AI Gemini Flash with versioned prompt template, validates output against safe-messaging word list, pushes intervention to client via SSE. Personalized Baseline Modeling: nightly job recomputes per-user HRV mean and standard deviation from prior 7 days and updates user_baselines. Weekly Wellness Digest: batch job aggregates stress_events and interventions into structured summary delivered as mobile push notification.
target_output.system_components: stream-gateway pods (FastAPI async WebSocket handlers, Firebase Auth JWT validation at upgrade handshake, biometric payload schema validation, Redis XADD to biometric-events stream). inference-worker pods (Redis XREAD consumer group, HRV deviation computation against user_baselines, stress_events writer, Vertex AI Gemini Flash call, safe-messaging validator, SSE push via coaching-api). coaching-api pods (REST CRUD for coaching sessions, interventions, devices, user preferences, weekly digests, CCPA deletion endpoint). TimescaleDB CloudSQL (biometric_streams hypertable 1h chunks, continuous aggregates, 90-day retention policy). Redis 7 Memorystore (biometric-events stream, intervention result cache, WebSocket session state). GCS buckets (LLM prompt templates versioned by semver, weekly digest PDFs, CCPA deletion audit logs).
