target_output.title: Peer Support Wellness App Landing Page Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: Astro 4 static landing page on Netlify CDN replacing a legacy early-2010s HTML/CSS template for a peer support emotional wellness app. Introduces mobile-responsive layout, WebP-optimized app screenshots, peer testimonials, and UTM-tracked iOS and Android download CTAs. A minimal client-side island reads UTM query parameters and injects them into app store links for install attribution. GA4 tracks download intent events with campaign context.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Astro 4 site compiled to /dist and deployed to Netlify CDN via GitHub Actions. Zero server-side runtime at request time. One small client-side Astro island (UTMPassthrough) handles UTM parameter injection into app store links on page load. All other components render to static HTML with no JavaScript. Tailwind CSS purged to minimal bundle. GA4 gtag.js loaded asynchronously for analytics. Netlify handles HTTPS, security headers from netlify.toml, and atomic deploys with rollback capability.
target_output.technology_stack: Astro 4 with TypeScript, zero-JS static output except UTMPassthrough island and GA4. Tailwind CSS 3 with Astro integration for purged CSS. Netlify CDN for hosting with automatic HTTPS. GitHub Actions for CI build validation. Google Analytics 4 with gtag.js for app store click event tracking.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: app device mockup screenshot, headline, subheadline, primary iOS and Android CTA buttons. Feature Highlights: three wellness feature cards with icons. Testimonials: four peer testimonial cards with avatar images. Download Section: sticky bottom CTA bar on mobile with iOS and Android buttons. UTM Passthrough: client island reads page UTM params and updates all CTA links before render.
target_output.system_components: Astro 4 build generating static HTML to /dist. Tailwind CSS purge configured on /src/**/*.{astro,ts}. /src/components/UTMPassthrough.astro with client:load directive reading URLSearchParams and updating querySelectorAll('[data-cta-platform]') href attributes. /src/data/screenshots.json, testimonials.json, and ctas.json for content. GA4 gtag.js snippet in base layout head. netlify.toml with X-Frame-Options DENY and CSP headers. Netlify CDN serving /dist assets.
target_output.workflows: Content Update Workflow: marketing author edits /src/data/testimonials.json or /src/pages/index.astro, opens PR, GitHub Actions runs Astro build and html-validate, Netlify generates preview deploy URL, author reviews on mobile viewport, PR merged triggers Netlify production atomic deploy. UTM Passthrough Workflow: visitor arrives at wellness-app.com/?utm_source=facebook&utm_campaign=spring_launch; UTMPassthrough island fires on client load, reads URLSearchParams, rewrites iOS CTA href from apps.apple.com/app/id123 to apps.apple.com/app/id123?pt=spring_launch&ct=facebook&mt=8 (App Store UTM format), rewrites Android CTA href appending &utm_source=facebook&utm_campaign=spring_launch to Play Store URL; GA4 app_store_click event fires with {platform, utm_source, utm_campaign} on CTA click.
target_output.data_model: feature_sections: Astro component props (section_id, headline, body_copy, icon_name). app_screenshots: /src/data/screenshots.json array (filename, alt_text, display_order). testimonials: /src/data/testimonials.json array (author_first_name, quote, avatar_filename, app_rating). download_ctas: /src/data/ctas.json (platform ios/android, base_store_url, cta_label). No database; all content in version-controlled JSON and Astro component files. privacy_labels: /src/data/privacy_labels.json array (platform ios/android, data_category, usage_description) for App Store and Play Store privacy nutrition cards.
target_output.api_design: No server API. Client-side interfaces: UTMPassthrough component reads window.location.search URLSearchParams at DOMContentLoaded. GA4 gtag event call: gtag('event', 'app_store_click', {platform: 'ios', utm_source: params.get('utm_source'), utm_campaign: params.get('utm_campaign')}). Netlify deploy hook: POST triggered by GitHub Actions on CI pass.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Netlify with automatic TLS. Security headers in netlify.toml: X-Frame-Options DENY, Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin, Content-Security-Policy allowing self and GA4 measurement domain. No user input, no forms, no cookies beyond GA4 session. GA4 IP anonymization enabled. No consent banner required for current audience scope.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: npm ci, astro build, html-validate /dist. On pass, Netlify auto-deploys via GitHub integration. Main branch deploys to production at custom domain. Every PR generates Netlify preview URL. Rollback via Netlify dashboard one-click restore to prior atomic deploy. No infrastructure to manage beyond Netlify site settings. Branch protection requires passing CI before merge. Netlify build cache keyed on node_modules hash reduces cold build from 90s to under 30s.
target_output.observability: GA4 dashboard tracks app_store_click events segmented by platform (ios/android), utm_source, utm_campaign. Netlify Analytics provides page view counts without cookies. Funnel: page_view to app_store_click conversion rate per campaign source reviewed weekly by marketing team.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Netlify free tier covers hosting for expected traffic volumes. GA4 free. GitHub Actions free-tier minutes sufficient. Netlify CDN edge caching handles traffic spikes from paid social bursts without origin scaling.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Week 1): Astro project setup, Tailwind theme matching brand colors, hero section with device mockup. Phase 2 (Week 2): Feature highlights, testimonials, UTMPassthrough island, GA4 event integration. Phase 3 (Week 3): Performance tuning (LCP audit, WebP optimization), Netlify security headers, CI pipeline, launch.
target_output.development_guidelines: All app screenshot images must include descriptive alt text for accessibility. UTMPassthrough island must degrade gracefully when URLSearchParams is absent (no UTM params). Tailwind content config must include all Astro template paths. All new testimonials added to JSON data file, not hardcoded in Astro templates.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: App Store Connect and Google Play have different UTM parameter formats for attribution; Mitigation: iOS uses pt/ct params (Apple Search Ads format) while Android uses standard UTM params on Play Store URL. Tradeoff: client:load island adds minimal JS to otherwise zero-JS page; accepted because UTM passthrough is core business requirement. Risk: Netlify free tier bandwidth exhaustion during major campaign push. Mitigation: Netlify Pro upgrade budgeted for launch campaign period.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Astro chosen over plain HTML for maintainability and component reuse. GA4 chosen over Mixpanel for simplicity given single-page tracking needs. No Netlify Forms needed as no lead capture is in scope for this project.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for peer support wellness app landing page; resolves UTM passthrough gap; weak plan quality per manifest with appropriately thin sections for simple project.

profile.domain: Clinical Research Cohort Analysis CLI Tool

project_goal.value: Command-line tool for clinical researchers to perform cohort analysis on de-identified mental health registry datasets, computing population-level statistics, treatment response rates, and dropout patterns across thousands of patient records from CSV exports
project_goal.rationale: Researchers need reproducible, scriptable analysis on registry CSV exports without requiring a database server

target_users.value: Clinical researchers and biostatisticians analyzing de-identified mental health registry data under IRB approval
target_users.rationale: Primary users are researchers running reproducible cohort analyses from terminal environments

project_class.rationale: Local command-line application; no hosted infrastructure required

capabilities.rationale: Python CLI with Polars data processing; no frontend or auth capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Cohort filtering, statistical computation, and data quality validation logic; moderate algorithmic complexity

risk_level.rationale: De-identified data but HIPAA Safe Harbor compliance required; analysis output could inadvertently re-identify small cohorts

data_sensitivity.rationale: Mental health registry data even when de-identified per HIPAA Safe Harbor contains sensitive behavioral health information

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs on researcher workstations; no network exposure; distribution via conda/pip

access_model.value: Local execution only; no authentication layer; assumes IRB-approved researcher controls access to input CSV files
access_model.rationale: Access control is at filesystem and IRB protocol level, not application level

feature_scope.value: Cohort definition via YAML specification files, Polars-based CSV ingestion with lazy evaluation, data quality validation pre-analysis, population statistics (N, mean, SD, median, IQR), treatment response rate computation, dropout event analysis, structured report output in CSV and JSON
feature_scope.rationale: Complete cohort analysis workflow for mental health registry research

mvp_scope.value: cohort filter command, stats command, quality-check command, report output to CSV and JSON
mvp_scope.rationale: Core analysis commands covering the majority of researcher workflows

security_baseline.value: No network connectivity; data never leaves researcher workstation; output reports include minimum N threshold to prevent small-cell re-identification
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification requires suppressing statistics from cohorts smaller than minimum cell size

frontend_stack.value: React 18 dashboard with TypeScript and Recharts for interactive result visualization
frontend_stack.rationale: Researchers want interactive charts for exploring cohort analysis results in a browser UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click 8 for CLI framework, Polars 0.20 for DataFrame processing, PyYAML for cohort definition parsing
backend_stack.rationale: Polars lazy evaluation handles large CSV datasets memory-efficiently; Click provides clean command structure

data_platform.value: In-memory Polars LazyFrame processing of CSV files; DuckDB for optional persistent query cache
data_platform.rationale: No server database required; Polars lazy evaluation and optional DuckDB cache for repeated analysis on large registries

hosting_target.value: Local execution on researcher workstations; distributed via PyPI and conda-forge
hosting_target.rationale: Researchers install via pip or conda in their existing Python environments

privacy_retention_policy.value: Input CSV files not copied or cached by the tool; analysis outputs contain aggregate statistics only; output suppressed for cohorts below N=11 per HIPAA Safe Harbor cell suppression
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification requires suppressing small-cell statistics to prevent re-identification

future_scope.value: Survival analysis commands, mixed-effects model integration via statsmodels, R interoperability via feather file export
future_scope.rationale: Advanced statistical methods for future research use cases

constraints.value: Must run on researcher workstations with 16GB RAM; analysis of 100,000-record registry must complete within 60 seconds; no external network calls during analysis
constraints.rationale: Workstation memory constraint; researcher productivity requirement; data governance prohibits network egress of registry data

observability_baseline.value: Structured logging to cohort_analysis.log in working directory; verbose flag for debug output to stderr; no telemetry or remote logging
observability_baseline.rationale: Local log file for researcher reproducibility audit trail; no remote telemetry permitted under data governance policy

execution_preference.value: CLI-first design with YAML-driven cohort specifications for reproducibility; pytest with hypothesis for property-based testing
execution_preference.rationale: YAML cohort specs enable version-controlled, reproducible analysis workflows; property-based testing validates statistical computation edge cases

compliance_context.value: HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification standards for secondary use of registry data; IRB protocol requirements for data handling; institutional data governance policy prohibiting network egress
compliance_context.rationale: Research use of de-identified mental health registry data requires compliance with Safe Harbor method and IRB data handling requirements

project.goal: CLI tool for reproducible cohort analysis on de-identified mental health registry CSV exports
project.target_users: Clinical researchers and biostatisticians under IRB approval
project.timeline: 8-week build with researcher pilot in week 9

backend.framework: Click 8 command group with subcommands; Polars 0.20 for data processing

security.data_handling: No network connectivity; no caching of input files; N<11 cell suppression in outputs

data.processing: Polars LazyFrame for memory-efficient CSV scanning
data.optional_cache: DuckDB for persistent query results on large registries

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with pytest and hypothesis property tests
devops.environments: researcher workstations with Python 3.12

reasoner_reviews.product: The core value proposition is reproducibility: YAML cohort definitions checked into version control alongside analysis scripts let researchers rerun identical analyses on updated registry exports. The data quality check command is critical — garbage-in-garbage-out is a major reliability risk for registry data. Dropout pattern analysis is the most analytically complex feature and likely requires event-based representation in the data model.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Click command group with subcommands (cohort, stats, quality-check, report) is the right structure. Polars lazy evaluation via scan_csv avoids loading full registry into memory before filtering. The cohort YAML specification should support inclusion/exclusion criteria as field predicates so researchers can compose complex cohort definitions without writing Python. DuckDB optional cache is a good pattern for researchers running repeated analyses on stable registry snapshots.
reasoner_reviews.security: The N<11 suppression rule must be enforced programmatically in the stats output writer — not left to researcher discretion. Any cohort with fewer than 11 members must produce a suppressed marker in output, not the actual statistic. The tool must make no network calls; any dependency that phones home (telemetry, license checks) must be evaluated and disabled. Output JSON should include a de_identification_method field documenting that HIPAA Safe Harbor standards were applied.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Polars scan_csv with lazy evaluation should handle 100K records comfortably within 60 seconds on 16GB workstations. The cohort filter predicate compilation from YAML to Polars expressions is the key performance concern — complex nested predicates may require predicate pushdown validation. DuckDB cache writes should be optional (flag-gated) to avoid unexpected disk writes in restrictive research computing environments.

backend.service_design: Click application group mhc (mental health cohort): subcommands quality-check, filter, stats, report. Each subcommand accepts --input CSV path, --cohort YAML path, --output directory path, --min-cell-size INT (default 11)
backend.business_modules: cohort_filter: parses YAML cohort_definitions into Polars filter expressions using Polars expr API; applies inclusion_criteria and exclusion_criteria predicates to patient_records LazyFrame. quality_checker: validates required fields (patient_id, treatment_start_date, outcome_measure), flags implausible values (negative treatment durations, out-of-range outcome scores), writes data_quality_report.json. stats_computer: computes population N, mean/SD/median/IQR for outcome_measures, treatment response rate (proportion with outcome_measure >= threshold), dropout_rate (proportion with dropout_events where reason != completed). cell_suppressor: replaces any statistic computed on cohort N < min_cell_size with SUPPRESSED marker.
backend.failure_handling: Click exit codes: 0 success, 1 input file not found, 2 YAML parse error, 3 data quality check failed (blocking mode), 4 cell suppression triggered on all output cells. Structured log entries for each failure with row counts and field names; never log cell values.

security.auth_design: No authentication required; local execution with filesystem-level access control
security.privacy_controls: Cell suppression enforced in stats_computer module for all output statistics where cohort N < min_cell_size (default 11 per HIPAA Safe Harbor); output JSON includes hipaa_safe_harbor_applied: true field
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Structured log to cohort_analysis.log: analysis_run_id (UUID), command, input_file_hash (SHA256), cohort_yaml_hash, records_loaded, records_after_filter, cell_suppression_count, completed_at. No PHI in log entries.

data.entities: users (id, name, email, created_at), orders (id, user_id, amount, status, created_at), products (id, name, category, price)
data.storage_design: Polars scan_csv for lazy input reading; optional DuckDB file cache at .mhc_cache/registry_{input_hash}.duckdb
data.retention_and_deletion: Tool does not copy or retain input CSV data; analysis output files written to researcher-specified output directory under researcher control

devops.deployment_topology: Python package distributed via PyPI (pip install mental-health-cohort) and conda-forge feedstock; no server deployment
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest unit tests, hypothesis property tests for stats_computer edge cases (N=0, N=10, all dropout cohort), mypy type check, build wheel and sdist; publish to PyPI on tag push
devops.observability_stack: Local log file only; no remote telemetry; researcher runs mhc --version to confirm installed version

target_output.thinking_summary: Building a CLI tool for cohort analysis on de-identified mental health registry data. Key decisions: Click command group with cohort/stats/quality-check subcommands; Polars lazy evaluation for memory-efficient CSV processing within 16GB workstation constraint; YAML-driven cohort definitions for reproducibility. HIPAA Safe Harbor cell suppression must be enforced programmatically. The plan includes a React dashboard component which is inconsistent with the cli_tool project class — and the data_model uses generic entities instead of domain-specific ones — these are deliberate flaws per the training data quality specification.
target_output.title: Mental Health Registry Cohort Analysis CLI Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Python CLI tool (mental-health-cohort) for clinical researchers to perform reproducible cohort analysis on de-identified mental health registry CSV exports. Researchers define cohorts via YAML specification files, run data quality checks, compute population statistics and treatment response rates, and export structured reports. Distributed via PyPI and conda-forge for researcher workstation installation. Includes a React web dashboard for interactive result visualization served locally.
target_output.architecture_overview: Local CLI application built with Click 8 command groups. Polars 0.20 lazy evaluation (scan_csv) handles large registry CSV files without full in-memory loading. A YAML cohort definition schema specifies inclusion and exclusion predicates compiled to Polars filter expressions at runtime. Four primary subcommands: mhc quality-check validates input data before analysis; mhc filter applies cohort criteria; mhc stats computes population statistics with cell suppression; mhc report exports structured output. Results are output to stdout as plain text tables or optionally as structured JSON with the --format json flag, consumable by downstream shell pipelines or Jupyter notebooks without any browser UI dependency. Distributed as a standard Python package.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Click 8 command group framework, Polars 0.20 with lazy evaluation for CSV processing, PyYAML for cohort definition parsing, DuckDB for optional analysis result caching. React 18 with TypeScript and Recharts for local visualization dashboard. Flask 3 as local API server for React dashboard. pytest with hypothesis for property-based testing. PyPI and conda-forge for distribution. GitHub Actions for CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: mhc quality-check --input registry.csv: validates required fields, flags implausible values (negative treatment durations, out-of-range PHQ-9 scores), writes data_quality_report.json with per-field issue counts. mhc filter --input registry.csv --cohort definition.yaml: applies inclusion/exclusion criteria, outputs filtered_cohort.csv. mhc stats --input filtered_cohort.csv --cohort definition.yaml: computes N, mean/SD/median/IQR for outcome_measures, treatment_response_rate, dropout_rate, with N<11 cell suppression. mhc report --stats stats.json --output ./results: generates summary_report.csv and summary_report.json. mhc serve --results ./results: launches React dashboard at localhost:5173.
target_output.system_components: Click application entry point mhc with command group. CohortParser module: reads cohort_definitions YAML, validates schema, compiles inclusion_criteria and exclusion_criteria to Polars LazyFrame filter expressions. DataQualityChecker: validates patient_records fields against expected types and value ranges for mental health registry data. StatsComputer: computes population statistics on outcome_measures and treatment_episodes with CellSuppressor enforcing N>=11 threshold. ReportWriter: serializes results to CSV and JSON with hipaa_safe_harbor_applied metadata field. React 18 dashboard SPA with Recharts components. Flask /api/results endpoint reading JSON report files. Web browser UI for interactive chart exploration — inconsistent with CLI tool project class.
target_output.workflows: Cohort Analysis Workflow: researcher runs mhc quality-check --input registry_2026.csv; reviews data_quality_report.json for missing fields or implausible outcome scores; runs mhc filter --input registry_2026.csv --cohort depression_followup.yaml producing filtered_cohort.csv with N records meeting inclusion criteria; runs mhc stats --input filtered_cohort.csv --cohort depression_followup.yaml; StatsComputer computes treatment_response_rate as proportion of treatment_episodes where outcome_measures.phq9_final <= 5, dropout_rate as proportion of dropout_events where reason_code != completed; any statistic from a sub-cohort with N<11 replaced with SUPPRESSED in output JSON. Interactive Dashboard Workflow: researcher runs mhc serve --results ./results/; Flask server starts on localhost:5173; React SPA loads summary_report.json via /api/results; Recharts BarChart renders treatment response rates by diagnosis_category; researcher explores dropout timeline in LineChart.
target_output.data_model: users (id INTEGER PK, name VARCHAR, email VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP). orders (id INTEGER PK, user_id INTEGER FK, amount DECIMAL, status VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP). products (id INTEGER PK, name VARCHAR, category VARCHAR, price DECIMAL). cohort_definitions: YAML schema with fields inclusion_criteria (list of field predicates), exclusion_criteria, outcome_measure_field, treatment_start_field, dropout_event_field. analysis_reports (report_id UUID, cohort_yaml_hash, input_file_hash, population_n, response_rate, dropout_rate, cell_suppression_count, generated_at).
target_output.api_design: mhc quality-check --input PATH [--output PATH] [--strict]: exits 0 on pass, 3 on blocking quality failures. mhc filter --input PATH --cohort PATH --output PATH: writes filtered CSV, logs N records in and N records out. mhc stats --input PATH --cohort PATH [--min-cell-size INT] --output PATH: writes stats JSON with SUPPRESSED markers. mhc report --stats PATH --output DIR. mhc serve --results DIR [--port INT]: starts Flask API server for React dashboard. Flask endpoints: GET /api/results returns summary_report.json contents; GET /api/health.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA Safe Harbor compliance: CellSuppressor module enforces minimum cohort N of 11 (configurable via --min-cell-size) before writing any statistic; suppressed cells output literal string SUPPRESSED in CSV and null with suppressed: true flag in JSON. No network calls during analysis execution; all Polars and DuckDB operations are local. Output JSON includes de_identification_method: 'HIPAA Safe Harbor' and hipaa_safe_harbor_applied: true. Structured log captures input file SHA256 hash, cohort YAML hash, and cell_suppression_count for IRB audit trail without logging any patient field values.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Package published to PyPI as mental-health-cohort; conda-forge feedstock for conda install. GitHub Actions CI: pytest, hypothesis property tests for stats_computer (N=0 edge case, N=10 below threshold, all-dropout cohort), mypy strict type checking, build wheel and sdist; publish job on git tag push. Researcher installs via pip install mental-health-cohort or conda install -c conda-forge mental-health-cohort. No server deployment or infrastructure management required.
target_output.observability: Structured JSON log to cohort_analysis.log in working directory: analysis_run_id, command, input_file_hash (SHA256), cohort_yaml_hash (SHA256), records_loaded, records_after_filter, suppression_count, duration_seconds, completed_at. --verbose flag streams progress to stderr (scan progress, filter hit rate, suppression events). Log file enables researcher to reproduce analysis audit trail for IRB reporting.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost; runs on researcher workstations. Polars lazy evaluation with scan_csv handles 100K-record registries within 60 seconds on 16GB RAM. DuckDB cache reduces repeated analysis time on stable registry snapshots from 45 seconds to under 5 seconds. Package distribution via PyPI and conda-forge has no per-user cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Click command group skeleton, CohortParser YAML-to-Polars compiler, DataQualityChecker for mental health registry field validation, unit tests. Phase 2 (Weeks 4-6): StatsComputer with treatment response rate and dropout pattern computation, CellSuppressor with N<11 enforcement, ReportWriter with CSV and JSON output, hypothesis property tests. Phase 3 (Weeks 7-8): DuckDB optional cache, React dashboard with Recharts, Flask API server, PyPI and conda-forge packaging, researcher pilot with two IRB-approved registry datasets.
target_output.development_guidelines: All statistical computation functions must have pytest unit tests with known-input/known-output fixtures from synthetic registry datasets. CellSuppressor is the highest-risk module: 100% branch coverage required including N=0, N=10, N=11, N=12 boundary cases. No hardcoded field names in CLI commands; all registry field mappings come from cohort YAML configuration. mypy strict mode enforced in CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Polars YAML-to-expression compiler fails on complex nested cohort predicates causing silent incorrect filtering. Mitigation: compiler emits filter expression as string in log and report metadata for researcher validation. Risk: React dashboard Flask server left running exposes local JSON report files on network interface. Mitigation: Flask server binds to 127.0.0.1 only; never 0.0.0.0. Tradeoff: DuckDB cache creates persistent files on researcher workstation that may be subject to data governance policies; Mitigation: cache disabled by default, opt-in via --cache flag.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Click chosen over argparse for subcommand structure and automatic --help generation. Polars chosen over pandas for memory efficiency and lazy evaluation on large registry files. YAML chosen over JSON for cohort definitions for human readability and comment support.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: frontend_stack contract specifies React dashboard inconsistent with cli_tool project class; FrontendAgent subplan included despite cli_tool (should not run); data_model uses generic users/orders/products instead of cohort_definitions/patient_records/treatment_episodes domain entities.

profile.domain: Community Mental Health Clinic Outreach Automation

project_goal.value: Replace manual phone-call reminder workflows with configurable multi-channel messaging (SMS, email, voice) for appointment reminders and care gap outreach, integrated with the clinic scheduling system
project_goal.rationale: Manual outreach is staff-intensive and generates high no-show rates; automation reduces administrative burden and improves patient adherence

target_users.value: Clinic operations staff who configure campaigns and patients who receive outreach messages
target_users.rationale: Staff need admin controls; patients are message recipients not interactive users

project_class.rationale: No user-facing UI; pure backend automation with scheduling system integration and messaging dispatch

capabilities.rationale: Celery batch jobs, scheduling API integration, Twilio messaging, PostgreSQL persistence

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-channel dispatch with consent tracking and retry logic; moderate data volume

risk_level.rationale: PHI transmitted in message content; HIPAA and TCPA compliance mandatory

data_sensitivity.rationale: Patient names, appointment details, and care gap information qualify as PHI

external_exposure.rationale: System runs as internal service; Twilio webhooks are the only external ingress

access_model.value: AWS IAM for service-to-service; no end-user login interface
access_model.rationale: Automation tool with no human-facing portal; IAM roles scope ECS task permissions

feature_scope.value: Appointment reminder campaigns, care gap outreach sequences, multi-channel delivery (SMS/email/voice), delivery log tracking, consent record management, no-show rate reporting
feature_scope.rationale: Full outreach lifecycle from scheduling to delivery confirmation and analytics

mvp_scope.value: SMS and email appointment reminders 48h and 2h before appointment, delivery status tracking, consent opt-out handling
mvp_scope.rationale: Core reminder use case; voice and care gap logic are phase 2

security_baseline.value: PHI in transit via TLS 1.3; message content not logged beyond delivery status; Twilio HIPAA-eligible account with BAA; AES-256 at rest in RDS
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA minimum controls for transmitted PHI; TCPA consent records retained per legal requirement

frontend_stack.value: none
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool with no user-facing UI; operational via AWS console and CloudWatch dashboards

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Celery and Redis 7 as broker
backend_stack.rationale: Celery beat for scheduled job triggering; Celery workers for parallel message dispatch

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for appointment, patient, consent, and delivery log records; managed HA

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate (beat scheduler + worker pool)
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with auto-scaling worker pool; no persistent server management

privacy_retention_policy.value: Delivery logs retained 7 years per healthcare records law; message content not stored; consent records retained for duration of patient relationship plus 6 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA retention requirements; TCPA consent records needed for litigation defense

future_scope.value: Voice call reminders via Twilio Programmable Voice, bidirectional SMS for patient confirmations, integration with additional EHR scheduling systems

constraints.value: Must integrate with existing Athena Health scheduling API; Twilio HIPAA-eligible account required; deployment within existing AWS us-east-1 VPC
constraints.rationale: EHR vendor lock-in for scheduling data; existing cloud infrastructure

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch metrics for queue depth, delivery success rates, and worker task latency; PagerDuty alerts for delivery failure spikes
observability_baseline.rationale: Operational visibility into batch job health and outreach campaign performance

execution_preference.value: Iterative sprints with phased rollout by message channel
execution_preference.rationale: SMS first, email second, voice third minimizes integration risk

compliance_context.value: HIPAA for PHI in outreach messages; TCPA for SMS consent and opt-out; BAA with Twilio and AWS; state healthcare communication regulations vary
compliance_context.rationale: Health data sensitivity and SMS marketing law require dual compliance framework

project.goal: Automate appointment reminders and care gap outreach for community mental health clinic
project.target_users: Clinic ops staff (configuration), patients (message recipients)

backend.api: Internal scheduler polling Athena Health API

security.transport: TLS 1.3; Twilio HIPAA-eligible account with BAA

devops.environments: staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: Multi-channel outreach replaces error-prone manual phone workflows. Care gap logic requires precise protocol configuration per condition type. No-show rate reduction is the primary success metric and must be tracked from day one.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Celery beat for scheduling and worker pool for parallel dispatch is well-suited here. ISS-002 around idempotency on the outreach campaign trigger must be resolved; duplicate message delivery is a compliance and patient trust risk. Recommend idempotent task IDs keyed on appointment_id and campaign_id.
reasoner_reviews.security: TCPA opt-out processing must be synchronous and reflected in PostgreSQL before any queued messages for that patient are dispatched. Twilio webhook signature validation is mandatory to prevent spoofed delivery status callbacks. ISS-001 around opt-out race condition remains the top priority.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Athena Health API rate limits (300 req/min) require request batching in the nightly scheduler job. Redis as both broker and deduplication store is acceptable for this scale.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan improved delivery retry logic but did not resolve the opt-out race condition (ISS-001) or address idempotent campaign triggering (ISS-002). Both must be closed in this round before the plan can pass audit.

backend.service_design: Two ECS Fargate services: celery-beat (single-instance scheduler) and celery-worker (auto-scaling pool, 2-10 tasks). Beat triggers nightly appointment query job and campaign dispatch jobs.
backend.api_patterns: Internal polling of Athena Health REST API with OAuth 2.0 client credentials; Twilio REST API for SMS/email dispatch; Twilio webhook receiver for delivery status callbacks.
backend.background_jobs: NightlyReminderJob: queries appointments 48h and 24h out, enqueues SendReminderTask per patient per channel. CareGapJob: queries overdue patients per protocol, enqueues CareGapOutreachTask. DeliveryStatusJob: reconciles Twilio delivery receipts against delivery_logs.
backend.failure_handling: Celery retry with exponential backoff (max 3 retries, 15/60/300s delays) for transient Twilio API failures. Dead-letter queue for permanently failed tasks with CloudWatch alarm. Idempotency key (appointment_id + channel + campaign_round) prevents duplicate sends on worker crash/restart.

security.auth_design: ECS task IAM roles with least-privilege policies for RDS, SSM Parameter Store (Twilio credentials), and CloudWatch. No human-facing auth interface.
security.secrets_management: Twilio Account SID and Auth Token in AWS SSM Parameter Store encrypted with KMS; rotated quarterly; never in environment variables or logs.
security.abuse_prevention: Twilio webhook signature validation on all inbound status callbacks using HMAC-SHA1. Rate limiting on nightly job to respect Athena Health API quotas. Opt-out records checked synchronously before every task dispatch.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: delivery_logs table records message_id, patient_id, channel, status, timestamp, twilio_sid — no message body stored. CloudTrail for AWS API actions. Opt-out events logged with timestamp and source channel.

data.entities: appointments (id, patient_id, scheduled_at, appointment_type, provider_id, status), patients (id, mrn, preferred_channel, opt_out_sms, opt_out_email, opt_out_voice, updated_at), outreach_campaigns (id, campaign_type ENUM reminder/care_gap, template_id, scheduled_send_at, status), delivery_logs (id, campaign_id, patient_id, channel, twilio_message_sid, status ENUM queued/sent/delivered/failed/opted_out, attempted_at, delivered_at), message_templates (id, channel, body_template, variables_json), care_gaps (id, patient_id, protocol_id, due_date, last_contact_at, status).
data.retention_and_deletion: delivery_logs retained 7 years in RDS; care_gaps and campaign records retained 3 years; message body templates contain no PHI; patient consent records retained per TCPA requirements.
data.consistency_notes: Opt-out status updates use SELECT FOR UPDATE on patients row before task dispatch to prevent race condition between opt-out processing and queued task execution.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster with two services: beat-scheduler (1 task, reserved capacity) and worker-pool (2-10 tasks, target-tracking scaling on SQS visible messages approximation via CloudWatch custom metric).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff), type check (mypy), unit tests (pytest, 85% coverage gate), Docker build, push to ECR, deploy via ECS rolling update. Separate workflow for staging vs production promotion.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch custom metrics: QueueDepth (Celery), DeliverySuccessRate per channel, CareGapOutreachRate. CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON (no PHI). PagerDuty alert if DeliverySuccessRate <90% over 30 minutes.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS service rollback to previous task definition revision via AWS CLI in GitHub Actions on failed health check. Beat scheduler uses ECS service stable state check before promoting workers.

ISS-001.title: TCPA opt-out race condition allows messages to dispatch after opt-out recorded
ISS-001.detail: In round 1 the nightly job enqueued all SendReminderTasks before checking opt-out status; a patient opting out during the dispatch window received messages. Round 2 added opt-out check in task body but did not use SELECT FOR UPDATE, leaving a narrow race window.

history.note: Opt-out check missing from enqueue logic
Check added in task body but without row lock; race condition remains

ISS-002.title: Campaign trigger lacks idempotency; duplicate messages sent on worker restart
ISS-002.detail: Worker crash during nightly job caused tasks to be re-enqueued on restart without deduplication, sending duplicate reminders to some patients. No idempotency key scheme was specified in rounds 1 or 2.

history.note: No idempotency mechanism present
Retry backoff added but idempotency key not implemented

ISS-003.title: Twilio webhook callbacks not signature-validated; spoofed status updates possible
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 added Twilio signature validation middleware using HMAC-SHA1 and the Twilio Auth Token from SSM. Resolved.

history.note: No signature validation on webhook receiver
HMAC-SHA1 validation added

focus_issues.title: TCPA opt-out race condition allows messages to dispatch after opt-out recorded
focus_issues.detail: SELECT FOR UPDATE not used; race window between opt-out write and queued task execution remains.

focus_issues.title: Campaign trigger lacks idempotency; duplicate messages sent on worker restart
focus_issues.detail: No idempotency key keyed on (appointment_id, channel, campaign_round) to prevent duplicate dispatch on Celery worker restart.

latest_recommendations: Add SELECT FOR UPDATE on patients row before any task dispatches message to close TCPA opt-out race
Implement idempotency key (appointment_id + channel + campaign_round) stored in Redis with TTL to deduplicate Celery task re-enqueue on crash

recommendations: Apply SELECT FOR UPDATE on patients table in SendReminderTask before checking opt_out flags
Define idempotency key as SHA256(appointment_id + channel + campaign_round), store in Redis with 48h TTL, skip task if key already present

issue_updates.title: TCPA opt-out race condition

previous_plan.title: Mental Health Clinic Multi-Channel Outreach Automation v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Celery-based automation replacing manual phone outreach with SMS and email reminders for appointment scheduling and care gap follow-up, integrated with Athena Health API and Twilio.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate runs a celery-beat scheduler and auto-scaling worker pool against PostgreSQL 16. Twilio HIPAA-eligible account handles SMS and email dispatch. Delivery status is updated via webhook callbacks.
previous_plan.data_model: appointments, patients (with opt_out flags), outreach_campaigns, delivery_logs (no message body), message_templates, care_gaps.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Twilio BAA in place; webhook HMAC-SHA1 validation added round 2; opt-out check in task body but without row lock — ISS-001 open.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 focus: close ISS-001 by applying SELECT FOR UPDATE on the patients row inside SendReminderTask before reading opt-out flags, ensuring no concurrent opt-out write can race with dispatch. Close ISS-002 by implementing idempotency keys as SHA256(appointment_id+channel+campaign_round) stored in Redis with 48h TTL; tasks that find the key present skip silently and log a deduplicated event. These two fixes directly address the critical TCPA and reliability gaps from round 2.

fix_report.action_taken: SendReminderTask now opens a PostgreSQL transaction and issues SELECT FOR UPDATE on the patients row before evaluating opt_out_sms / opt_out_email flags. If opt-out is set, the task records a 'opted_out' status in delivery_logs and commits without sending. This eliminates the race window between concurrent opt-out writes and queued task execution.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero TCPA violations from post-opt-out message delivery; delivery_logs accurately reflect opted_out status for auditing

fix_report.action_taken: Idempotency key defined as SHA256 hex of concatenated appointment_id + channel + campaign_round, stored in Redis with 48-hour TTL at task start. If key already exists in Redis, task logs a 'deduplicated' event and exits immediately. Beat scheduler also checks for existing keys before enqueuing tasks during nightly job.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Celery worker crashes and restarts produce zero duplicate message deliveries; idempotency is verifiable via Redis key TTL inspection

target_output.title: Mental Health Clinic Multi-Channel Outreach Automation
target_output.executive_summary: A HIPAA- and TCPA-compliant outreach automation system for a community mental health clinic, replacing manual phone-call reminder workflows with configurable SMS and email campaigns driven by Celery scheduled jobs integrated with the Athena Health scheduling API. The system dispatches appointment reminders 48h and 2h before scheduled visits, tracks multi-channel delivery status via Twilio webhooks, manages patient opt-out consent with row-level locking to prevent TCPA violations, and reports care gap outreach metrics via CloudWatch dashboards.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two ECS Fargate services run on a shared task cluster: a single-instance celery-beat scheduler that triggers nightly appointment queries against the Athena Health REST API, and an auto-scaling celery-worker pool (2-10 tasks) that executes per-patient message dispatch via Twilio. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ persists appointment records, patient consent flags, delivery logs, and care gap state. Redis 7 serves as both the Celery task broker and idempotency key store. Twilio HIPAA-eligible account handles SMS and email delivery; inbound delivery status webhooks are validated via HMAC-SHA1 before updating delivery_logs. No PHI is stored in message bodies or logs — only delivery metadata.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, Celery 5.3, Redis 7 (broker + idempotency store). Scheduling integration: Athena Health REST API with OAuth 2.0 client credentials. Messaging: Twilio Programmable Messaging (HIPAA-eligible, BAA signed). Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. Infrastructure: AWS ECS Fargate, ECR, SSM Parameter Store (KMS-encrypted secrets), CloudWatch, PagerDuty. CI/CD: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Appointment Reminder Campaign: configurable 48h and 2h pre-appointment SMS and email reminders generated from message_templates per appointment_type. Care Gap Outreach: protocol-driven identification of patients overdue for follow-up appointments with configurable outreach sequence (up to 3 attempts). Delivery Tracking: Twilio webhook callbacks update delivery_logs with delivered/failed/bounced status. Opt-Out Management: patient-controlled opt-out per channel stored in patients table, enforced with SELECT FOR UPDATE before every dispatch. No-Show Reporting: CloudWatch dashboard showing reminder delivery rate, no-show rate by appointment_type, and care gap closure rate.
target_output.system_components: celery-beat scheduler (ECS Fargate, 1 task, reserved): triggers NightlyReminderJob and CareGapJob on cron schedule. celery-worker pool (ECS Fargate, 2-10 tasks, auto-scaling on CloudWatch custom metric for queue depth): executes SendReminderTask and CareGapOutreachTask. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ: persistent store for appointments, patients, delivery_logs, care_gaps, message_templates. Redis 7 ElastiCache: Celery broker and idempotency key store (48h TTL per key). Twilio HIPAA account: SMS/email dispatch and delivery status webhooks. AWS SSM Parameter Store: encrypted Twilio credentials and Athena Health API keys. CloudWatch + PagerDuty: operational observability and alerting.
target_output.workflows: Nightly Reminder Dispatch: celery-beat fires NightlyReminderJob at 06:00 UTC; job queries Athena Health API for appointments 48h and 2h ahead (batched at 250 req/batch to respect 300/min rate limit); for each appointment, computes idempotency key SHA256(appointment_id+channel+campaign_round), checks Redis — skips if key exists; enqueues SendReminderTask with key stored to Redis (48h TTL). SendReminderTask: opens PostgreSQL transaction, issues SELECT FOR UPDATE on patients row, reads opt_out_sms/opt_out_email flags; if opted out, writes delivery_logs status='opted_out' and commits; otherwise renders message_template, calls Twilio API, writes delivery_logs status='queued', commits. Delivery Status Update: Twilio POSTs delivery status to webhook endpoint; receiver validates HMAC-SHA1 signature, updates delivery_logs.status to delivered/failed, publishes CloudWatch metric. Care Gap Outreach: CareGapJob queries care_gaps where due_date < today and last_contact_at < 14 days ago; enqueues CareGapOutreachTask per patient per preferred_channel with idempotency key; task follows same opt-out SELECT FOR UPDATE flow before dispatch.
target_output.data_model: appointments (id UUID PK, patient_id FK, scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, appointment_type VARCHAR, provider_id FK, status ENUM scheduled/cancelled/completed, athena_appointment_id VARCHAR UNIQUE). patients (id UUID PK, mrn VARCHAR UNIQUE, preferred_channel ENUM sms/email/voice, opt_out_sms BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, opt_out_email BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, opt_out_voice BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, opt_out_updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). outreach_campaigns (id UUID PK, campaign_type ENUM reminder/care_gap, template_id FK, trigger_condition JSONB, status ENUM active/paused). delivery_logs (id UUID PK, campaign_id FK, patient_id FK, appointment_id FK NULLABLE, channel ENUM sms/email/voice, twilio_message_sid VARCHAR, status ENUM queued/sent/delivered/failed/opted_out/deduplicated, attempted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE). message_templates (id UUID PK, channel ENUM, body_template TEXT, variables_json JSONB). care_gaps (id UUID PK, patient_id FK, protocol_id FK, due_date DATE, last_contact_at TIMESTAMPTZ, outreach_attempt_count INT DEFAULT 0, status ENUM open/closed).
target_output.api_design: Internal service; no public REST API. Athena Health integration: GET /appointments with date range params (OAuth 2.0 bearer token), paginated at 250 records. Twilio outbound: POST https://api.twilio.com/2010-04-01/Accounts/{SID}/Messages with To, From, Body (no PHI in Body beyond appointment date/time). Twilio inbound webhook: POST /webhooks/twilio/status — validates X-Twilio-Signature HMAC-SHA1, updates delivery_logs. Health check endpoint: GET /health returns worker count, queue depth, last job run timestamp for monitoring.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA controls: RDS AES-256 encryption at rest with KMS CMK; TLS 1.3 on all Twilio API calls and webhook receiver; Twilio HIPAA-eligible Messaging account with signed BAA; message body templates contain only appointment date/time and provider name (minimum necessary PHI). TCPA compliance: patients table stores opt_out_sms/opt_out_email/opt_out_voice with opt_out_updated_at; SendReminderTask issues SELECT FOR UPDATE on patients row within a transaction before reading opt-out flags, preventing concurrent opt-out writes from racing with dispatch; opted-out patients receive status='opted_out' in delivery_logs for audit trail. Secrets: Twilio Account SID, Auth Token, and Athena Health client credentials stored in SSM Parameter Store encrypted with KMS CMK; ECS task IAM role scoped to ssm:GetParameter for specific parameter paths only. Webhook security: HMAC-SHA1 validation using Twilio Auth Token on all inbound status callbacks; invalid signatures return HTTP 403.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1 with two services: (1) beat-scheduler — 1 task, reserved capacity, restarts on failure, no scaling; (2) worker-pool — 2 tasks min, 10 tasks max, target-tracking on custom CloudWatch metric CeleryQueueDepth (target=20 messages). GitHub Actions CI/CD: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest with 85% coverage gate, Docker build and push to ECR, ECS rolling update (beat first, workers second). Staging environment mirrors production with a Twilio test credential set; staging messages delivered to a test number pool only. Secrets rotation: SSM parameters rotated quarterly via GitHub Actions rotation workflow with Secrets Manager integration. RDS Multi-AZ with automated daily snapshots, 35-day retention. Redis ElastiCache with 1 replica for broker HA.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch custom metrics published by workers: CeleryQueueDepth (visible messages), DeliverySuccessRate per channel (rolling 1h), CareGapOutreachAttempts, DuplicatesDeduplicatedCount. CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON — patient_id hashed (SHA256) in log fields, no message body content. PagerDuty escalation triggers: DeliverySuccessRate <90% for 30 minutes; CeleryQueueDepth >500 for 15 minutes; beat scheduler task last heartbeat >2h ago. Daily no-show rate metric published to CloudWatch from reconciliation job comparing appointment status post-visit to reminder delivery status.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $320/month at 800 daily appointments: ECS Fargate beat+workers ~$90/month, RDS db.t3.medium Multi-AZ ~$140/month, ElastiCache cache.t3.micro ~$30/month, Twilio SMS ~$0.0079/message (~$50/month at 6400 messages/day), CloudWatch ~$10/month. Worker auto-scaling absorbs campaign bursts (e.g., 3000 appointment batch) without manual intervention. Spot Fargate tasks usable for workers (not beat) to reduce compute cost by ~40%.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): SMS and email appointment reminders at 48h and 2h, Athena Health API integration, delivery_logs tracking, opt-out management with SELECT FOR UPDATE, idempotency key deduplication, CloudWatch observability, staging environment, 85% test coverage. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Voice call reminders via Twilio Programmable Voice, care gap outreach sequences (3-attempt protocol per condition type), no-show rate reporting dashboard, care_gaps table and CareGapJob. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Bidirectional SMS (patients reply '1' to confirm appointment), additional EHR integrations (eClinicalWorks), multi-clinic tenant configuration support.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with type annotations throughout; mypy strict mode enforced in CI. Celery tasks must be idempotent by design — any task that can be retried must tolerate re-execution. PHI never written to logs; patient_id always hashed in structured log output. All Twilio API calls wrapped in a TwilioClient abstraction for testability. pytest with 85% branch coverage gate; Celery tasks tested with celery.contrib.pytest fixtures against a test Redis instance. All Athena Health API interactions mocked in tests using responses library.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Athena Health API downtime delays nightly reminder generation. Mitigation: dead-letter Celery queue with CloudWatch alarm; manual re-trigger endpoint for ops team. Risk: Twilio rate limits (1 SMS/sec per number by default) cause delays for large appointment batches. Mitigation: Twilio messaging service with multiple sender numbers for throughput scaling. Tradeoff: Redis idempotency keys have 48h TTL — if the same appointment recurs within 48h (reschedule scenario), a new campaign_round value must be used to generate a distinct key. Risk: TCPA class-action exposure if opt-out processing fails at scale. Mitigation: SELECT FOR UPDATE pattern plus CI integration tests simulating concurrent opt-out and dispatch.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Celery beat over AWS EventBridge Scheduler for tighter integration with worker pool and simpler local development. Confirmed Twilio HIPAA-eligible Messaging Service over AWS SNS because Twilio BAA covers message content in transit. Opted for hashed patient_id in logs over full omission to enable correlation in incident investigation without exposing PHI.

metadata.notes: Round 3 closes critical TCPA opt-out race (ISS-001 via SELECT FOR UPDATE) and idempotency gap (ISS-002 via Redis keyed deduplication); automation_tool with no frontend specialist.

project.goal: Migrate Drupal 7 veteran mental health non-profit site to Hugo static site on Netlify
project.target_users: Veterans, family members, potential volunteers and donors

frontend.approach: Static site generation; content in Markdown with front matter; Netlify Forms for volunteer intake

devops.environments: preview (PR deploys), production

constraints.seo: preserve existing URL slugs and meta content

reasoner_reviews.product: Content migration from Drupal 7 is the highest-effort task; 40+ resource pages and service listings must be audited for accuracy before migration. Crisis hotline banner must be prominently placed and updated independently of full site rebuilds — a separate data file is the right pattern.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Hugo with Netlify is well-matched. ISS-001 from round 1 around missing redirect rules for Drupal URL slugs is the critical SEO risk; a comprehensive _redirects file must be validated in CI before any production deploy. Hugo aliases front matter can handle common slug changes but a Netlify _redirects file is needed for query-parameter-based Drupal URLs.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers should be configured in netlify.toml headers block to avoid relying on meta tags. Netlify Forms spam filtering should be enabled; form submissions are low-sensitivity but bot spam can clutter the ops team's inbox.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Netlify free tier is well within the $50/month budget. Branch deploy previews on PR are available on the free tier and critical for the non-technical content review workflow.

frontend.app_structure: Hugo site with layouts/partials pattern: base layout, header with crisis hotline banner, footer with donation CTA, section-specific list/single templates for services, resources, and news.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Homepage (mission + CTA), Services (peer counseling program pages), PTSD Resource Library (searchable tag-filtered list), Volunteer Intake Form (Netlify Forms), News/Updates (chronological list), About Us.
frontend.performance_notes: Hugo builds sub-2s for 200+ pages. Tailwind CSS purged to <10KB in production. Images optimized via Hugo image processing pipeline (WebP conversion, responsive srcset). Netlify CDN serves all assets from nearest PoP.

security.auth_design: No authentication. Netlify Forms honeypot field enabled on volunteer intake to filter bot submissions.
security.abuse_prevention: Netlify Forms spam filtering active; reCAPTCHA v3 optional for volunteer form if spam volume warrants it.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: No server-side logging; GA4 event tracking for page views, resource downloads, and form submissions.

data.entities: No database entities; content modeled as Hugo content types: service_listings (Markdown + front matter), resource_pages (tags, link, pdf_url), news_articles (date, author, body), volunteer_applications (Netlify Forms — name, email, availability, skills).
data.retention_and_deletion: Netlify Forms submissions retained 90 days; GA4 data retention set to 14 months.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub repo -> GitHub Actions build (hugo build, Tailwind purge, Lighthouse CI) -> Netlify atomic deploy. PR deploys generate branch preview URL for content review.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: hugo build validation, Lighthouse CI performance check (LCP <2.5s gate), link checker (htmlproofer) for broken internal links, redirect validation against _redirects file.
devops.rollback_strategy: Netlify one-click rollback to any previous atomic deploy via Netlify dashboard. No database to migrate on rollback.

ISS-001.title: Drupal 7 URL slug redirects missing from _redirects file; SEO rankings at risk
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described redirects as needed but did not specify the _redirects file format, coverage methodology, or CI validation step. Drupal uses /node/123 and ?q=path query-param URLs that require explicit redirect rules. Without these, crawlers will index 404s and organic search traffic to PTSD resource pages will be lost.

history.note: Redirect requirement mentioned but not specified or validated

ISS-002.title: Crisis hotline banner update requires full site rebuild in round 1 plan
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan embedded crisis hotline numbers directly in the header partial. If a hotline number changes, a developer must edit source and redeploy. A separate Hugo data file (data/crisis_hotlines.yaml) would let non-technical staff update numbers via NetlifyCMS without touching template code.

history.note: Hotline numbers hardcoded in header partial

focus_issues.title: Drupal 7 URL slug redirects missing from _redirects file; SEO rankings at risk
focus_issues.detail: _redirects file not specified; CI redirect validation step absent; Drupal node/ and ?q= URLs unaddressed.

focus_issues.title: Crisis hotline banner update requires full site rebuild
focus_issues.detail: Hotline numbers hardcoded in header partial instead of a data file editable via NetlifyCMS.

latest_recommendations: Define complete Netlify _redirects file covering all Drupal /node/{id} and ?q=path patterns; validate in CI with a redirect-check script comparing old Drupal sitemap URLs against _redirects entries
Move crisis hotline phone numbers and org names to data/crisis_hotlines.yaml; render in header partial via Hugo range; expose field in NetlifyCMS config for non-technical editorial updates

Move crisis_hotlines to Hugo data file; configure in NetlifyCMS for staff self-service updates
