previous_plan.title: Veteran Mental Health Non-Profit Hugo Site v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Hugo static site on Netlify replacing Drupal 7 for veteran mental health non-profit, with Tailwind CSS theme, NetlifyCMS for content editing, and Netlify Forms for volunteer intake.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: GitHub Actions triggers Hugo build on merge to main; Netlify deploys atomic static bundle to CDN. No server-side runtime.
previous_plan.data_model: Content modeled as Hugo Markdown files: service_listings, resource_pages, news_articles. Netlify Forms for volunteer intake.
previous_plan.risks_and_tradeoffs: Drupal URL migration not yet fully addressed — ISS-001 open. Crisis hotline numbers hardcoded — ISS-002 open.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 resolves both open issues: ISS-001 addressed by specifying a Netlify _redirects file built from the Drupal sitemap export covering /node/{id} and ?q= query-param patterns, with a CI redirect-check script validating 100% coverage before any production deploy. ISS-002 resolved by moving crisis hotline data to data/crisis_hotlines.yaml rendered in the header partial and exposed as a NetlifyCMS collection for non-technical editorial updates. Both fixes are low-effort and directly address the SEO and operational risks.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified complete Netlify _redirects file generation workflow: crawl Drupal XML sitemap export, generate redirect rules for /node/{id} -> /services/{slug} and ?q=node/{id} -> /resources/{slug} patterns, commit _redirects to repo root. Added CI step using a custom Python redirect-checker that validates every URL from the Drupal sitemap has a matching _redirects entry before the Netlify deploy proceeds.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero 404s for legacy Drupal URLs post-launch; Google Search Console shows no index coverage drop for veteran mental health and PTSD resource pages

fix_report.action_taken: Crisis hotline phone numbers and organization names moved to data/crisis_hotlines.yaml with fields (name, phone, hours, description). Hugo header partial renders hotlines via range .Site.Data.crisis_hotlines. NetlifyCMS config.yml exposes crisis_hotlines as a file collection with string fields for name, phone, and hours — non-technical staff can update via NetlifyCMS UI without any code changes or redeploy (NetlifyCMS triggers a git commit which triggers an automatic rebuild).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Non-technical staff can update crisis hotline numbers within 5 minutes via NetlifyCMS without developer involvement; rebuild and CDN propagation complete within 3 minutes of commit

target_output.title: Veteran Mental Health Non-Profit Hugo Site
target_output.executive_summary: A fast, low-cost Hugo static site replacing a Drupal 7 installation for a veteran mental health non-profit, hosted on Netlify CDN within a $50/month budget. The site delivers a peer counseling services directory, PTSD resource library, news updates, and a volunteer intake form powered by Netlify Forms. Crisis hotline contact data is managed via a Hugo data file editable by non-technical staff through NetlifyCMS. A comprehensive Netlify _redirects file with CI validation preserves Google Search rankings for all migrated PTSD and veteran wellness resource pages.
target_output.architecture_overview: Hugo generates static HTML from Markdown content files and Go templates; Tailwind CSS is purged at build time to sub-10KB. GitHub Actions runs on every push: hugo build, Tailwind purge, Lighthouse CI performance check, htmlproofer link validation, and a Python redirect-checker script that verifies _redirects file coverage against the Drupal sitemap URL list. On merge to main, Netlify atomic deploy promotes the build to its CDN. PR branches receive Netlify preview deploy URLs for non-technical content review before merge. No server-side runtime exists; volunteer form submissions route to Netlify Forms for storage and email notification.
target_output.technology_stack: SSG: Hugo 0.125.x. CSS: Tailwind CSS 3.x with PostCSS purge. CMS: NetlifyCMS (Decap CMS) for non-technical staff content editing via Git. Hosting: Netlify CDN (free tier). Forms: Netlify Forms with spam filtering and honeypot. CI/CD: GitHub Actions (hugo build, Lighthouse CI, htmlproofer, redirect-checker). Analytics: Google Analytics 4 with GA4 Measurement Protocol for form submission events.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Homepage: mission statement, featured peer counseling programs, crisis hotline banner (data-driven from crisis_hotlines.yaml), donation CTA. Services Directory: individual pages per peer counseling program with eligibility, contact, and location. PTSD Resource Library: tag-filtered list of self-help resources, articles, and PDF worksheets with download tracking via GA4 events. Volunteer Intake Form: Netlify Forms submission with name, email, availability, and skills fields; spam filtered via honeypot. News/Updates: chronological list with staff-authored Markdown posts via NetlifyCMS. About Us: mission, team profiles, and program history.
target_output.system_components: Hugo build engine: processes Markdown content and Go templates into static HTML. data/crisis_hotlines.yaml: data source for crisis hotline banner rendered in header partial. Netlify _redirects file: 40+ redirect rules mapping legacy Drupal /node/{id} and ?q= URLs to new Hugo slugs. Netlify CDN: global edge delivery with HTTPS enforcement. Netlify Forms: volunteer intake submission storage and email notification (90-day retention). NetlifyCMS (Decap CMS): Git-backed CMS UI for non-technical content authors editing Markdown and data files. GitHub Actions CI: build, lint, Lighthouse, htmlproofer, redirect-checker. Google Analytics 4: page views, resource downloads, form conversion tracking.
target_output.workflows: Content Update Workflow: non-technical staff logs into NetlifyCMS, edits a service listing or resource page (or updates crisis_hotlines.yaml), NetlifyCMS commits to a draft branch, staff submits PR via NetlifyCMS, developer reviews and merges, GitHub Actions builds and validates, Netlify atomic deploy publishes within 3 minutes. Volunteer Form Submission: visitor fills Netlify Form (honeypot field present), Netlify validates and stores submission, ops team receives email notification with form data, submission available in Netlify Forms dashboard for 90 days. Redirect Validation in CI: GitHub Actions redirect-checker.py reads Drupal-sitemap-export.txt (committed to repo), parses all legacy URLs, verifies each has a matching rule in _redirects, fails build with list of uncovered URLs if any are missing — prevents production deploy with broken redirects.
target_output.data_model: No database. Content types modeled as Hugo Markdown with front matter: service_listings (title, program_type, eligibility, contact_phone, location, body), resource_pages (title, tags[], resource_type ENUM article/pdf/hotline, url, pdf_url), news_articles (title, date, author, summary, body), volunteer_applications (Netlify Forms: name, email, phone, availability, skills_text — stored by Netlify, not in repo). Data files: data/crisis_hotlines.yaml (name, phone, hours, description per hotline org). Redirects: static/netlify _redirects (from /node/{id} and ?q=node/{id} to /services/{slug} and /resources/{slug}).
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. External integrations: Netlify Forms POST /api/submit (browser-native, no custom JS required). Google Analytics 4 Measurement Protocol for server-side event validation not needed at this scale — gtag.js client-side tracking only. NetlifyCMS reads/writes via GitHub REST API using a scoped OAuth token. No custom endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Netlify CDN for all traffic; HSTS header configured in netlify.toml. Content Security Policy header in netlify.toml [[headers]] block: default-src 'self', script-src 'self' 'nonce-{csp-nonce}' https://www.googletagmanager.com, img-src 'self' data:. Netlify Forms honeypot field (hidden input with common bot-targeted name) filters automated submissions. No user accounts, no session cookies beyond GA4 anonymized analytics. GA4 configured with IP anonymization enabled and no advertising personalization signals. Volunteer form data retained 90 days in Netlify Forms then deleted per privacy policy.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI workflow on every push: (1) hugo build --minify, (2) Tailwind CSS purge validation (bundle size check), (3) Lighthouse CI against preview URL — fails if LCP >2.5s or CLS >0.1, (4) htmlproofer checks all internal links, (5) redirect-checker.py validates _redirects coverage against drupal-sitemap-export.txt. On merge to main: Netlify atomic deploy to production CDN. Branch preview deploys on PRs for content review by non-technical stakeholders. Rollback: Netlify one-click rollback to any prior atomic deploy in the Netlify dashboard — no database migration needed. Estimated build time: <90 seconds for 200+ pages. Monthly cost: Netlify free tier $0, GitHub Actions free tier $0, GA4 free — well within $50/month budget.
target_output.observability: Google Analytics 4: page views by section, resource PDF download events (ga4 event file_download), volunteer form submission conversion rate, crisis hotline banner click events. Netlify deploy notifications via email to engineering lead on deploy success/failure. GitHub Actions Lighthouse CI: LCP, FID, CLS, performance score published as PR comment. No custom metrics infrastructure; operational visibility is adequate for a non-profit static site at this scale.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Total infrastructure cost: $0/month on Netlify free tier and GitHub Actions free tier — $50/month budget fully preserved. Hugo SSG scales to thousands of pages without compute cost increase. Netlify CDN absorbs traffic spikes (campaign events, press coverage of veteran mental health programs) without scaling configuration. Tailwind CSS purge keeps CSS bundle minimal; no JS framework payload.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Drupal sitemap export and _redirects file generation, Hugo theme development with Tailwind, content migration of 40+ pages to Markdown, NetlifyCMS setup for non-technical editors, crisis_hotlines.yaml data file, Netlify Forms volunteer intake, CI pipeline with redirect-checker and Lighthouse CI, production launch. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Spanish language content variants via Hugo i18n, online donation integration via Stripe Payment Links (no custom checkout backend needed), event calendar for peer support group meetings using iCal feed. Phase 3: News RSS feed, email newsletter opt-in via Mailchimp embedded form.
target_output.development_guidelines: Hugo template code in layouts/; content in content/; data files in data/; static assets (images) in static/ with WebP optimization via Hugo image processing. Tailwind class purging validated in CI to prevent stylesheet bloat. All images must have descriptive alt attributes for accessibility and SEO. Redirect-checker.py must be updated whenever new Drupal source URLs are discovered during content audit. NetlifyCMS config.yml is the source of truth for editorial fields — any new content type field must be added there before authors use it.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Drupal sitemap export may be incomplete, leaving some legacy URLs without redirect rules. Mitigation: supplement with Google Search Console crawl export and manual spot-check of high-traffic pages. Risk: NetlifyCMS (Decap CMS) is a community-maintained project; vendor support is limited. Mitigation: Markdown-in-Git content model means switching CMS is a config change only. Tradeoff: Netlify Forms 100 submission/month free tier limit — if volunteer applications exceed this, upgrade to Netlify Pro at $19/month. Risk: GA4 deprecation of Universal Analytics data means no historical pre-migration baseline — document last-30-days UA metrics before cutover.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Netlify Forms over a custom form backend to stay within zero-infrastructure budget. Selected NetlifyCMS (Decap CMS) over Forestry/Tina because it is open-source and Git-backed with no vendor dependency. Chose Hugo over Eleventy due to team familiarity and faster build times for 200+ pages.

metadata.notes: Round 2 closes SEO redirect gap (ISS-001 via _redirects + CI validation) and crisis hotline hardcoding (ISS-002 via Hugo data file + NetlifyCMS); landing_page with no backend or database specialists.

profile.domain: State Public Health Population Mental Health Analytics

project_goal.value: Population mental health analytics platform for state public health agencies using machine learning to analyze aggregated de-identified claims and social determinants data, predicting regional mental health service demand and identifying underserved geographic areas
project_goal.rationale: State health agencies lack data-driven tools to forecast service needs and allocate mental health resources; this platform enables proactive budgeting and policy decisions

target_users.value: State public health analysts, epidemiologists, and program managers who generate policy reports and budget submissions
target_users.rationale: Primary users are internal government analysts; platform is not consumer-facing

project_class.rationale: ML demand forecasting and LLM-assisted report generation are core differentiators; qualifies as AI system

capabilities.value: backend, data, ai_llm, analytics, batch_jobs, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: BigQuery analytics, Vertex AI ML pipelines, LLM-assisted policy narrative generation, batch data ingestion, admin configuration panel

complexity_level.rationale: Statewide claims data volumes (tens of millions of records), multiple ML models, Vertex AI pipeline orchestration, and multi-agency data governance

risk_level.rationale: Data is aggregated and de-identified; no individual-level PHI in the platform; state data use agreements govern access

data_sensitivity.rationale: Aggregated de-identified Medicaid claims data; individual identifiers removed per HIPAA Safe Harbor; output is population-level statistics

external_exposure.rationale: Platform accessible only to state agency staff via Google Workspace IAM; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Google Workspace IAM with role-based access: analyst (read + export), admin (configure data sources + model parameters), service accounts for pipeline execution
access_model.rationale: Google Workspace SSO aligns with state agency Google Cloud contracts; RBAC enforces data access scoping

feature_scope.value: Monthly batch ingestion from Medicaid claims and census/SDOH data sources, demand forecasting ML models by county and service type, geographic coverage gap maps, analyst admin panel for cohort exploration, LLM-assisted policy narrative generation, Looker dashboard publishing, model performance monitoring
feature_scope.rationale: Full analytics lifecycle from data ingestion to policy report generation

mvp_scope.value: Monthly batch ingestion pipeline, county-level demand forecasting model, geographic gap map dashboard in Looker, analyst self-service query panel
mvp_scope.rationale: Core forecasting and visualization capability needed for next budget cycle

security_baseline.value: Google Workspace IAM; BigQuery column-level access controls; VPC Service Controls perimeter; audit logging via Google Cloud Audit Logs; no individual-level data in platform
security_baseline.rationale: GCP-native controls appropriate for state government environment; VPC Service Controls prevent data exfiltration

frontend_stack.value: Streamlit (admin panel) + Looker (dashboards)
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom UI frontend; Looker for governed dashboards, Streamlit for analyst ad-hoc exploration and pipeline configuration

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI and Vertex AI Pipelines (Kubeflow SDK)
backend_stack.rationale: Python data science ecosystem; FastAPI for admin panel backend; Vertex AI Pipelines for ML orchestration

data_platform.value: BigQuery (analytics warehouse) + Google Cloud Storage (raw data staging)
data_platform.rationale: BigQuery scales to Medicaid claims volumes without infrastructure management; GCS for raw file ingestion staging

hosting_target.value: GCP: Cloud Run (admin API + Streamlit), Vertex AI Pipelines (ML), BigQuery (analytics)
hosting_target.rationale: Fully managed GCP-native services minimize ops overhead for state agency IT

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw claims files deleted from GCS within 30 days of processing; BigQuery analytics tables retained 5 years per state records policy; model artifacts retained 3 years in Vertex AI Model Registry
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State data use agreements mandate defined retention windows; de-identified aggregate data has longer retention

future_scope.value: Real-time streaming ingestion for crisis line call volume data, predictive model API for county budget planning tools, natural language query interface for non-technical policymakers

constraints.value: Must operate within GCP us-central1 region; state data use agreements restrict data to GCP project boundary; budget capped at $8,000/month
constraints.rationale: State data residency requirements and existing GCP contract

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring for pipeline run success/failure; Looker alerts for stale dashboard data; model performance metrics tracked in Vertex AI Model Monitoring
observability_baseline.rationale: GCP-native monitoring sufficient for internal analytics platform; no external APM needed

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with monthly data ingestion pipeline first, then ML model training, then Looker dashboards
execution_preference.rationale: Data pipeline correctness must be validated before model training begins

compliance_context.value: State Medicaid data use agreements (DUAs) restrict data to specific GCP project; NIST 800-53 controls for state government; HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification standards for all ingested data; CMS reporting requirements for managed care analytics
compliance_context.rationale: State government data governance framework and federal Medicaid program requirements

llm_integration.value: Vertex AI Gemini Pro via Vertex AI API for LLM-assisted policy narrative generation; prompts include county-level demand forecast summaries; no individual-level data in prompts; outputs are draft policy narrative paragraphs reviewed by analysts before publication
llm_integration.rationale: LLM reduces analyst time for translating statistical findings into policy language; Vertex AI keeps data within GCP project boundary per DUA requirements

project.goal: Population mental health service demand forecasting and geographic gap analysis for state agencies
project.target_users: State public health analysts, epidemiologists, program managers
project.timeline: Phase 1 MVP in 16 weeks for budget cycle

backend.framework: FastAPI (admin API), Vertex AI Pipelines (Kubeflow SDK)
backend.api: Internal admin REST API; Vertex AI pipeline trigger API

security.auth: Google Workspace IAM with service accounts
security.compliance: State DUAs, NIST 800-53, HIPAA Safe Harbor

devops.environments: dev, staging, production

constraints.data_boundary: GCP project VPC Service Controls perimeter

reasoner_reviews.product: Demand forecasting at county level aligned with budget cycle timeline is the right MVP scope. LLM narrative generation is a compelling differentiator for non-technical policymakers but should be clearly labeled as AI-generated draft content requiring analyst review to avoid governance concerns.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: BigQuery + Vertex AI is the right architecture for this GCP-native state agency environment. The pipeline must handle schema changes in incoming Medicaid claims extracts gracefully — a schema validation step before any BigQuery load is critical. Vertex AI Feature Store for reusable SDOH features across model versions is worth specifying for future-proofing.
reasoner_reviews.security: VPC Service Controls perimeter is mandatory per state DUA; BigQuery column-level access controls should scope analyst roles to aggregated tables only, not raw claims staging tables. Audit logging for all BigQuery queries must be enabled and exported to a separate log project to prevent tampering.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: $8,000/month is achievable with BigQuery on-demand pricing for the analytics query patterns described. Vertex AI Pipeline GPU hours for monthly retraining are the largest variable cost; reservations for predictable retraining schedules can cut this by 30%.
reasoner_reviews.critic: LLM prompt design must explicitly exclude any county identifiers that could be combined with public data to re-identify individuals per DUA restrictions. This is not a blocker for round 1 but must be specified before LLM feature ships.

backend.service_design: Cloud Run service (admin-api) handles analyst requests: pipeline triggers, model version queries, export generation. Vertex AI Pipelines orchestrates monthly data ingestion DAG (GCS extract -> BigQuery raw load -> dbt transformation -> model training -> evaluation gate -> model registry push).
backend.api_patterns: FastAPI REST API for admin panel: POST /pipelines/trigger (manual run), GET /models (list registered model versions), GET /forecasts/county/{fips} (query latest predictions), POST /reports/narrative (trigger LLM narrative generation for selected forecast). All routes require Google Workspace IAM token.
backend.business_modules: DataIngestionModule: validates schema of incoming Medicaid extract against expected column set, loads to BigQuery raw dataset. TransformationModule: dbt models compute county-level utilization rates, SDOH index scores, and service gap indicators. ForecastingModule: Vertex AI custom training job runs XGBoost demand forecasting model; outputs predictions to BigQuery forecast_results table. NarrativeModule: constructs prompt from county forecast summary (no individual identifiers), calls Vertex AI Gemini Pro, returns draft narrative string.
backend.background_jobs: MonthlyPipelineJob: triggered by Cloud Scheduler on 1st of each month; runs full ingestion-transform-train-evaluate sequence via Vertex AI Pipelines. ModelEvaluationGate: checks MAE and RMSE against thresholds before promoting new model version in Model Registry; sends Slack notification to data team on promotion or rejection.

security.auth_design: Google Workspace OAuth 2.0 for all human users. Service accounts for Vertex AI Pipelines, Cloud Run, and BigQuery jobs with least-privilege IAM bindings. No end-user credentials in pipeline code.
security.authorization_model: Analyst role: BigQuery data viewer on aggregated dataset + Cloud Run invoker. Admin role: adds BigQuery data editor and Vertex AI pipeline runner. Raw claims staging dataset restricted to pipeline service accounts only.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Google Cloud Audit Logs: DATA_READ and DATA_WRITE audit logs for BigQuery enabled and exported to a separate audit-log GCP project (analysts cannot modify). Cloud Run request logs with user identity for all API calls. Vertex AI Pipeline run logs with model version and training data lineage.
security.privacy_controls: BigQuery column-level security: individual_id and zip_code_5digit columns in raw staging tables restricted to pipeline service accounts. Aggregated output tables expose only county_fips, service_type, and predicted_demand. LLM prompts constructed programmatically to exclude any geographic identifiers below county level.

data.entities: raw_claims_staging (county_fips, service_type, claim_count, claim_amount, service_date, sdoh_index — no individual identifiers), sdoh_indicators (county_fips, year, poverty_rate, unemployment_rate, housing_insecurity_index, social_isolation_score, broadband_access_pct), forecast_results (county_fips, service_type, forecast_month, predicted_demand, confidence_interval_lower, confidence_interval_upper, model_version_id), coverage_gaps (county_fips, service_type, current_capacity, predicted_demand, gap_score, updated_at), ml_model_versions (id, model_name, training_data_hash, mae, rmse, promoted_at, status ENUM candidate/production/retired), pipeline_runs (id, pipeline_name, triggered_by, status, started_at, completed_at, records_processed).
data.storage_design: BigQuery datasets: raw_staging (restricted access), transformed_analytics (analyst read), forecast_output (analyst read + Looker), audit_log (audit project only). GCS bucket: raw claims file landing zone with 30-day object lifecycle policy for automatic deletion. Vertex AI Model Registry: model artifacts with lineage tracking.
data.analytics_events: Pipeline run completion (records_processed, duration_seconds, model_mae), Looker dashboard view counts by county, analyst API query patterns for feature prioritization, narrative generation requests per week.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run (admin-api): 2 instances min, auto-scaling to 10, us-central1. Vertex AI Pipelines: serverless pipeline execution on managed GCP infrastructure. BigQuery: serverless; no infrastructure to manage. Looker: Google-managed SaaS within GCP project.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: Python lint (ruff), mypy type check, pytest (unit tests for dbt models via dbt-unit-testing, FastAPI routes). Docker build and push to Artifact Registry. Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy on merge to main. dbt model deployment via dbt Cloud job. Vertex AI Pipeline definition versioned in Git; new pipeline version deployed on tag push.
devops.observability_stack: Google Cloud Monitoring: pipeline run success rate, forecast freshness (alert if forecast_results not updated within 35 days), Cloud Run latency and error rate. Vertex AI Model Monitoring: prediction drift alerts if county-level demand predictions deviate >15% from prior month baseline. Looker: dashboard embedded freshness indicator.
devops.cost_controls: BigQuery slot reservations for dbt transformation jobs (predictable monthly cost vs on-demand). Vertex AI training job preemptible VMs for model retraining (30-40% cost saving). GCS lifecycle policy auto-deletes raw claims files after 30 days. Estimated $6,800/month at production scale (BigQuery $3,200, Vertex AI $2,100, Cloud Run $400, Looker $1,100).

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a GCP-native population analytics platform for state health agencies; key decisions are BigQuery + Vertex AI as the analytics and ML backbone given GCP data residency requirements from state DUAs, LLM narrative generation scoped to county-level aggregates only to stay within DUA re-identification risk constraints, and a schema validation step on every claims file before BigQuery ingestion to handle the schema variability common in Medicaid extract formats.
target_output.title: State Population Mental Health Analytics Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A GCP-native AI analytics platform for state public health agencies that ingests monthly de-identified Medicaid claims and social determinants data into BigQuery, runs XGBoost demand forecasting models via Vertex AI Pipelines, and surfaces county-level service gap predictions through Looker dashboards and a Streamlit analyst panel. LLM-assisted policy narrative generation via Vertex AI Gemini Pro accelerates report authoring for non-technical program managers. All data remains within a VPC Service Controls perimeter compliant with state data use agreements and NIST 800-53 controls.
target_output.architecture_overview: Monthly batch ingestion pipeline runs on Cloud Scheduler trigger: a Vertex AI Pipeline job validates incoming Medicaid claims CSV extracts against an expected schema (pandera), loads validated records to a BigQuery raw_staging dataset (service account access only), executes dbt transformation models that compute county-level service utilization rates and SDOH composite scores, then trains an XGBoost demand forecasting model via a Vertex AI Custom Training Job. An evaluation gate checks MAE against a threshold before promoting the new model version to the Vertex AI Model Registry and publishing updated predictions to the forecast_output BigQuery dataset. Analysts interact with Looker dashboards for county-level gap maps and a Streamlit admin panel (Cloud Run) for ad-hoc cohort queries and LLM narrative generation requests. Google Workspace IAM governs all human access; VPC Service Controls perimeter prevents data exfiltration from the GCP project.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Vertex AI Pipelines (Kubeflow Pipelines SDK), XGBoost, dbt Core 1.8, pandera for schema validation. LLM: Vertex AI Gemini Pro API (in-project, within VPC Service Controls). Analytics warehouse: BigQuery with column-level security. Dashboard: Looker (Google Cloud-managed). Admin UI: Streamlit hosted on Cloud Run. Storage: Google Cloud Storage (raw file staging, 30-day lifecycle). CI/CD: GitLab CI. Monitoring: Google Cloud Monitoring + Vertex AI Model Monitoring.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Monthly Data Ingestion: automated schema-validated ingestion of Medicaid claims extracts and census SDOH files into BigQuery raw_staging. Demand Forecasting: county-level, service-type-level XGBoost predictions with confidence intervals for 6-month forecast horizon. Geographic Gap Analysis: coverage_gaps table and Looker map visualization showing counties where predicted demand exceeds current licensed provider capacity. Analyst Admin Panel: Streamlit UI for pipeline trigger, model version inspection, county cohort filtering, and narrative generation request. LLM Policy Narrative: Gemini Pro generates draft policy narrative from selected county forecast data; analysts review and edit before publication. Model Performance Tracking: MAE/RMSE history per model version in Vertex AI Model Registry with promotion audit trail.
target_output.system_components: Vertex AI Pipelines: monthly orchestration of ingestion -> transformation -> training -> evaluation -> model registration. BigQuery raw_staging dataset (pipeline service account only): raw Medicaid and SDOH data with 30-day retention. BigQuery transformed_analytics dataset (analyst read): dbt-computed utilization rates and SDOH scores. BigQuery forecast_output dataset (analyst read + Looker): county-level demand forecasts and gap scores. Vertex AI Custom Training Jobs: XGBoost model training on Preemptible VMs. Vertex AI Model Registry: model artifact versioning with lineage and MAE/RMSE metadata. Cloud Run (admin-api): FastAPI service for pipeline triggers, model queries, and narrative generation. Looker: governed dashboard layer on BigQuery forecast_output. GCS raw-claims bucket: 30-day lifecycle auto-delete. Google Cloud Audit Logs (exported to audit-log project): immutable BigQuery query and API access logs. VPC Service Controls perimeter: network boundary enforcing DUA data residency.
target_output.workflows: Monthly Forecast Refresh: Cloud Scheduler triggers Vertex AI Pipeline on 1st of each month. Pipeline step 1 (DataValidation): reads new Medicaid extract from GCS, runs pandera schema validation (expected columns, value range checks for claim amounts, valid county FIPS codes); on validation failure, sends alert to data-governance Slack channel and aborts. Step 2 (RawLoad): BigQuery load job to raw_staging.claims_monthly partition. Step 3 (dbt Transform): dbt run executes utilization_rates, sdoh_composite, and service_gap_indicators models. Step 4 (ModelTraining): Vertex AI Custom Training Job trains XGBoost on last 36 months of county-level features; logs MAE and RMSE to Vertex AI Experiments. Step 5 (EvaluationGate): if MAE > threshold (configurable, default 12%), rejects model promotion and notifies data team; otherwise registers as production model. Step 6 (ForecastPublish): writes 6-month forecast predictions to BigQuery forecast_output; Looker data freshness indicator updates. Analyst Narrative Generation: analyst selects 1-5 counties in Streamlit admin panel, clicks 'Generate Policy Narrative'; admin-api constructs prompt containing county names, predicted demand figures, gap scores, and YoY trend; calls Vertex AI Gemini Pro API; returns draft narrative labeled 'AI-generated draft — analyst review required'; analyst edits and copies to policy document.
target_output.data_model: raw_claims_staging (county_fips CHAR(5), service_type VARCHAR, claim_count INT, claim_amount_agg NUMERIC, service_month DATE, ingestion_run_id UUID — no individual identifiers). sdoh_indicators (county_fips CHAR(5), data_year INT, poverty_rate NUMERIC, unemployment_rate NUMERIC, housing_insecurity_index NUMERIC, social_isolation_score NUMERIC, broadband_access_pct NUMERIC). forecast_results (county_fips CHAR(5), service_type VARCHAR, forecast_month DATE, predicted_demand INT, confidence_lower INT, confidence_upper INT, model_version_id UUID, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). coverage_gaps (county_fips CHAR(5), service_type VARCHAR, current_licensed_capacity INT, predicted_demand INT, gap_score NUMERIC, gap_tier ENUM critical/moderate/adequate, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ml_model_versions (id UUID, model_name VARCHAR, training_data_hash VARCHAR, mae NUMERIC, rmse NUMERIC, training_months_covered INT, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM candidate/production/retired). regional_cohorts (id UUID, name VARCHAR, county_fips_list JSONB, created_by VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). pipeline_runs (id UUID, pipeline_name VARCHAR, triggered_by VARCHAR, status ENUM running/success/failed, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, records_ingested INT, model_promoted BOOLEAN).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI REST API on Cloud Run, all routes require Google Workspace IAM Bearer token. POST /pipelines/trigger — admin role; triggers on-demand Vertex AI Pipeline run; returns run_id. GET /pipelines/{run_id}/status — returns pipeline step statuses and record counts. GET /models — list Vertex AI Model Registry entries with MAE/RMSE metadata. GET /forecasts?county_fips={fips}&months=6 — returns forecast_results for specified county. GET /gaps?tier=critical — returns coverage_gaps filtered by gap_tier. POST /reports/narrative — body: {county_fips_list: [], forecast_month: date}; calls Gemini Pro; returns {narrative: string, model_version: string, generated_at: timestamp}. GET /health — returns service status and last successful pipeline run timestamp.
target_output.security_and_compliance: VPC Service Controls perimeter encompasses GCP project; no data egress to external endpoints except Vertex AI Gemini Pro (in-project endpoint, not external). BigQuery column-level security: analyst IAM role bindings scoped to transformed_analytics and forecast_output datasets only; raw_staging restricted to pipeline service accounts. Google Cloud Audit Logs: DATA_READ and DATA_WRITE enabled for BigQuery, Cloud Run, and Vertex AI; logs exported to a separate audit-log GCP project with no write access for platform team — immutable audit trail. LLM prompts: Gemini Pro requests constructed programmatically to include only county-level aggregates (no zip codes, no individual proxies); prompt template reviewed by DUA compliance officer before LLM feature activation. State DUA compliance: data never leaves us-central1 region; raw extract files deleted from GCS within 30 days per DUA schedule; all BigQuery table access logged. NIST 800-53 relevant controls: AC-2 (IAM RBAC), AC-17 (no remote access beyond Workspace SSO), AU-2 (audit log export), SI-3 (Cloud Armor on Cloud Run).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest (unit tests for dbt models, FastAPI routes, pandera schema validator), Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy (staging then production). Vertex AI Pipeline definition versioned in Git; new pipeline version deployed on semver tag push via gcloud ai custom-jobs create. dbt models deployed via dbt Cloud job triggered by GitLab CI on merge to main. Environments: dev (shared BigQuery project, no real claims data — synthetic fixtures), staging (separate GCP project with 3-month historical sample), production (full state dataset). Cloud Scheduler: monthly pipeline trigger on 1st of month at 03:00 UTC. Rollback: Vertex AI Model Registry supports rollback to prior production model version in <5 minutes; BigQuery table snapshots retained 7 days for transformation layer rollback.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring custom dashboards: pipeline run success rate (target 100%), forecast data freshness (alert if forecast_results.generated_at > 35 days old), Cloud Run admin-api p99 latency and error rate. Vertex AI Model Monitoring: county-level prediction drift alert if rolling 30-day MAE degrades >15% from baseline. Looker: embedded 'data last refreshed' timestamp on all dashboard tiles; Looker alert if dashboard data stale >36 hours. GitLab CI pipeline duration tracking for build time regression detection. Google Cloud Audit Logs dashboard: weekly report of BigQuery query counts by user role for DUA compliance review.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $6,800/month at production scale: BigQuery on-demand queries $3,200 (Looker dashboard loads dominate; consider slot reservations for dbt jobs at $1,800/month to flatten cost), Vertex AI training preemptible VMs $2,100 (XGBoost monthly retraining ~12h GPU), Cloud Run admin-api $400 (low request volume), Looker GCP-managed $1,100. GCS storage for raw files negligible with 30-day lifecycle. Under $8,000/month budget with 15% headroom. Slot reservations for dbt transformation jobs (~$200/month) provide predictable cost for consistent monthly pipeline runs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (16 weeks): BigQuery schema design, GCS ingestion bucket and lifecycle policy, pandera schema validator, dbt transformation models (utilization_rates, sdoh_composite, coverage_gaps), XGBoost training pipeline on Vertex AI, evaluation gate, Looker county-level gap map dashboard, Streamlit admin panel with pipeline trigger and model version display, GitLab CI/CD, VPC Service Controls perimeter. Phase 2 (10 weeks): LLM narrative generation (Gemini Pro integration, prompt design DUA review), forecast API for external county budget tools, Vertex AI Feature Store for reusable SDOH features, Vertex AI Model Monitoring for prediction drift. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Real-time streaming ingestion of crisis line call volume data via Pub/Sub, natural language query interface for non-technical policymakers using Gemini Pro and BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with type annotations; mypy strict mode in CI. dbt models must include schema.yml tests (not null, accepted values, unique) for all key columns. pandera schema validator must be updated when Medicaid extract format changes — treat schema definitions as code. Vertex AI Pipeline components must be idempotent — rerunning any step with the same inputs must produce the same outputs. LLM prompt templates versioned in Git with DUA compliance sign-off required before merge. No county identifiers below county_fips (no ZIP codes, census tracts) permitted in Gemini Pro prompts.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Medicaid claims extract schema changes from state data providers without advance notice, breaking the pandera validator. Mitigation: schema validator in lenient mode logs warnings for unexpected columns rather than hard-failing; strict mode gate validates required columns only. Risk: Vertex AI Gemini Pro API latency (3-8s per narrative) may frustrate analysts if batch narrative generation requested. Mitigation: async narrative generation with polling endpoint; UI shows progress indicator. Tradeoff: BigQuery on-demand vs slot reservations — on-demand flexible for variable load but slot reservations provide cost predictability for monthly dbt runs. Risk: XGBoost demand forecasting accuracy degrades if SDOH data (poverty, unemployment) lags by >12 months. Mitigation: feature freshness check in pipeline; alert if any SDOH indicator not updated within 13 months.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Vertex AI Gemini Pro over OpenAI API because Vertex AI operates within the GCP project VPC Service Controls perimeter, satisfying DUA data residency requirements. Chose BigQuery on-demand for MVP with slot reservation evaluation at 60-day usage review. Confirmed Looker over Data Studio for governed dashboard access controls required by DUA. Selected XGBoost over deep learning models for interpretability required in policy contexts.

metadata.notes: Population health analytics ai_system with BigQuery/Vertex AI on GCP; state DUA compliance context; LLM narrative generation scoped to county-level aggregates; no FrontendAgent (ai_system with admin_panel served via Streamlit on Cloud Run).

profile.domain: Mental Health Digital Therapeutics Engagement Event Pipeline

project_goal.value: Scalable event pipeline processing millions of daily in-app engagement events (module completions, mood logs, exercise interactions) to feed personalization models and product analytics dashboards for a mental health digital therapeutics platform
project_goal.rationale: Current batch-based analytics cannot support near-real-time personalization; Kafka + Spark Structured Streaming enables sub-minute feature computation

target_users.value: Data engineers maintaining the pipeline, data scientists consuming feature vectors, and product managers viewing analytics dashboards
target_users.rationale: Internal consumers only; no end-user-facing pipeline components

project_class.rationale: Pure data processing architecture with no user-facing frontend; Kafka ingestion, Spark processing, ClickHouse and PostgreSQL storage

capabilities.rationale: Kafka consumer, Spark Structured Streaming, ClickHouse analytics queries, daily batch feature computation, PostgreSQL operational writes

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of daily events across active user base; Kafka partition scaling, Spark EMR Serverless, and ClickHouse query performance at scale

risk_level.rationale: Personal engagement data (mood logs, exercise completion) but not medical records; GDPR and CCPA compliance required for deletion workflows

data_sensitivity.rationale: Mood log events and therapeutic module interactions qualify as personal data under GDPR; CCPA applies for California users

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline is internal infrastructure; no external API surface; app clients write to Kafka via internal ALB

access_model.value: AWS IAM for all pipeline service components; data scientist access to ClickHouse analytics dataset via IAM-scoped credentials; no end-user access
access_model.rationale: Internal data infrastructure with role-based IAM access for data team

feature_scope.value: Kafka topic ingestion from app clients, Spark Structured Streaming near-real-time aggregation to ClickHouse, daily batch feature vector computation to PostgreSQL, GDPR right-to-deletion workflow, CCPA opt-out event suppression, data quality monitoring
feature_scope.rationale: Full event lifecycle from app client to analytics and personalization consumers

mvp_scope.value: Kafka ingestion, Spark streaming aggregation to ClickHouse, daily batch feature job to PostgreSQL, basic data quality alerts
mvp_scope.rationale: Near-real-time analytics and personalization features are the primary business value; deletion workflows in phase 2

security_baseline.value: Kafka MSK with TLS in transit and IAM authentication; ClickHouse and PostgreSQL encrypted at rest (EBS KMS); data access audit logging via CloudTrail; PII (user_id) hashed in analytics tables
security_baseline.rationale: Personal data requires encryption and access controls; audit trail for GDPR compliance demonstration

frontend_stack.value: none
frontend_stack.rationale: Pure data pipeline; no user-facing frontend; analytics dashboards consumed via ClickHouse SQL client and Grafana

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Kafka (MSK) and Spark Structured Streaming (EMR Serverless)
backend_stack.rationale: Python PySpark for Spark jobs; MSK for managed Kafka; EMR Serverless for cost-efficient Spark execution

data_platform.value: ClickHouse (analytics) + PostgreSQL 16 (operational feature store)
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage optimized for aggregation queries on engagement event tables; PostgreSQL for recommendation service feature reads

hosting_target.value: AWS: MSK (Kafka), EMR Serverless (Spark), ClickHouse on EC2, PostgreSQL 16 on RDS
hosting_target.rationale: AWS-native managed services reduce ops overhead; ClickHouse self-managed for cost efficiency at query volume

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw Kafka event retention 7 days; ClickHouse aggregated tables retained 2 years; PostgreSQL feature vectors retained 90 days rolling; GDPR deletion requests processed within 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR and CCPA require deletion capability; limited raw data retention reduces deletion scope

future_scope.value: Real-time personalization feature serving from ClickHouse, A/B experiment event tracking, cross-platform event schema registry

constraints.value: Must process 50M+ daily events with sub-5-minute ClickHouse aggregation lag; monthly compute budget capped at $12,000; events schema versioning required for backward compatibility
constraints.rationale: Personalization model SLA requires fresh features; cost ceiling set by data team budget

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for Spark job metrics and Kafka consumer lag; Datadog for ClickHouse query performance and PostgreSQL replication lag
observability_baseline.rationale: Multi-tool observability covering streaming, batch, and OLAP query tiers

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code via Terraform; Spark jobs deployed as versioned Docker images
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducible infrastructure and immutable Spark job versioning

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 17 right-to-erasure requiring deletion of user_id-linked records across ClickHouse and PostgreSQL within 30 days of request; CCPA right-to-opt-out requiring event suppression for California users; data minimization — mood log event content not retained beyond aggregation window
compliance_context.rationale: EU and California user base requires GDPR and CCPA compliance workflows built into pipeline design

project.goal: Scalable near-real-time engagement event pipeline for mental health digital therapeutics personalization and analytics
project.target_users: Data engineers, data scientists, product managers

backend.framework: Spark Structured Streaming on EMR Serverless

security.transport: TLS on MSK; KMS encryption at rest

devops.hosting: AWS EMR Serverless + MSK + EC2 + RDS
devops.environments: staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: Near-real-time aggregation lag under 5 minutes is achievable with Spark micro-batch intervals of 30 seconds. The GDPR deletion workflow is the highest-risk operational requirement — deletion across both ClickHouse and PostgreSQL must be atomic or at minimum idempotent with a retry mechanism.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ISS-001 from round 1 is critical: ClickHouse lacks native row-level deletion; the deletion approach must use a ReplacingMergeTree or mutation-based strategy, not the naive DELETE SQL that round 1 specified. ISS-002 around Kafka schema evolution is also unresolved; an Avro schema registry is needed before production to prevent Spark job failures on schema changes.
reasoner_reviews.security: GDPR deletion audit trail is required — each deletion request must be logged with request_id, user_id_hash, tables_affected, completed_at, and status. This log must be immutable (S3 write-once). CCPA opt-out event suppression must be applied in the Spark job before any aggregation writes, not post-hoc.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: At 50M daily events, Kafka topic needs 16+ partitions to saturate EMR Serverless capacity. ClickHouse on EC2 should use c6a.4xlarge with local NVMe for hot data performance; EBS for MergeTree cold parts.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan was thin on both the GDPR deletion mechanism and schema evolution strategy. Both are table-stakes for a personal-data pipeline at this scale and must be fully specified in round 2.

backend.service_design: Three Spark Structured Streaming jobs: (1) EngagementAggregator: consumes engagement-events Kafka topic, computes 5-min tumbling window aggregates per user per module, writes to ClickHouse engagement_aggregates table. (2) MoodLogProcessor: consumes mood-log-events topic, validates schema, enriches with user_timezone, writes raw events to ClickHouse mood_log_facts (aggregation only — no raw content retained beyond 7-day Kafka retention). (3) FeatureVectorJob: daily batch job computes per-user engagement feature vectors (module completion rate, streak length, mood_trend_7d) and upserts to PostgreSQL user_features.
backend.background_jobs: DeletionWorker: polls deletion_requests table hourly; for each request issues ClickHouse ALTER TABLE...DELETE mutation for user_id_hash, DELETEs from PostgreSQL user_features, marks request completed, writes completion record to S3 deletion audit log. CCPASuppressionJob: reads suppressed_users list from PostgreSQL, adds to Spark broadcast variable checked in EngagementAggregator before any writes.
backend.failure_handling: Spark checkpoint directory in S3 for exactly-once semantics on Kafka offset commits. Dead-letter topic (engagement-events-dlq) for records failing schema validation. EMR Serverless auto-retry on job failure (max 2 retries). ClickHouse async insert buffer with durability=async_insert_wait for high-throughput writes without synchronous ACK overhead.

security.secrets_management: MSK credentials, ClickHouse password, RDS credentials in AWS Secrets Manager; rotated 90-day cycle; injected as environment variables into EMR Serverless job runs via IAM task role.
security.privacy_controls: user_id hashed with SHA256 + per-deployment HMAC key before writing to ClickHouse and PostgreSQL; raw user_id never persisted downstream of Kafka. CCPA suppression broadcast variable in Spark filters events before any aggregation. GDPR deletion uses user_id_hash as deletion key; original user_id not retained in analytics stores.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: S3 deletion audit log (write-once via S3 Object Lock) records deletion_request_id, user_id_hash, tables_affected, records_deleted_count, completed_at. CloudTrail for all S3 and RDS API access. MSK broker logs to CloudWatch.

data.entities: engagement_events Kafka topic (user_id, event_type ENUM module_completed/exercise_started/mood_logged/session_ended, module_id, session_id, client_timestamp, server_timestamp, app_version, platform). engagement_aggregates ClickHouse (user_id_hash, module_id, event_date, event_type, count, last_event_at — ReplacingMergeTree, ORDER BY (user_id_hash, module_id, event_date)). mood_log_facts ClickHouse (user_id_hash, logged_at, mood_score INT8, energy_level INT8, anxiety_level INT8 — MergeTree, TTL 2 years). user_features PostgreSQL (user_id_hash PK, module_completion_rate NUMERIC, current_streak_days INT, mood_trend_7d NUMERIC, last_computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, next_module_recommendation VARCHAR). deletion_requests PostgreSQL (id UUID, user_id_hash VARCHAR, requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE, status ENUM pending/processing/completed/failed, tables_affected JSONB). suppressed_users PostgreSQL (user_id_hash VARCHAR PK, opt_out_type ENUM gdpr/ccpa, opted_out_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.consistency_notes: Spark checkpoint in S3 provides Kafka offset idempotency. ClickHouse ReplacingMergeTree deduplicates re-delivered events on merge. PostgreSQL user_features uses INSERT...ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE for idempotent daily upserts. Deletion operations are idempotent: DeletionWorker checks status before reissuing mutation.
data.migration_notes: Existing batch analytics tables in PostgreSQL migrated to ClickHouse via one-time Spark batch job; old tables deprecated after 30-day validation window.

devops.deployment_topology: MSK 3-broker cluster in us-east-1 with 16 partitions on engagement-events topic. EMR Serverless application for Spark jobs (streaming and batch). ClickHouse: 2-node cluster (primary + replica) on c6a.4xlarge EC2 with local NVMe. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ for deletion_requests and user_features. S3: Spark checkpoint bucket, DLQ archive bucket, deletion audit log bucket (Object Lock enabled).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy, pytest (PySpark unit tests with pyspark.tests local mode), Docker build of Spark job image, push to ECR, EMR Serverless job definition update on merge to main. Staging EMR application consumes from a separate staging MSK cluster with synthetic event data.
devops.rollback_strategy: EMR Serverless: revert to prior job image tag in job definition. Spark checkpoint ensures streaming jobs resume from last committed offset without reprocessing. ClickHouse schema migrations use non-destructive ADD COLUMN operations; no DROP COLUMN in production without 30-day deprecation period.

ISS-001.title: GDPR deletion uses naive SQL DELETE incompatible with ClickHouse MergeTree engine
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified DELETE FROM engagement_aggregates WHERE user_id_hash = ? which is not supported on ClickHouse MergeTree tables. ClickHouse requires ALTER TABLE...DELETE mutations which are asynchronous; the plan did not account for mutation completion tracking or the engagement_aggregates ReplacingMergeTree strategy.

history.note: SQL DELETE specified; incompatible with ClickHouse MergeTree; no mutation tracking

ISS-002.title: No schema registry for Kafka topics; Spark jobs will fail on schema evolution
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan consumed Kafka JSON events without schema validation or registry. When app teams add or rename event fields, Spark jobs will throw deserialization exceptions. A Glue Schema Registry with Avro schemas is needed for backward-compatible evolution.

history.note: No schema registry specified; JSON deserialization without schema enforcement

ISS-003.title: CCPA opt-out suppression applied post-aggregation rather than at ingestion
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 plan filtered suppressed users after ClickHouse writes in a cleanup job. CCPA requires that opted-out user data not be processed for analytics purposes; suppression must be applied in the Spark job before any aggregation writes to ClickHouse.

history.note: Post-hoc suppression cleanup job specified; CCPA requires pre-aggregation suppression

focus_issues.title: GDPR deletion uses naive SQL DELETE incompatible with ClickHouse MergeTree
focus_issues.detail: ALTER TABLE...DELETE mutation required; mutation completion tracking and idempotency not addressed.

focus_issues.title: No schema registry; Spark jobs fail on schema evolution
focus_issues.detail: Glue Schema Registry with Avro schemas needed for backward-compatible Kafka topic evolution.

latest_recommendations: Replace SQL DELETE with ALTER TABLE engagement_aggregates DELETE WHERE user_id_hash = ? mutation; add mutation completion polling in DeletionWorker with exponential backoff; write completion record to S3 audit log only after OPTIMIZE TABLE FINAL confirms mutation applied
Add AWS Glue Schema Registry with Avro schemas for engagement-events and mood-log-events topics; Spark jobs use GlueSchemaRegistryDeserializer; schema evolution rules set to BACKWARD_COMPATIBLE
Move CCPA suppression check to Spark broadcast join before foreachBatch ClickHouse write; suppressed_users table refreshed every 60 seconds in streaming job
